## Thread: stacked IF terminology? — 35 this month / 43 total

_Thread started [2002-10](2002-10.md#t-f7c4db87b1)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: stacked IF terminology?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T00:48:08
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3dc1ccdb_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.B9E5A46E006639C616D5FAF0@news-east.usenetserver.com> <appeds$hu9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3dc04f92_3@Usenet.com> <01HW.B9E62471001A347516E86E00@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
Comments interspersed below, Edward...

Pete.

Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:01HW.B9E62471001A347516E86E00@news-east.usenetserver.com...
>
<snip>
> > An INCORRECTLY (or "ugly" if you like <G>) Nested IF would use both the
true
> > and false branches and would probably have statements after the IF
like...
>
> So you're saying *never* nest the ELSE part?

No, I'm saying it is ugly to do so, and I'm saying I wouldn't do it from
preference.(Actually, I feel strongly enough about it that I would never do
it, but I have no problem with what other people do, and it would be against
my own principle of encouraging freedom of choice for me to say that no-one
else should do it...<G>)

> I very much see the point
> about the additional statements. I often preach about flattening, about
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
While the above is OK, it suffers from testing the same thing twice, and if
you need to add more conditions it becomes messy...

Given that EVALUATE is not an option, why not:

move zero to truth-value
if CALIFORNIAN
   add 1 to truth-value
end-if
if VEGETARIAN
    add 2 to truth-value
end-if
*> if you don't mind using GO TO, you could now add 1 to truth-value and
*> code a GO TO...DEPENDING ON truth-value here...
*> (Each branch could be to a PERFORM statement if you need to maintain
control flow.)
*> if you prefer not to use GO TO, then the following will do the job...
if NOT truth-value = zero
   if NOT truth-value = 1
      if NOT truth-value = 2
         if NOT truth-value = 3
            perform internal-logic-error
         else
             *> CALIFORNIAN AND VEGETARIAN
             perform serve-raddichio
         end-if
      else
          *>  NOT CALIFORNIAN AND VEGETARIAN
          peform serve-iceberg-lettuce
      end-if
   else
       *> CALIFORNIAN AND NOT VEGETARIAN
       perform serve-salmon
   end-if
else
    *>  NOT (CALIFORNIAN OR VEGETARIAN)
    perform serve-meat-and-potatoes
end-if

This is a typical case of converting a redundant Boolean expression into a
truth value and using the value to decide what is happening. It is a simple
matter to extend the truth table by adding another condition which would
cause 4 to be added to the truth value, another condition again would add 8
to the truth value, and so on.... in this way every possible combination of
the  (in this example, 4...) conditions is covered, and yet each condition
is tested only once.

Storing compound conditions as a truth value is useful because the truth
value can be checked at various places in the program even if the actual
conditions are no longer obtainable ("remembered" conditions). However, it
is important to document them as shown above...

Obviously, EVALUATE is more elegant.

> Now it's true that in this case I would prefer to use an EVALUTE with
> two subjects, or to use both conditions in each IF as a sequential
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is fine for two conditions. I agree that this is technically a nested
IF but, for myself I don't consider it as such. I prefer your label of
"stacked IF".

I still don't like the redundant test on CALIFORNIA and would be unlikely to
code it myself, for this reason.

> etc. If you're saying always avoid nesting in the ELSE, how does it
> work in general? Of course it's convenient that you already said the
> book is out of print, so naturally I have to ask you instead of looking
> it up.
>
On the contrary, it is extremely inconvenient that the book is out of print
<G> I would LOVE to get my hands on a copy of it...

To answer your question, it works that you only nest IFs in the true branch
so that a nested IF is only required to implement a decision tree. I showed
it in the last post. (I learned the technique from the good Doctor McCracken
and it has served me long and well. I have passed it on to at least 50 other
COBOL programmers and God knows how many people reading this will decide it
is not a bad way to deal with nested IFs...but I really don't mind whether
they adopt it or not.)

Please be aware Edward that I am NOT saying "always avoid nesting in the
ELSE". What I am saying is that I personally always avoid nesting in the
ELSE. I really don't mind what other people do. I have maintained COBOL code
all my life; some of it was easy, some of it was difficult, some of it was
what I considered good style and some of it wasn't, but, in the final
analysis, if you call yourself a programmer, you should be able to program
in the language, and have enough understanding of it to deal with how others
use it.

There is no universal COBOL programming standard. There cannot be and
shouldn't be. Implementing local standards for a given shop is one thing,
doing it for the World is quite another...

In forums like this we can discuss alternative styles but it amuses me to
see how evangelical these discussions quickly become. (Like it really
MATTERS, or the fate of the World depends on it...said it before; I say it
again: "It is only computer programming, not life and death...")



> On Wed, 30 Oct 2002 15:07:02 -0500, William M. Klein wrote
> > It might help me to understand your question better if you showed us
what
> > you think is a "nested IF statement" without being a "stacked IF"
statement.
>
> Yes, I should do that. The full example above is nested. There is
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I am afraid that (IMHO) this is a "nested IF" statement - no matter what
you
> > think it might be.
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> > It seems to me that ANY time you have an IF statement "embedded" in
either
> > the IF branch or the ELSE branch, you have a "nested IF" and there is
really
> > no way to distinguish between them.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That is a very worthwhile objective and I endorse it. The danger is that
style can stifle as well as stimulate...

Pete.
```

```
> isn't likely to live long.

That's why we only have it the dragstrip.

But that does remind me of when I moved back to Wisconsin from Illinois a
few years ago.  The behavior of drivers when the light is yellow is subtly
different on the two sides of the Cheddar Curtain... in Illinois, drivers
don't "jackrabbit" off the line because people seem to interpret a yellow
light as a "step on it" instruction... in Wisconsin, drivers *do*
"jackrabbit" because the yellow is more often interpreted as "stop now."

Took a little getting used to...

MCM
```

```
I vaguely remember that a fairly extensive British survey concluded
that the majority of Britons, or perhaps just a very large segment of the populace,
could not recite the correct display sequence from memory.

IIRC, Red - Amber - Green - Amber - Red was the most popular choice.

I wouldn't be surprised if that conclusion turned out to be still true today.
```

```
>From: dashwood@enternet.co.nz  (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
>Date: 11/1/02 11:41 AM Eastern Standard Time

>Hahaha!
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pete

Doesn't cover the blinking red before green we have in some areas either :)

Not to be confused with the blinking red of a traffic light either 'off' for
the evening or at an intersection where there is no full traffic light.  These
are found usually on left turn lights meaning - ok to turn if you have
clearence before you get the full right of way of a green light.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: stacked IF terminology?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-03T12:17:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211031217.616bcd93@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.B9E5A46E006639C616D5FAF0@news-east.usenetserver.com> <appeds$hu9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3dc04f92_3@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0210311212.3dd7a44e@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211010841.4abbe41d@posting.google.com>`

```
dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood) wrote

> Thanks Richard. I had a #5 bet with a colleague (I am currently in
> the U.K.) that SOMEONE would respond to my post with the fact that
> it didn't cover British traffic light sequences...<G>

I had hoped that you would respond with an explanation about how
your mechanism would have usefully dealt with the situations not
covered.

> > If cond-1 and cond-2 and cond-3 are ALL true  do action 1
> > If cond-1 and cond-2 are true, but cond-3 is NOT true, do action 2
> > If cond-1 is true and cond-2 is NOT true, do action 3
> > If cond-1 is not true do action-4

While this is 'exhaustive' for 4 states, there are in fact 8
possible (some less probable) states (plus some flashing and
alternating).

> > For me an IF is "nested"  CORRECTLY when nesting occurs in
> > the TRUE Branch only.
> > My reasoning here is that it represents a decision tree.

Trees have branches on both sides. You have artificially cut off
one side.

The 'obvious' place to put the red+amber test would be after
the ELSE before Prepare-to-stop.

> > ELSE
        IF amber light
            PERFORM Prepare-to-go
        ELSE
> >         Perform Prepare-to-stop
        END-IF
> > END-IF

This, however, would be contrary to your 'nesting only in the
true branch'.

Alternately a test for red+amber could be prepended to the code
but then the whole of your current code would be in the false
branch of that.

It seems that you would do something like:

       IF NOT red + amber + green light
           IF NOT red + amber light
               IF NOT amber + green light
> >               IF NOT red light
> >                  IF NOT amber light
> >                     IF NOT green light

Which does fullfil your 'nesting in true', but to me has several
'uglinesses':

First is the highly contrived negative testing required to make
it all in the true branch.  Many programmers make mistakes with
negative tests, especially in conjunction with ANDs and ORs,
where they would write perfectly correct positive tests.

In fact there were changes to Cobol made specifically because of
how the use of NOT was confusing to many.  I would expect that
many programmers would be unsure of what all these meant:

      IF NOT Red OR Amber
      IF NOT ( Red OR Amber )
      IF NOT Red AND Amber
      IF NOT Red AND NOT Amber

as a test put these into groups that are equivalent without
looking the rules up the in manual.

Second that it ensures that the actions are as far removed from
the test as it is possible to make them.  Making it difficult to
directly relate condition and action makes for poor understanding
and thus makes changes prone to errors.

In a 'case' statement there is direct relationship:

        condition
             action
        condition
             action
        ...

with little chance of mistaking which action goes with which
condition.

In your code there is

       condition
           condition
               condition
                   condition
                       condition
                           condition
                               condition
                                   action
                               action
                           action
                        action
                    action
                action
            action
            action

Which requires:
      Relying on indentation being perfectly done.
      Picking through each ELSE IF and back.
      Comments, which may or may not be true.

none of which are reliable until _proven_ to be so at great
expense of time.  Of course if you had written this yourself
then you _know_ you can rely on all those without having to
check them, but the 'next programmer' cannot.  He is looking
at the code, perhaps, because it doesn't quite work.

It seems that McCameron invented the anti-EVALUATE.

> > with pages of a single IF statement.

If wouldn't necessarily be the pages per se. that made it a
nightmare, it was probably the large separation between the
condition and the resulting action.

I have worked with a 1500 line EVALUATE statement that was
perfectly clear _beacuse_ the condition-actions were tightly
linked.  This was a Windows program so each WHEN could be
treated as if it were a paragraph with the event as its
name.

> in 1969, as described in his book: "COBOL Logic and Programming"

In 1969 there were no Cobol constructs that later came in with
ANS85.  It may have been quite reasonable when using Cobol'61 or
68 to constrain the way IF ... ELSEs were used in order to avoid
ugly 'ELSE NEXT SENTENCE' or, worse, forgetting the ELSE and
winding up with incorrect code. But move on, we no longer need
these obsolete rules now that we have real solutions to the
problems of the 60s.

> Now, sadly, long out of print.

I suspect that there is a good reason why it is out of print, such
as: it is archaic and obsolete.

> An INCORRECTLY (or "ugly" if you like <G>) Nested IF would use
> both the true and false branches and would probably have
> statements after the IF  like..

What is 'incorrect' or 'ugly' is what you are not used to and
don't immediately recognise.

You see your code as: "simple and elegant", I see it as contrived
and difficult when compared to, say, an EVALUATE, or its
equivalent in IF .. ELSEs.


BTW. In NZ there is also another mode: flashing alternate
red/green which means 'out of service - proceed with caution as
if an uncontrolled intersection'.
```

```
you are responding to...

Comments below...


riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0211031217.616bcd93@posting.google.com>...
> dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood) wrote
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I did. Previously, with the use of truth values. If you REALLY want to
look at every possible combination (apart from flashing) then I would
simply assign a truth value to each colour and use EVALUATE...

However, that would NOT demonstrate the use of the decision tree noted
below, or the use of a Nested IF to implement it.

> > > If cond-1 and cond-2 and cond-3 are ALL true  do action 1
> > > If cond-1 and cond-2 are true, but cond-3 is NOT true, do action 2
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The object if the exercise was simply to demonstrate the use of a
Nested IF; not a treatise on the possible sequences of traffic lights
around the World
(which, by the way, I am perfectly familiar with...apart from Eileen's
flashing red. Right turns on a red light are quite legitimate in
California but the light doesn't flash.) I therefore opted for
something with 4 states. It was an EXAMPLE, for Chrissake!


> > > For me an IF is "nested"  CORRECTLY when nesting occurs in
> > > the TRUE Branch only.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No I haven't. Both the true and false branches are documented, but the
IF is ONLY nested in the TRUE side.


========================  complete irrelevance 
=======================
> The 'obvious' place to put the red+amber test would be after
> the ELSE before Prepare-to-stop.
>
If I was going to do a RED+AMBER test I would have used a truth value
as discussed in earlier post to this thread. If there were 8 values I
CERTAINLY would not use a Nested IF, especially when EVALUATE is now
available. In the olden days I would have broken the tests down.

Anything on combinations of lights OTHER than the ones I chose to
demonstrate the nested IF is therefore irrelevant...

> > > ELSE
>         IF amber light
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> It seems that you would do something like:

Assumption on your part, and incorrect assumption at that...

> 
>        IF NOT red + amber + green light
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I agree. I wouldn't do it. If you had read what I wrote you wouldn't
have believed that I would do it.

> First is the highly contrived negative testing required to make
> it all in the true branch.  Many programmers make mistakes with
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> looking the rules up the in manual.

No problem at all... I'm perfectly familar with both of De Morgan's
Laws and have run seminars on Boolean Algebra and Propositional
Calculus... (However, I would use parentheses to remove any ambiguity
for the sake of people less logically endowed <G>)


> 
> Second that it ensures that the actions are as far removed from
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I can't believe you are arguing this! Of course, an EVALUATE is to be
preferred. I said as much in my post. The discussion was predicated on
the fact that EVALUATE was NOT available!!!




> with little chance of mistaking which action goes with which
> condition.
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> It seems that McCameron invented the anti-EVALUATE.

There WAS no EVALUATE when Fritz wrote his book! Cheesh!


> 
> > > with pages of a single IF statement.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Yes, it is certainly archaic. However, it has a style of its own which
makes it a classic. Many COBOL programmers in the Sixties (self
included) struggled with nested IFs; McCameron's book cleared the
topic nicely.

And if you REALLY believe I still code the way I did then and HAVEN'T
moved on then you really haven't got much idea of how I write COBOL.
(But, then I rarely post public samples of code because people seem to
misinterpret why they are posted, and go off on to their own private
tangent...<G>)


> > An INCORRECTLY (or "ugly" if you like <G>) Nested IF would use
> > both the true and false branches and would probably have
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is no COBOL that I don't recognise. I'm a COBOL programmer. What
I consider ugly is purely a subjective judgement that has more to do
with aesthetics than failure to understand.

 
> You see your code as: "simple and elegant", I see it as contrived
> and difficult when compared to, say, an EVALUATE, or its
> equivalent in IF .. ELSEs.
> 

Again, you are ascribing a position to me which I never adopted. The
code is predicated on NOT using IF...ELSE...END-IF  (for efficiency)
and not using
EVALUATE (as being not available). If you want to nest an IF, then I
suggest it is simpler and more elegant to nest only in the TRUE
branch. But I really don't care how you do it. If required to, I'll
debug your code no matter how you code it.


> 
> BTW. In NZ there is also another mode: flashing alternate
> red/green which means 'out of service - proceed with caution as
> if an uncontrolled intersection'.

Ah, yes! I remember that now....of course. Wonder why I didn't think
it was relevant to a discussion of nested IFs in COBOL...? It must be,
or you wouldn't have raised it would you <G>?
==========================  end of complete irrelevance
====================

Pete.
```

```
dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood) wrote

> Amazed at your response, Richard. It is not like you to NOT read what
> you are responding to...

OTOH, it is not like you to think that I am responding to something
completely different.

> I agree. I wouldn't do it. If you had read what I wrote you wouldn't
> have believed that I would do it.

If I had read what you wrote _elsewhere_, then maybe ..

> I can't believe you are arguing this! Of course, an EVALUATE is to be
> preferred. I said as much in my post. 

No. You didn't.  Not in the post that I responded to.  You may have
done so in a completely different, later, post.

> The discussion was predicated on
> the fact that EVALUATE was NOT available!!!

Not in the actual post that I responded to, and _read_.  Perhaps you
were reading something else entirely.

> There WAS no EVALUATE when Fritz wrote his book! Cheesh!

There were, however, equivalent structures in other languages at the
time.

Regardless of whether there was an EVALUATE or not, the 'only true'
nesting is the antithesis of how a case statement makes a clear
relationship between the conditions and the actions.  The 'else only'
nesting (or stacked IF) does emulate an EVALUATE nicely.

The issue was that you posted that the nesting only in the true branch
was the only 'correct' or 'desirable' or 'proper' way, you taught it
and recommended it, and that other structures, such as balanced
IF..ELSE and a nested (or stacked) IF that emulated an EVALUATE was
'ugly', 'incorrect', a 'nightmare' and you wouldn't use it.

I considered that the opinions you expressed in that post that I
responded to was wrong.  While it may have appeared useful 30 years
ago, if you hadn't seen a better way, I think that your continuing
enthusiasm for it was misplaced.

> And if you REALLY believe I still code the way I did then and HAVEN'T
> moved on then you really haven't got much idea of how I write COBOL.

No, I haven't much idea about how you code, I can only base this on
what you call 'proper', 'correct' and 'desirable'.

> Again, you are ascribing a position to me which I never adopted. The
> code is predicated on NOT using IF...ELSE...END-IF  (for efficiency)
> and not using
> EVALUATE (as being not available). 

No it wasn't, but then I only read the post that I was _responding_ to
and not some other collection of posts that you had in mind.

> If you want to nest an IF, then I
> suggest it is simpler and more elegant to nest only in the TRUE
> branch. 

And I suggested that was a very poor way of doing it (for reasons
stated), emulating an EVALUATE is simpler and more elegant.

In fact I often prefer stacked IFs (nested enirely in each ELSE) over
an EVALUATE where there is a variety of conditions necessitating a
rather contrived 'EVALUATE TRUE'.

> > BTW. In NZ ..

> Ah, yes! I remember that now....of course. Wonder why I didn't think
> it was relevant to a discussion of nested IFs in COBOL...? It must be,
> or you wouldn't have raised it would you <G>?

You do understand what 'BTW' means don't you ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: stacked IF terminology?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.SPAMNOT.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-09T08:25:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DCCC63A.6050907@optonline.SPAMNOT.net>`
- **References:** `<01HW.B9E5A46E006639C616D5FAF0@news-east.usenetserver.com> <appeds$hu9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3dc04f92_3@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0210311212.3dd7a44e@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211010841.4abbe41d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211031217.616bcd93@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211041113.73591314@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042118.4d4f038@posting.google.com> <3DC8ABAC.3080702@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <aqbs5e$2ca$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  5-Nov-2002, Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> obvious to a non-programmer what it means?

That's where we disagree, "Evaluate True" is required to handle the 
COBOL 88-levels. I went nuts the first time I tried to code COBOL II and 
wanted to set up a case structure, e.g.:

Evaluate	variable
When		88-level ...

End-Evaluate

That's invalid, the "True" form makes it work, as in:

Evaluate	True
When		88-level ...

End-Evaluate

Every IF statement evaluates (small "e") as either True or False. I just 
don't see the issue with:

Evaluate True.

As always, YMMV

:-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: stacked IF terminology?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-10T07:45:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DCE0E9A.27E805D7@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<01HW.B9E5A46E006639C616D5FAF0@news-east.usenetserver.com> <appeds$hu9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3dc04f92_3@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0210311212.3dd7a44e@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211010841.4abbe41d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211031217.616bcd93@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211041113.73591314@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042118.4d4f038@posting.google.com> <3DC8ABAC.3080702@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <aqbs5e$2ca$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DCCC63A.6050907@optonline.SPAMNOT.net>`

```
Liam Devlin wrote:
> 
> That's where we disagree, "Evaluate True" is required to handle the
> COBOL 88-levels.

"EVALUATE TRUE" is not _required_.  It is perfectly correct to handle
88-levels with IF.
 
> Evaluate        variable
> When            88-level ...

Or, putting it another way: using EVALUATE means that you never need
88-levels.

   EVALUATE variable
   WHEN value-1
   WHEN value-2 THRU value-3
         action-1
   WHEN value-4
         action-2

> As always, YMMV

Yes, entirely a matter of prefernece.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ EVALUATE TRUE (was: stacked IF terminology?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-09T13:58:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aqjpe9$8v2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<01HW.B9E5A46E006639C616D5FAF0@news-east.usenetserver.com> <appeds$hu9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3dc04f92_3@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0210311212.3dd7a44e@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211010841.4abbe41d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211031217.616bcd93@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211041113.73591314@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042118.4d4f038@posting.google.com> <3DC8ABAC.3080702@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <aqbs5e$2ca$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DCCC63A.6050907@optonline.SPAMNOT.net> <3DCE0E9A.27E805D7@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
The disadadvantage of the technique

  Evaluate Data-Name
   When Value-1 ...
    When Value-2 thru Value-3

is the same as all uses of "explicit" value checks versus 88-levels, i.e.
that you must change EVERY occurrence of the test in the procedure division
when do this - instead of a single place when you use 88-levels.  Certainly
it can be done either way, but IMHO, the 88-level method is always BETTER
for a "condition" that is used multiple times in the Procedure Division.
(When it is used a single place in the Procedure Division, I see little
advantage one way or another).  Even for compilers that support "true
constants" (extension in '85 Standard, required in the 2002 Standard), there
is still an advantage in using 88-levels as these allow for ADDING
additional values (not expanding a range) with a single place to do this.

    ****

And back to the original issue of when/if to use "EVALUATE TRUE" - I like to
use it for multi-condition case structures.  Although these MAY be difficult
to "maintain" or understand, the often better (IMHO) reflect some business
cases, e.g.

Evaluate TRUE also TRUE also FALSE    *> and some people may NOT like mixing
trues and falses

  When A > B Also C = D  also A = C
      Perform Case-1
  When A < B Also C > D  Also A > C
   When A < B Also C = D Also A > C
      Perform Case-2
   When ...

In the "abstract" this doesn't look great, but it certainly is typical of
SOME business logic.  Changing this to nested IF's certainly CAN be done,
but does tend to look a little "odd" (using one form of optional punctuation
and indenting)

If (A > B)
  and (C = D)
  and (A not = C)
       Perform Case-1
Else If
 ( (A < B)
  and (C > D)
  and (A not > C))
 or
  (A < B)
  and (C = D)
  and (A not > C))
    Perform Case-2
 Else IF
 ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE TRUE (was: stacked IF terminology?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-09T20:28:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211092028.3e6299e6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.B9E5A46E006639C616D5FAF0@news-east.usenetserver.com> <b3638c46.0211010841.4abbe41d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211031217.616bcd93@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211041113.73591314@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042118.4d4f038@posting.google.com> <3DC8ABAC.3080702@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <aqbs5e$2ca$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DCCC63A.6050907@optonline.SPAMNOT.net> <3DCE0E9A.27E805D7@Azonic.co.nz> <aqjpe9$8v2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote

> The disadadvantage of the technique
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> when do this - instead of a single place when you use 88-levels.  

OTOH using 88 levels means that text searches or greps for use of the
'data-name' are defeated. Of course one could have all your 88 levels
prepended by the data-name but then it looks quite contrived IMHO.

In general if a value changes or is added then it is not just a matter
of fiddling the 88 levels, all the uses of the 88 levels have to be
checked because usually the new value is a _different_ option than
needs a new condition branch.

So generally I prefer to be able to text search easily for data-name
rather than to search for several 88 levels.

> but IMHO, the 88-level method is always BETTER
> for a "condition" that is used multiple times in the Procedure Division.

Dislike 88s, never use them.  I sometime, however, use symbolic
constants (or variables that emulate this) instead of literals:  Then
I get both advantages (IMHO) with the data name _and_ meaningful
condition names:

      EVALUATE Interface-Function
      WHEN Read-with-Lock
            ...

where, for example these are defined as:

      01  Interface-Function        PIC X.
      01  IF-values.
          03  Read-with-Lock        PIC X VALUE "L".
          03  Read-no-Lock          PIC X VALUE "R".
              etc

> And back to the original issue of when/if to use "EVALUATE TRUE" - I like to
> use it for multi-condition case structures.  Although these MAY be difficult
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>    When ...

'Backwards' decision tables.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: stacked IF terminology?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-07T07:37:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DCA182E.2C85F9BC@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<01HW.B9E5A46E006639C616D5FAF0@news-east.usenetserver.com> <appeds$hu9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3dc04f92_3@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0210311212.3dd7a44e@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211010841.4abbe41d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211031217.616bcd93@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211041113.73591314@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042118.4d4f038@posting.google.com> <3DC8ABAC.3080702@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
Liam Devlin wrote:
> Richard wrote:
> > In fact I often prefer stacked IFs (nested enirely in each ELSE) over
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> What the devil's wrong with "Evaluate True"?

There is nothing "wrong" with EVALUATE TRUE, it is just (as I said) a
personal preference.

'EVALUATE variable WHEN value ...' is a structure found in many
languages as a case statement.

'EVALUATE TRUE WHEN condition ..' is just 'IF condition .. ELSE IF's
with another syntax.

Thus we have available two identical structures with only minor syntax
changes.

    EVALUTE TRUE WHEN ( condition 1 )
        action 1
    WHEN ( condition 2 )
        ...

    IF ( condition 1 )
        action 1
    ELSE IF ( condition 2 )
        ...

I find it clearer to only use EVALUATE when it is specifically a case
statement.  YMMV.

(Or is it just that I am a refugee from the 70s who thinks that all
these new fangled '85 ideas are just a passing fad ?).
```

```
> being, I would NEVER write the alternative "ugly" form...<G>)
> 
> An INCORRECTLY (or "ugly" if you like <G>) Nested IF would use both the
true
> and false branches and would probably have statements after the IF 
like...
> 
> IF NOT red-light
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Although this may technically qualify as a "Nested IF", to me it is ugly
and
> I wouldn't use it.
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
> ordered one for $8.44 including shipping!  Search on the book title not on

Ah-ha. Pete is normally such a stickler that I didn't consider the 
possibility that he'd misspelled the name -- it's McCameron, and now I 
can find it with no trouble. Thanks.

There seem to be later editions into the 1980s, including a hardback in 
1986 which may or may not differ from the softbacks in the early 1980s. 
Looks like the softbacks at least were in a 4th edition.

Edward
```

```
> > > > the IF branch or the ELSE branch, you have a "nested IF" and there

> > >

it

> > > statement.

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > (This is the ONLY usage that I would refer to as a "Nested IF";
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where to Find History?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T02:14:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2814c$3277ba00$abc2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<app5i2$k09$1@panix1.panix.com> <3DC1069E.8020509@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid> <aprcq7$cao$1@panix1.panix.com> <01HW.B9E6B856003CF6C816E86E00@news-east.usenetserver.com> <aprkag$s57$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
It could be that knowing the history of virtual storage might be a little
misleading as far as todays processors are concerned.  In the past we used
to worry about "locality of reference" i.e. keep your data near the code
that processes it.  Recently on the assembler list, some who know much more
about todays processors than I do, have indicated that this may no longer
be ideal.  The latest processors have both an instruction cache and a data
cache and if your data is within 256 of the instructions you can actually
experience reduced performance due to flushing of the cache.  Here is an
excerpt cut and pasted from one of the emails:

"We have noticed that one of our programs takes significantly longer on
our z/900 then the older (and slower?) es/9000s.  The CPU time for one job
on the z/900 was 20k seconds and 14k seconds on the es/9000.  Has anyone
else encountered this problem?  For the moment we are using the es/9000s
for
the overnight run as we are tight on time.  Both machines are running
OS/390
2.10 and the code is an inhouse assembler written program.  In both
cases the CPU time was aprox 70% of run time."
 
 
" You could be running into a caching issue.  The z/Series significantly 
 changed the caching architecture, splitting into data and instruction
cache.  If you modify data <= 256 bytes away from a series of executing
instructions,  every modification causes a instruction cache buffer flush. 
One of our
 software products was a victim of this; the solution was to add 256 bytes
to  a GETMAIN'd area to separate the data and the instruction.
 

<snip>
```

```
people will steal anything not nailed down) so I replaced it with a 
booklet that had a yellow cover over white paper.  All had the same info.

As for "green card" I once took a course where the instructor frequently 
told us to check our green card.  The class was attended by many people 
from other countries, and they all automatically reached for their 
wallets.  Apparently they were more familiar with INS than IBM.
```

```
Russell Styles <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:2jqw9.19339$VJ5.1074412@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
>     I still remember one bit from the IBM 360 Principles of Operation.
> It mentions that if you happen to pause the CPU in the middle of a certain,
> lengthy, "interruptable" instruction, and leave it paused for over a year,
> that it would fail.

Somebody at IBM had w-a-a-a-y-y-y too much time on their hands
to even think of including that "precaution" in an IBM manual..
.
What company is going to spend tens or hundreds of thousands
building a climate-controlled, raised-floor, lowered-ceiling room
to house a multi-million dollar computer tasked with running
mission-critical applications designed and developed by an
extremely expensive cadre of professional analysts and programmers,
and then allow somebody to pause the CPU for a year.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where to Find History?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T09:56:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B9E800440037CBD61710ECE0@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<app5i2$k09$1@panix1.panix.com> <3DC06194.1070306@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid> <apq1jg$hge$1@panix1.panix.com> <3DC1069E.8020509@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid> <2jqw9.19339$VJ5.1074412@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Fri, 1 Nov 2002 2:46:06 -0500, Russell Styles wrote
> I still remember one bit from the IBM 360 Principles of Operation.
> It mentions that if you happen to pause the CPU in the middle of a certain,
> lengthy, "interruptable" instruction, and leave it paused for over a year,
> that it would fail.

Yeah. It also said that the IPL button was inoperative while the CPU 
was running. A local IBM tech at the UF decided to demonstrate this 
fact and pushed the IPL button on the 360/50 several times. Bells rang, 
the CPU halted. It didn't IPL ... but that was the necessary next step.

Edward
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<app5i2$k09$1@panix1.panix.com> <apq1jg$hge$1@panix1.panix.com> <7DED2F2254DD9C09.9F510E65A2C1A962.2056190F0FC29D39@lp.airnews.net> <3DC2FF96.9000703@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
In article <3DC2FF96.9000703@optonline.NOSPAM.net>,
Liam Devlin  <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:
>SkippyPB wrote:
>> On 30 Oct 2002 20:38:56 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com enlightened us:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>An applications guy, obviously:-)

Quite obvious... a systems coder would have been waxing rhapsodic about 
the Perfect System, running infinitely fast and encumbered by an 
infinitely small user-load.

The course was my first COBOL course, taught in the evening; most of the 
folks who attended had Other Jobs during the day... some were even in 
Operations, looking to, by education, improve themselves to the dizzying 
heights of... A Programmer!  One day he asked the class two Zen of 
Programming questions; the first was:

'What is the job of Operations?'

... and he got a slew of The Usual Answers, from the Ops crew 
themselves... maintain computer and job function, mount tapes, fix things 
when they Blew Up... and his response was a steady stream of 'Nope!' and 
some head-shaking.  When the class finally gave up he taught:

'All right... the job of Computer Operations is to come in at five 
o'clock, start the daily backup job running and then go out back and smoke 
hashish.  Write *all* of your batch jobstreams with this in mind and Ops 
will *love* you.

After the laughter subsided he continued with:

'What is the definition of a User?'

... and there were the usual attempts... but the class had wised up and 
they didn't go on as long.  When the silence returned he taught:

'A computer user - and remember, as programmers you will be users, too - 
is someone who, when prompted for a 'Y' or 'N' response, keys in the 
numeral '8'.  Write *all* of your online jobs with this and mind and the 
Users will love you.'

He was also the fellow who taught us about being fired for demanding that 
the manager buy a mainframe with 16K of core... and how The Consultant 
managed to earn US$5,000 for nine business days of assuming the 
Programmer's Position (feet on the desk, cup of coffee in one hand, 
cigarette in the other, mapping out memory-locations on the ceiling's 
acoustic tiles) and then one day of coding a single statement to many 
SELECTs... but those are other stories for other times.

He was a good man, knew his stuff and taught it well... but out of mud one 
cannot make diamonds, the best one can hope for is a good brick... and so 
I try to float along in my usual brick-like manner.

DD
```

```
Charles, all

I can understand your desire to see Cobol tools unbundled and offerred at
lower prices.  However, many Micro Focus large customers want just the
opposite.  They want fewer well integrated products that provide seemless
interface to a very wide range of functions including support for multiple
languages, multiple communications options, multiple platforms, and multiple
application functions.  In the recent annoucment, note that Visual Studio
.NET will be bundled into the product.

Don Higgins
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T13:55:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fJvw9.255717$Fw2.7331664@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <aptnld$prd$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
Don  - the price of the compiler would not be such an issue if there were an alternative to
the to runtime pricing. At least IMNSHO. The runtime license model makes a lot of sense
if you are in the one computer one person environment. It makes much less sense if you
are serving stuff up to the web, or on a UNIX, Mainframe, Linux, AS/400 platform.

Yes, I know the argument about companies cannot make enough money off the compiler
alone, but I again point out that products like Turbo Pascal sold for $49 and sold quite literally,
millions of copies. Microsoft sells their compilers at a very modest cost ($300 - $700 usually)
and considers them a profit center. And IBM COBOL for AIX or AS/400's sells for $899,
with no runtime licensing issues.

Yep, MicroFocus may be superior to any of those product, and even in some cases, much more
complex. But then again, in 2002, there are other successful models that could be explored too. Everything
from giving away an old version of the compiler (with no support of course) to perhaps, giving away
the runtimes so that MF Cobol applications become totally ubiquitous. (If every copy of Windows came
with a MF runtime, how long would it be before you sold 1,000,000 copies of a $299 compiler? )
Or use Linux as a test bed. There are a LOT of possibilities.

COBOL is just too darn *good* a language, and is often the best solution for many common issues,
but the steep entry price of the compiler is too much for the small shops, and the every mounting
cost of runtimes is unbelievably daunting for small to medium shops.

"Don Higgins" <don.higgins@microfocus.com> wrote in message news:aptnld$prd$1@hyperion.microfocus.com...
> Charles, all
>
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
to learn Cobol .NET. This is like eating your seed corn and not having any
to plant the next year.  The companies want people with experience but only
programmers working for a big company will have it.  This is very limiting
and I believe it is short sighted in the long run. I certainly am not going
to spend $3,700 for a product to train myself and keep up with the latest
and greatest technology in the mere hope that someday it may be useful in
getting a job.

Did I mention that JAVA is free?  Did I mention that JAVA is free? Did I
mention that JAVA is free?
While certainly not perfect is is definitely highly competive with .NET.
Did I mention that JAVA is free?


There is definitely a place in the world for makers of rocket fuel but most
cars still run on gas and that is where the real market is.  Of course that
can change but when it does the price of rocket fuel will adjust to market
conditions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-02T08:58:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211020858.5c6ffc54@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <aptnld$prd$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <01c28241$81f509c0$c6caf943@chottel>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote

> keep up with the latest and greatest technology 
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> conditions.

.NET may be the 'latest', but it is only Microsoft's name on it that
makes it claim 'greatest'.  So far it seems to be solutions looking
for problems that people actually care about (as distinct from jumping
into because MS says so).

In fact there seems to be so much confusion about what .NET is that
many seem to think that it may be the solution to everything even when
they don't know there is a problem.

It seems to me that the main ideas behind .NET were formed from
considering how to have a post-dotcom and post-PC business model that
generated sufficient revenue.  That is how to ensure that MS customers
continued to buy new MS software on a regular basis instead of
considering that their Win98/W2k/XP machines were all they ever needed
_and_ how to change MS to a services based revenue stream rather than
sales based (for example see how licence 6 has done this).

Of course since the initial talk about what .NET would be, MS have had
to rather flatten the concept by allowing that other companies may
want to be servers charging for services.

The basis of .NET seems to be that, for example, software house would
no longer sell an application or a library to do a specific task, they
would provide it as a service function and charge per use. Programmers
would write in C# and instead of buying libraries and code to build
into their programs, would simply attach to services over the
internet.  Some of these services would be provided by MS and some by
independant companies and by partners.

The 'advantage' to the .NET user is that (supposedly) the application
service is always updated and centrally managed and thus provides the
latest functionality without needing to be updated and recompiled into
the client program.

The only purpose of the .NET languages is to a) not be Java, b)
connect to .NET services, c) be C# or C# with a different syntax.

Now, in order to pay for these services, some central control over
authentication and payment is required: passport and wallet. 
Originally only MS could be trusted to do this.

As a business model for MS it seems that it could be a winner.  They
would provide the central control (because they are a bunch of control
freaks) and provide some services, such as Office chargable on a
per-document or per-page basis.  Other would provide other services
and MS would take a percentage (something they tried to do before). 
Where a significant revenue was being generated they would notice this
and then could offer their own 'better' version of it, or simply
redirect users to it (as they have been known to do before).

But this business model does rely on users being convinced they need
to use it.  So far this part has failed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gary Crook" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2002-11-02T22:10:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G3Yw9.218692$qM2.73130@sccrnsc02>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <aptnld$prd$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <01c28241$81f509c0$c6caf943@chottel>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote
> This approach means only programmers working at big companies will be able
> to learn Cobol .NET.

Micro Focus has typically offered educational products as well as commercial
ones. Example:

http://microfocus.com/shop/description.asp?productid=6

I don't expect COBOL .NET will be any different.

What it currently doesn't have is an entry-level (sub-$1000) product. We've
tried this at various points over the years, but with little success. There
simply doesn't seem to be enough sustainable demand at that level. Generally
speaking, COBOL's heritage is rooted in large systems. Micro Focus and the
other COBOL vendors have, in my view, done a fantastic job bringing COBOL
into the realm of mid-tier distributed computing. Simply, there is a value
proposition here that works well for ISV's and the vendors (i.e. a
business!). That (required)
dynamic is missing when you move further down the systems food chain.

> Did I mention that JAVA is free?  Did I mention that JAVA is free? Did I
> mention that JAVA is free?

What is it that you're suggesting?

Gary.
```

- **Date:** 2002-11-04T11:37:47
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <aptnld$prd$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <01c28241$81f509c0$c6caf943@chottel> <G3Yw9.218692$qM2.73130@sccrnsc02> <01c282e3$d7a07740$ca95f243@chottel>`

```
Charles,

Java has it's uses but enterprise scale business applicattions is not one of
them. I'm not trashing Java, I'm just saying that you should use the right
tool for the right job. This is one reason why Micro Focus is allowing COBOL
to interface to many different technologies, so in a mixed language
application, everything fits together smoothly.

Besides, the larger Java applications require an EJB Application Server to
support them and such things like BEA WebLogic and IBM WebSphere are not
free.

Paul
www.microfocus.com
The Future of COBOL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-04T13:45:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6Sux9.277747$121.7878714@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <aptnld$prd$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <01c28241$81f509c0$c6caf943@chottel> <G3Yw9.218692$qM2.73130@sccrnsc02> <01c282e3$d7a07740$ca95f243@chottel> <aq5m9i$krp$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
Ah - but Java with WebSphere and its constellation of add on's *is* very much a
"enterprise scale" business support product. Not only that, execution speeds and
response times are controllable and are very fast. Comes at a cost, yes.

But not a penny of that cost is in runtimes. :)

Like I said earlier, look at targeting a wider market; give the runtimes away and
sell the compiler for $299 on Windows and Linux. Watch thousands of new sales
spring up, and watch most of the revenue you currently realize from runtimes switch
into support contracts for the compiler and runtime.

Relia and AcuCorp should take a look at that model too.

This should especially be true on the new S/390 Linux platforms, where
competition is going to start heating up soon. Dave Bond over at Tachyon is
about to release the Tachyon O/S product for l/390, and that will, at least
in all probability, allow users to move compiled binaries from OS/390
and z/OS to Linux and run at least the batch processing. At native
speeds. Lots of interesting things are going to happen when that
happens, including some possible fireworks.

-Paul


-Paul

"Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote in message news:aq5m9i$krp$1@hyperion.microfocus.com...
> Charles,
>
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-03T14:53:32
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel>`

```
Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel...
> Does adding ".NET" after the COBOL make it worth $3,700 per seat?  Maybe
> greed is what will kill off COBOL.
>
I believe it isn't greed as such.

The reasoning goes like this...

"We need to recoup the cost of development, the R & D, and make a profit.
What is the siaze of the installed base likely to be...?   Uh, huh, that
means we need to charge a bit under $4000 per seat. Guess that prices it
beyond the small developer so we better target the Corporates with our
Marketing campaign."

What they haven't realised is that COBOL is no longer viable in a Corporate
environment, for anything other than maintaining existing COBOL.

(I would have introduced it to the site I am managing if it were. Simply
cannot justify it. It would be like going back to horse and buggy when you
have a Ferrari...Note that both are effective means of getting from A to
B...).

The New Technology has simply swept past it.

As a result, they find that the Corporates don't exactly rush to embrace NET
COBOL. That means the actual base is even smaller than they projected.

That means they must charge more for it.

That means it is even more difficult to sell...

And so on.

It isn't greed, it is necessity. I expect to see considerable reorganization
amongst COBOL vendors in the next 5 - 10 years. Some current players will
definitely leave the Market Place.

It is ironic, because the failing attraction of COBOL is not down to the
vendors. The major vendors have consistently supported the language and
implemented the facilities that it needed.

Personally, I believe there are four factors that are killing COBOL:

1. Lack of a proper adminstering Standards body, and the consequent lack of
credibility and involvement of the User Base. (This has now reached the
point where this body has zero credibility and nobody really cares what they
do. It is too late now to salvage COBOL.)

2. The refusal by most Fortress COBOL sites to pick up OO programming and
thereby ensure that COBOL could participate in the Object revolution which
is now taking place. Fortress COBOL sites represent the majority of the
potential COBOL user base. If they don't want something there is little
point in providing it. (This one really grieves me. I have been using OO
COBOL for nearly 4 years now and find the Fujitsu implementation to be
outstanding. I have COBOL Object Components that co-exist beautifully with
components written in other languages, but this is unlikely to become the
norm. I know of no Component developer who is using COBOL to produce
commercial components. (The ones I produce are for my own use; I don't
intend to sell them commercially.))

3. The historically superior attitude of COBOL programmers when dealing with
the User base. To be fair, I haven't seen much of this recently, but it was
prevalent in the 80s and into the 90s, and that is the time when today's
Managers were working in the Business...
(This has won COBOL no friends in the Business and led to Users doing things
for themselves rather than interface with the IT department. The logical
progression from spreadsheets, word-processors, and databases is user built
applications which do not require professional programmers; this is starting
to emerge in some application areas now. Wizards are provided which interact
with the User and eventually generate the required application code.)

4. The tenacious adherence by COBOL programmers to the COBOL file system.
The rest of the World went to open databases and a free exchange of data.
Many COBOL people remained convinced (some are still, to this day, and I'll
be surprised if this post brings no response in defence of the COBOL file
system) for far too long that Relational Databases are best avoided and
there is nothing like ISAM or VSAM/KSDS, conveniently ignoring that it
requires one-off programs to manipulate them...

If COBOL eventually disappears from the realms of serious professional
computer programming, it won't be through the greed of the vendors.

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-11-03T16:05:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c28352$d4043e40$0fccf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Why don't C++ vendors have this same problem?  I don't believe it costs
more to develop and integrate a Cobol compiler than it does to do the same
with C++.  Their standard is so complex that several years after its debut
there is still no 100% conforming compiler.

I pretty much agree with your first 3 points, although I don't think Cobol
programmers have a  lock on having a superior attitude.  Plently of newbies
who only know the PC environmrnt look down on anything mainframe and legacy
related.

I disagree with point 4 somewhat.  I haven't used VSAM since the middle
1980's.  Most large companies use some form of relational database and if a
smaller company cannot afford a DBMS then having the Cobol file system
available with the language can only be a plus for them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-04T11:43:04
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Pete,

Economies of scale are important to a company that wants to survive.

With your horse and buggy/Ferrari analogy, I would liken COBOL to the
internal combustion engine rather then the vehicle itself. An important
factor here also is maintainability of the code by people other than the
original programmer. As a regular of this group I'm sure you are familiar
with all of the arguments.

Paul
www.microfocus.com
The Future of COBOL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-11-04T12:00:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
"Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote in message news:<aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>...
> Pete,
> 
> Economies of scale are important to a company that wants to survive.
>
Absolutely. 

> With your horse and buggy/Ferrari analogy, I would liken COBOL to the
> internal combustion engine rather then the vehicle itself.

Why?

The engines of the software we currently use for development are not
even remotely related to COBOL.

Would you say a liquid fuelled rocket is just an extended internal
combustion engine? While the Titan IV certainly combusts internally,
it is a far cry from reciprocating pistons.
(Some people might argue this, but I'll warrant that few of them would
be NASA engineers...<G>)
My analogy was intended to convey generations of technolgy, not a
specific technology.

> An important
> factor here also is maintainability of the code by people other than the
> original programmer. As a regular of this group I'm sure you are familiar
> with all of the arguments.
> 
Yes, I am familiar with all the arguments re maintenance of code and
have been familiar with them for the last 37 years.

However, I don't agree that this argument is "an important factor" in
today's World. Modern technology uses Objects and Components, which
(appropriately enough <G>), are made from Object code which nobody
will be maintaining.

The days of Source Code being king are numbered. Modern systems are
developed by re-using Components and Objects, just like building
blocks. The days when we needed to code programs to convert data or
pass it from one process to another are gone. Tools like DTS enable
systems to be built in minutes, which previously would have taken days
or weeks. The Internet has revolutionised the "presentation layer" of
our systems, the advent of "quick build" systems that actually work,
have all contrived to make in-house development and the consequent
requirement for Source Code development and maintenance, less
important. Major (component based) Application packages like SAP,
SIEBEL, Chordiant, etc. will gradually replace the use of bespoke
in-house software.
(It is FAR cheaper to buy a package and use some one-off BPR to bring
the business into line, than it is to maintain a Fortress COBOL shop.)

The old argument that source code must be maintained rings hollow in
the world of New Technology.

The only source code we currently maintain is VB and a little Perl
(which we are phasing out). All of our existing systems will be
replaced by Siebel within the next year.

Despite our business being a major Utility with many millions of
customers, there is NO COBOL on the site, and the last of the IBM
mainframes will be decommisioned shortly.(Along with the last of the
PL/1 code it runs. The last of the PL/1 programmers is due for (only
just) early retirement.)

Given the above, I'm afraid the "ease of maintenance" argument cuts no
ice with me. However, I would allow that, of the procedural
development languages, COBOL, when well written, is certainly the
easiest to maintain.

Pete.


> Paul
> www.microfocus.com
…[107 more quoted lines elided]…
> >                 http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-04T20:24:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211042024.5ce00625@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com>`

```
dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood) wrote 

> Would you say a liquid fuelled rocket is just an extended internal
> combustion engine? While the Titan IV certainly combusts internally,
> it is a far cry from reciprocating pistons.
> (Some people might argue this, but I'll warrant that few of them would
> be NASA engineers...<G>)

A rocket is an external combustion engine in that the combustion
chamber is open to the outside all the time and not just occasionally.

> However, I don't agree that this argument is "an important factor" in
> today's World. Modern technology uses Objects and Components, which
> (appropriately enough <G>), are made from Object code which nobody
> will be maintaining.

It may be argued that objects can be inhereted to change or extend
their behaviour and thus the base classes never need be enhanced. 
However this can lead to layer upon layer upon layer upon layer as
each new feature is required by some application due to changes in the
real world.

At some point you wind up with the OO equivalent of Windows 'DLL hell'
and are forced to rationalise the situation by redesigning the base
classes to reflect the actual new reality.

Experience with OO in 'conventional' programming doesn't transfer well
into business applications.  While much software has rather static
design and requirements, business applications must follow the
business, and this changes to keep competitive.

Just as C shops do little 'maintenance' and Cobol shops spend most of
their time 'maintaining' (actually changing the system to meet new
business needs) so it is rather optimistic to think that business
objects won't need to be 'maintained' just because typical C++
(non-business) object have a history of not needing maintenance.
 
> The days of Source Code being king are numbered. Modern systems are
> developed by re-using Components and Objects, just like building
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> or weeks. 

20 or 30 years ago they said that about 4GLs, such as LINK.  They
were, of course, absolutely right - as long as the required system
fitted into what the 4GL did easily then it was easy to meet the
requirements.

However, as soon as it was required to extend beyond what the 4GL did
then it was necessary to move to a higher level of complexity and a
much deeper understanding of the underlying mechanism otherwise it
became unmanageable.

Of course, all these disadvantages could have been already predicted
and the objects may well already have solved all the unpredicable and
unknowable new ideas for the next few decades.

> (It is FAR cheaper to buy a package and use some one-off BPR to bring
> the business into line, than it is to maintain a Fortress COBOL shop.)

I like the idea of 'bring the business into line'.  That is, you make
the business fit the software, a typical 'suit' and accountant based
attitude. "We can't do that because our computer won't let us".

I follow several competitors in a specific product market area where
one is run 'by the seat of the pants' by the owner, another was taken
over by a conglomerate and is run by an accountant, a third has moved
to being a head office with outsourced warehousing.

Though these are exactly the same industry their computer requirements
are diametrically opposed.  Packaged solutions only work effectively
where the business is also 'packaged'.

> The old argument that source code must be maintained rings hollow in
> the world of New Technology.

Only as long as the business is 'Old Technology', ie the same as
everyone else is now, or actually was last year.
 
> Despite our business being a major Utility with many millions of
> customers, 

Yes, you _are_ a packaged business with a packaed business model and
can well accept a packaged 'major Utility' application.  Just as a new
hamburger joint can be a McDonalds and use a McDonalds packaged
business model and a McDonalds packaged computer solution.

> Given the above, I'm afraid the "ease of maintenance" argument cuts no
> ice with me. 

And will continue to do so for many years, unless those who decide on
the Business Model come up with a cunning new scheme.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-04T20:26:05-08:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-07T02:53:13
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com...
> dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood) wrote
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Combustion does not occur outside the chamber, or outside the rocket.

> > However, I don't agree that this argument is "an important factor" in
> > today's World. Modern technology uses Objects and Components, which
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, a plethora of Class extensions can reduce the effectiveness of OO.
However, my point is that Businesses in general will not be extending the
Classes because they will not be maintaining the code. Instead, the
Component vendor will provide a new Class or Component. It is therefore fair
to argue that there will  be an ongoing requirement for programming on
Vendor sites, for some time yet. However, the days when virtually every
Corporation has its own software development/maintenance dept. (the EDP
department in COBOL's heyday) are definitely numbered. Basically, it is no
longer viable to maintain a bespoke software department in-house for the
majority of modern corporations. The existing IT departments will be used
more for service and help desk support and for operational support of the
Network, not for software development as the term "COBOL programming"
implies.


> At some point you wind up with the OO equivalent of Windows 'DLL hell'
> and are forced to rationalise the situation by redesigning the base
> classes to reflect the actual new reality.
>
It is still easier to do that than to re-write...

> Experience with OO in 'conventional' programming doesn't transfer well
> into business applications.  While much software has rather static
> design and requirements, business applications must follow the
> business, and this changes to keep competitive.
>
 My experience has been that OO programming is more dynamic and responsive
to change than standard structured programming. There is therefore little
problem in it changing as the Business changes. Even so, it won't need to...
Smarter packages will interact with end users and generate whatever is
necessary, including code to utilise existing Components where appropriate.
"Programming" within the next 10 years will have a completely different
meaning than is understood by "COBOL Programming". BPR (so long as you do it
once...) is cheaper than software development.

> Just as C shops do little 'maintenance' and Cobol shops spend most of
> their time 'maintaining' (actually changing the system to meet new
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
My belief is not predicated on the fact that existing non-business C++
objects require little maintenance. I believe what I do because I see it as
a logical extrapolation of current trends, and the necesary response to the
Market Place.

> > The days of Source Code being king are numbered. Modern systems are
> > developed by re-using Components and Objects, just like building
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> requirements.

Well, I don't know who you mean by "they". I was around in IT 30 years ago
and I certainly never predicted that 4GLs would mean the end of programming.
Neither did anyone I remember working with. In fact we smiled when "The Last
One" was introduced (it was supposed to be the last time anyone would need
to write program code). We laughed outright when it was replaced a year
later by "The Next One". (However, our smiles disappeared rapidly when JSP
was mandated not long after that <G>). Object technology is a horse of an
entirely different colour. Combined with the Internet, we at last have a
platform independent technology that is open and will allow data to be
shared by all kinds of "standard" software. This has never been available
before the present time. Coupled with that we have an explosion in computer
literacy, with the average household now containing at least one computer
and users therefore expecting more from commercial systems. These same users
are demanding less technology and more functionality in their workplace
environments. I believe they will get it. The reason they will get it is
because in the entirety of history no-one has ever successfully bucked a
Market...
>
> However, as soon as it was required to extend beyond what the 4GL did
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
May have been true for 4GLs. It is not true for New Technology. More complex
results can be obtained with an even lesser level of understanding. That is
the power of interaction. Smart software simply asks the right questions,
evaluates the answers, then writes the low-level code. If you have ever used
a Wizard, you have seen the beginnings of this process. It is set to explode
in the next few years and will make its way into the commercial arena. Smart
"agents" that will configure your SAP system according to the requirements
you state, are certainly in the pipeline. Extend this idea and it is only a
matter of time before non-technical Business users will be developing and
tailoring their own commercial applications, without any requiremnet for the
intervention of a technical programmer.

> Of course, all these disadvantages could have been already predicted
> and the objects may well already have solved all the unpredicable and
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I agree that this idea has traditionally been anathema to computer people.
We have always striven to ensure that our software met the needs and
requirements of the Business as it existed. The tail does not wag the dog...
At least, it never used to. Times have changed. It is not just the attitude
of a 'suit' (I find it interesting that even in the somewhat conservative
United Kingdom I am no longer required to wear a suit (or even a tie) to
work. And not just on Fridays...Although I may opt to do so if I want to (I
don't <G>)). As mentioned above, there is no longer economic justification
for maintaining in-house software development on an on-going basis when the
Business can be re-engineered without (in most cases) any real
inconvenience. If you can save many thousands of pounds a year (in our case,
I believe the savings will be around �15,000,000 a year, although it will
take around 3 years to break even and recoup the cost of migration and new
development) then it has nothing to do with having a "typical suit and
accountant based attitude".  rather, it is just plain common sense and
simple arithmetic.

> I follow several competitors in a specific product market area where
> one is run 'by the seat of the pants' by the owner, another was taken
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That really depends on how you perceive and define "package". The "packages"
of today are a far cry from the "packages" of yesteryear.

> > The old argument that source code must be maintained rings hollow in
> > the world of New Technology.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Disagree on that. The state of the business technologically has little to do
with its requirement for data. Businesses require timely and accurate data
just the same today as they have throughout history and as they are likely
to do for the foreseeable future. Information is power. You need power to
run a Business. New Technology delivers the power. Old technology will
continue to be bypassed simply because it cannot compete in terms of speed.
(Both old and new technologies are accurate enough; the issue comes down to
one of speed. Speed of development, speed of implementation, speed of
operation).

Source code has as much relevance in this scenario as roller skates do to a
steeplejack.

> > Despite our business being a major Utility with many millions of
> > customers,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Except that we DON'T do that. All our Legacy systems are bespoke. Our New
World systems will use a general purpose package (Siebel) but it will do
exactly what our Busines requires it to in the majority of cases. In the
cases where it won't, the Business processes will be changed to match the
package. There is no problem with this; in fact, it has caused us to review
many of our Business processes and this has been of considerable benefit.

> > Given the above, I'm afraid the "ease of maintenance" argument cuts no
> > ice with me.
>
> And will continue to do so for many years, unless those who decide on
> the Business Model come up with a cunning new scheme.

Whatever cunning new scheme the powers-that-be come up with, procedural
Source code will not figure in it...

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

                http://www.usenet.com
```
