###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-07T00:35:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote
pages and pages of stuff arguing his his component theory, but still 
came to the wrong conclusion, so I snipped it all. <G>.

Peter, I agree with you 100%, components are the wave of the future. 
 All software is going to be constructed out of basic components, glued 
or hacked together with some very basic programming tools.  Basically, 
all that is needed is a common language for moving data into and out of 
components. In effect, what is needed is a Lingua Franca for component glue.

The most percisely defined data description model in existence is the 
Cobol data record defining mechanism, and the most common, precisely 
defined method of moving data from one object definition to another is 
the cobol move statement.  The Cobol data field, with all the 
information about it available at the function level, plus a copy of 
it's own name and picture clause, is the most common bussiness object in 
the world.  Every computer in the world can move data into and out of it 
using precisely the same method: namely "move".  So in one sense you are 
wrong.  Components came to the bussiness world when Grace thought up the 
class ALPHABETIC, and ALPHANUMERIC.  The most common components in the 
world are cobol data fields.

That is why I cannot figure out why the Cobol language is such a poor 
tool for gluing them back together, especially since it is the only one 
that already impliments the MOVE.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-11-11T16:27:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0211111627.4ae5084e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca>`

```
Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca>...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote
> pages and pages of stuff arguing his his component theory, but still 
> came to the wrong conclusion, so I snipped it all. <G>.
> 
Hahaha!

It isn't MY component theory and time will tell whether the conclusion
was wrong or not <G>


> Peter, I agree with you 100%, components are the wave of the future. 

Actually, they are the wave of the present.

The future will use even more powerful technology enabled by OO and
components.

The future will see non-technical users sitting down and interacting
with a computer in plain English (or whatever...) until it achieves
what they want. Once it does this, it will "remember" how to do it.
There will be no programming as we understand the term required. Think
of the Wizard you use to make a new DUN connection... then multiply
that concept by 100 and add iteration and interaction to it...

New technology breakthroughs like Extended UV lithography will enable
the use of plain language interfaces and the use of "smarter" software
to build and generate systems without intervention from Human
Programmers, guided simply by an Application requirement "explained"
in plain language by a "Business User".

I expect to see this within 8 years from now (EUV will be 3 before it
is readily available, and it will take another five for the software
to come of age...)


>  All software is going to be constructed out of basic components, glued 
> or hacked together with some very basic programming tools.  Basically, 
> all that is needed is a common language for moving data into and out of 
> components. In effect, what is needed is a Lingua Franca for component glue.
>
Yes, I already suggested that COBOL would be ideal for this
(especially when constructing Business systems and Business
Components)publicly, in April last year (privately since 1999). But I
was wrong. (Well, not actually wrong, just that this phase of the
development will be very short; a year or two at most, then it will be
overtaken by events.) No sooner will people start constructing systems
in this way than the technology to automate the construction will
arrive, thereby obviating the need to do it manually.
 
> The most percisely defined data description model in existence is the 
> Cobol data record defining mechanism, and the most common, precisely 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The COBOL Language is an excellent tool for gluing them all together
but Object code directly generated without Programmer intervention, is
even better.

There will simply be no need for Procedural code programming. As I
have remarked here once or twice <G>, the need for Source code is
declining. Not just COBOL; anything that requires Procedural source.
We can build functionality and encapsulate it. We can then join up the
encapsulated functionality and make more powerful functionality from
it. The logical extrapolation from that, is for the system to do it
for us. Add to this the impetus derived from 400,000,000 users all
wanting their own computers to do stuff for them, without them having
to learn programming, and it is just a matter of time before the
software described above evolves and is obtainable.

All the system requires is tremendous processing power (by current
standards; EUV will deliver up to 50 times what we have currently -
look for super computers on a desktop within 6 years...) and a Human
to discuss what must be produced. (Not necessarily technical, but
should have some knowledge of the application and be able to interact
with a smart system.)

To those who say "Yeah, yeah, we've heard all this before and COBOL is
still around..." I say that we have never had the hardware and
software capability before that we have today. OO and Components
(Objects) are changing the whole face of programming and providing the
basis for the scenario I have described.

Wrong conclusion? Maybe...but would you bet against it <G>?

Time will tell...

Pete.

PS. Long after this technology arrives, I'll still be writing COBOL
and so will many here... the difference is we won't do it for a
living; we'll do it for small scale "cottage industry" hand crafted
systems which we will probably use primarily for ourselves...because
we love doing it <G>, not because it is commercially viable.
```

- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca> <b3638c46.0211111627.4ae5084e@posting.google.com> <kx_z9.311361$8o3.9245844@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

Read it again, Paul...

I said 8 years (minimum - 10 would be a fair figure).  

I could produce evidence of certain applications where it is ALREADY
possible for a non-technical user to interact with software and have
what he/she wants generated 100%... There are packages for Graphic
Artists which enable them to build killer Web Sites with no knowledge
of HTML, XML, etc. (Yes, currently, they need to learn to use the
package, but every release makes it easier and eventually it will come
down to a natural language interface.)

Multiply the power of today's hardware/software technology many times
(and the indications are that this is already available within certain
labs) and you may not be so ready to risk your hard-earned readies
<G>.

It is foolish to underestimate the capability and imagination of
software engineers, when driven by a multi-trillion dollar Market
Place...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-13T06:56:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DD1F78A.BF3D6E05@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca> <b3638c46.0211111627.4ae5084e@posting.google.com> <kx_z9.311361$8o3.9245844@twister.austin.rr.com> <b3638c46.0211120156.5fa2cbe9@posting.google.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> I could produce evidence of certain applications where it is ALREADY
> possible for a non-technical user to interact with software and have
> what he/she wants generated 100%... 

With a word procesor, such as WordStar even a manager could produce a 3
line memo that was 100% what he wanted 20 years ago.

> There are packages for Graphic
> Artists which enable them to build killer Web Sites with no knowledge
> of HTML, XML, etc. 

Actually this is not an objective example.  Most web site builders start
with a vauge idea of what they want plus some images, and they produce
what the software does easily for them.  This is a compromise where the
target is determined by the capabilities of the software as much as by
the artist making it up as he goes.

This has also been the failing of every 4GL and 'System Generator' in
the past, and will continue to be in the future.  The system will
perform perfectly where the generator's designer has built it to meet
the needs of the given user, and fails miserably on the next user along
whose needs do not match the facilities available, and is not willing to
compromise his objective requirements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-13T00:34:22
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3dd19b66_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca> <b3638c46.0211111627.4ae5084e@posting.google.com> <kx_z9.311361$8o3.9245844@twister.austin.rr.com> <b3638c46.0211120156.5fa2cbe9@posting.google.com> <3DD1F78A.BF3D6E05@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3DD1F78A.BF3D6E05@Azonic.co.nz...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Your point being that if the requirement is simple, I have a fighting chance
of my prophecy coming true?

Hinges on the word "simple".

As users and computers progress, what is complex today becomes simple
tomorrow.

> > There are packages for Graphic
> > Artists which enable them to build killer Web Sites with no knowledge
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That is completely arguable. I would argue that that may have been true to a
large extent 4 years ago. Today, it is much less true. As I can't quantify
it, I won't argue it further... <G>.

> This has also been the failing of every 4GL and 'System Generator' in
> the past, and will continue to be in the future.
Like most people participating in this argument, you have completely failed
to factor in the effects of interaction and iteration (never mind a quantum
leap in computer power). Previous packages did not support this, except at
very rudimentary level, by making you do it over until you got something
closer to what was required. Future software will actively support it and
may even be predicated on it. You will be expected to iterate towards a
better solution and the system will interact with you to find out what it
should discard and what it should carry forward to the next attempt. New
Technology makes it possible to build and discard whole systems in seconds.
We haven't had that capability in the past.

> The system will
> perform perfectly where the generator's designer has built it to meet
> the needs of the given user, and fails miserably on the next user along
> whose needs do not match the facilities available, and is not willing to
> compromise his objective requirements.

This is unimaginative in the extreme. Firstly, the system will be much more
flexible than that, secondly the system will be much more powerful than
that, thirdly, the people will be much more imaginative than that.

There would be no point in approaching the system in the first place if it
couldn't do what you want. If you don't expect to get some benefit from it
why would you sit down with it? If it was completely unable to provide what
is required to the majority of people then it would not exist, as it would
be non-viable in the Market Place.

Yet we see an ever expanding number of sites where successful packages are
being implemented. Someone must benefit from them or we wouldn't. I can't
see SAP or Siebel (for example) being considered as unusable and providing
no benefit.(Apparently, the Market Place doesn't see them that way
either...).

Your comments are valid for the software of the past (and present, to some
extent <G>). They do not attempt to address the software of the future, in
terms that are fair to the software of the future.

Pete..

                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-13T00:13:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211130013.56077cca@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca> <b3638c46.0211111627.4ae5084e@posting.google.com> <kx_z9.311361$8o3.9245844@twister.austin.rr.com> <b3638c46.0211120156.5fa2cbe9@posting.google.com> <3DD1F78A.BF3D6E05@Azonic.co.nz> <3dd19b66_5@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote 
 
> As users and computers progress, what is complex today becomes simple
> tomorrow.

I suspect that the argument also hinges on what or who a 'user' is.

In a warehousing environment the 'user' is the warehouseman, possibly
the supervisor, perhaps the warehouse manager.  The manager may have a
general view of what happens, the warehouseman is the only one that
cares enough about the detail, but only about the detail in his
immediate vicinity.

The whole point of having 'systems analysts' and/or 'business
analysts' is that they are trained to avoid the bias that each
different user has.

In my experience the most that any user knows about what is required
is about 60% of their actual needs.  This is because those above and
below fake the rest.
 
> This is unimaginative in the extreme. Firstly, the system will be much more
> flexible than that, 

The system will only be as flexible as it is implemented to be.

Businesses can be a lot more imaginative then you give them credit.

> secondly the system will be much more powerful than
> that, 

Being able to set an option and rebuild the whole system in 3 seconds
does not mean that that it can do the type of system that it hasn't
been designed to work with.

Does it do 'loyalty cards' ? No, not unless someone has sat down and
created the systems components that will deal with loyalty cards.

Does it do shipment costings based on import duty rates ? No, not
unless someone has sat down and created the components that will know
about duty rates.

> thirdly, the people will be much more imaginative than that.

What ? _users_ ?

> There would be no point in approaching the system in the first place if it
> couldn't do what you want. If you don't expect to get some benefit from it
> why would you sit down with it? If it was completely unable to provide what
> is required to the majority of people then it would not exist, as it would
> be non-viable in the Market Place.

Which 'majority of people' ?  As I said before, you are a 'utility'
company, it is likely that you do much the same as many other
utilities with just a few differences. Given that some software
developers have worked with several similar companies they have most
likely covered most of what you want. Most other utility companies
could expect that the system you would use would cover most of their
needs.

That same system would also do much of what any corporate would do in
billing, sales, servicing, probably even planned maintenance of the
fleet.

Would it do Freight Forwarding ?  Customs clearances ?  Export
documentation ?  Dangerous goods warehousing ? RF warehouse operation
? Container ship planning ?

Probably not unless someone sat down and wrote scads of new
components.

Would it be effective at these tasks ?

Probably not.  It is not enough to be able to _do_ the task, it needs
to be done _efficiently_ (this is not computer efficiency, this is
user efficiency).

It is not enough to put a 'tick in the box'.

Just because the system can generate several different ways of doing
debtors systems does not mean that one of these matches what is
actually required by some fringe operation.

They may be able to 'make do' with it.

> Yet we see an ever expanding number of sites where successful packages are
> being implemented. Someone must benefit from them or we wouldn't. I can't
> see SAP or Siebel (for example) being considered as unusable and providing
> no benefit.(Apparently, the Market Place doesn't see them that way
> either...).

Of course, common systems are becoming commoditised.  30 years ago
every debtors system was bespoke.  Now packages and component systems
have reduced the need for writing new ones.  That just means that more
esoteric systems can be added that are not available anywhere as a
package.  Eventually those will become packages and even newer areas
will be the leading edge.

Some business plans are already fully packaged, some haven't been
started yet.

> Your comments are valid for the software of the past (and present, to some
> extent <G>). They do not attempt to address the software of the future, in
> terms that are fair to the software of the future.

I think that you are missing how inventive businesses can be - more so
than developers who will always be trying to catch up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-11-13T10:20:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0211131020.23bc5810@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca> <b3638c46.0211111627.4ae5084e@posting.google.com> <kx_z9.311361$8o3.9245844@twister.austin.rr.com> <b3638c46.0211120156.5fa2cbe9@posting.google.com> <3DD1F78A.BF3D6E05@Azonic.co.nz> <3dd19b66_5@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0211130013.56077cca@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0211130013.56077cca@posting.google.com>...

<snipped reasoned and interesting response>

After reading and thinking on your response, Richard, I realised that
I had not made something plain in my discussion.

I never intended to suggest a "one size fits all" package that would
handle everything from loyalty cards to container shipping via a
single interface.

What I intended was generic software that would address each type of
Business.

The fact that I am currently in the Utility business has no bearing on
my opinion about this. I have worked in most areas of commerce during
my career. I see the similarities and the differences between one type
of enterprise and another.

I firmly believe that software development across the board will
change in the future, along the lines I have outlined. However, I
believe that it will be oriented towards individual industries.

It's a bit like what happened in the past...we had bespoke software
development using COBOL or whatever, but it was still oriented towards
a given enterprise. No-one would expect to pick up programs from a
Trucking company and use them for airline reservations.

My argument is aimed towards the WAY in which I believe software will
be developed (a shift towards Business Users doing it for themselves)
and not the TYPE of software.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-13T18:11:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211131811.76148f3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca> <b3638c46.0211111627.4ae5084e@posting.google.com> <kx_z9.311361$8o3.9245844@twister.austin.rr.com> <b3638c46.0211120156.5fa2cbe9@posting.google.com> <3DD1F78A.BF3D6E05@Azonic.co.nz> <3dd19b66_5@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0211130013.56077cca@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211131020.23bc5810@posting.google.com>`

```
dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood) wrote 

> I firmly believe that software development across the board will
> change in the future, along the lines I have outlined. However, I
> believe that it will be oriented towards individual industries.

However, many of the clients that I work for are crossing boundaries
and require software that will work together even though it is parts
of several 'industries'.

> It's a bit like what happened in the past...we had bespoke software
> development using COBOL or whatever, but it was still oriented towards
> a given enterprise. No-one would expect to pick up programs from a
> Trucking company and use them for airline reservations.

No, but if a Trucking company also did warehousing and customs
clearances and had a few freight aircraft (as they do) then it would
expect to have one system (ie one customer file) that would integrate
all these operations.

Now it is often necessary to have some glue to patch different systems
together, bespoke glue.

> My argument is aimed towards the WAY in which I believe software will
> be developed (a shift towards Business Users doing it for themselves)
> and not the TYPE of software.

Of course you haven't designated just who these 'users' are.  I would
not expect a warehouseman to do this.  Accounts might be able to
'design' for the accounting needs, but my experience is that when
accounts are involved in choosing or specifying software they are
clueless about the needs of dealing with the products.
If what you mean by 'users' is a committee of accounts, managers,
marketers, salesmen, and warehousemen then they will create what any
commitee does.

If, however, you just mean an employee, such as a 'business analyst'
or 'systems analyst' capable of crossing all divisions then this is
how it has been done for decades, but they are not what I call a
'user'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-14T10:47:40
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3dd37c9c_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca> <b3638c46.0211111627.4ae5084e@posting.google.com> <kx_z9.311361$8o3.9245844@twister.austin.rr.com> <b3638c46.0211120156.5fa2cbe9@posting.google.com> <3DD1F78A.BF3D6E05@Azonic.co.nz> <3dd19b66_5@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0211130013.56077cca@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0211131020.23bc5810@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211131811.76148f3@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0211131811.76148f3@posting.google.com...
> dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood) wrote
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
What I have outlined doesn't preclude that. It just wouldn't happen on day
1. Once you had systems working for "area1" and "area2" you would then work
with integration management software that could look at both these systems,
"understand" the objects involved, and then find what flows you wanted
between them by interaction with you.


> > It's a bit like what happened in the past...we had bespoke software
> > development using COBOL or whatever, but it was still oriented towards
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Why a customer file? Certainly it would expect customers to be able to use
trucks or aircraft if that is what it had on offer (without a duplicate
customer base) but that is an innate part of using objects. The customer
object could connect with a truck object or a plane object. None of this
would be visible to the Users who built the software... they would just see
an integrated functionality.

> Now it is often necessary to have some glue to patch different systems
> together, bespoke glue.
>
Yes, it certainly is at the moment. I am suggesting that this glue in the
future will be invisible and provided by the system automatically as it
derives from you what you want connected to what and how. In other words the
Business Process will drive the development of the software. (This is
probably an "end point" of computing as it is what we have been seeking all
along - computers that can interact "intelligently" with Humans to do what
the Humans require of them, WITHOUT the need for computer programming
knowledge. I have little faith in Artificial Intelligence as it stands at
the moment so I see that as being far in the future, but in the meantime,
human intelligence can be used by interaction and iteration. It comes down
to a non-technical interface that will allow the Human to describe what is
required and the computer to provide it. I don't see this as far in the
future - maybe 10 years...)

> > My argument is aimed towards the WAY in which I believe software will
> > be developed (a shift towards Business Users doing it for themselves)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> clueless about the needs of dealing with the products.
I use the term USER as anyone who has a requirement for computer processing.
The Business processes will provide the basis, and the computer system will
provide automation and data processing where required, to support the
process (much as it does now...except that we need a bunch of technocrats to
provide the interface between man and machine. I see the need for this
disappearing. We only have it now because an untrained user cannot make a
computer do anything. That is what the software I am describing will
change.)

> If what you mean by 'users' is a committee of accounts, managers,
> marketers, salesmen, and warehousemen then they will create what any
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'user'.

Certainly, the people in these roles will be the first generation of
'users'.

I don't share your scepticism about joint working (committees) as I have
seen this work very well in certain environments. I agree that some
committees are useless, but that doesn't mean that ALL committees are.

On RAD projects, joint development workshops can be used to ensure that all
stakeholders are represented in the end solution and are happy with it. This
is actually better than a business analyst gathering requirements and
interpreting them with the consequent room for error and misunderstanding in
the "translation" from Business requirement into a program spec. If the
programmers are party to the joint workshop they understand exactly what is
required and a better result is invariably produced. This is one instance of
a "committee" working successfully...

As I said in earlier post I see no reason why the software I am describing
could not be used in such an environment to generate a prototype of the
required functionality then and there. The stakeholders can look at it,
refine it, and iterate it, until a result that suits everybody is achieved.
We do this currently with screen formats and reports; it is a logical
progression to do it with the whole system (if the technology can generate
the whole system; I believe the time is coming when it will be capable of
doing that.)

Pete.
```


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus now supports Microsoft .NET and IBM eSeries and iSeries

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-11-12T15:53:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0211121552.189df4cc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <3DCA25E0.1020604@Sympatico.ca> <b3638c46.0211111627.4ae5084e@posting.google.com> <aqr42h$dq1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<aqr42h$dq1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> > There will simply be no need for Procedural code programming
> 
> 
> I am trying to picture a way to remove all the procedural elements of business
> rules.  Maybe you could help me.

There is no need to do that. The procedures in the Business can be
implemented in many ways and Business rules can be computerized in
many ways. Emulating them in procedural code is just one way. It
happens to be the way it has  traditionally been done, and it is the
way that COBOL does it. We use variables in computer storage to
represent the data elements that occur in the Business and apply
procedural code to them, manipulating and modelling what happens in
real life. It is simply a question of emulating these rules in
procedural code.

But this is not the only way.

Non-procedural systems can encapsulate these variables and cause them
to behave the same way they do in the real world when certain stimuli
are applied. However the process is more dynamic and flexible. Instead
of having to repeat these objects in every procedure that references
them, we can simply extend their behaviour to accommodate new or
modified processes. This is possible because they contain their own
behaviours.

I am saying that the time will come when users will be able to
"explain" what the rules are in simple language by interaction with a
smart system. (This will be useful, particularly in cases where the
rules have not been definitively identified; the interactive process
helps to "gel" the Human thought process - I have seen this effect in
RAD workshops. When people are required to interactively discuss
business processes, everybody present ends up with a better and wider
understanding. At the moment we do this in Workshops but there's no
fundamental reason why a smart computer couldn't participate as just
another Workshop member.)

When the user (or users, if it is a joint development Workshop) are
happy that the rules required are identified and the data to support
them is identified, the computer can generate the necessary Object
definition.

This Object can then be incorporated into further system development.
I believe the period of manually putting these together will be a
short one (maybe a couple of years) and eventually (again, by
interaction) the computer will simply connect the right Objects in the
right way and generate the required system functionality.

Tools like DTS (MSs Data Transformation Services - a part of SQL
Server - already allow flows of data to be manipulated by moving icons
on a screen. Quite complex interactions conversions, sorts etc. can be
done with a few mouse clicks. The same stuff in procedural code would
be dozens of programs and thousands of lines of code. Instead you can
do it in minutes (seconds, actually...<G>) and it generates what is
required, instantly. The step from
data flow to data Object is a very short one and I understand that a
version which will manipulate and connect Objects is in the pipeline.)

Business Procedures can be implemented WITHOUT Procedural code.

In my opinion, they should be... but then I WOULD say that, it is the
OO way...<G>

Pete.
```

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote 

> > A rocket is an external combustion engine in that the combustion
> > chamber is open to the outside all the time and not just occasionally.
> >
> Combustion does not occur outside the chamber, or outside the rocket.

Irrelevant.  A steam locomotive also keeps its combustion entirely
_inside_ the firebox and this is inside the engine.

The test is whether the combustion is _open_ to the outside, not
whether it is within the boundry of the unit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT - External vs. Internal combustion engines

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-12T19:51:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DD15BAD.1000305@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0211112359.3563b310@posting.google.com> <s54A9.1635$vM1.127899@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:217e491a.0211112359.3563b310@posting.google.com...

>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>whether it is within the boundry of the unit.

That's not what various sites around the Web say:

http://www.rmi.org/sitepages/pid456.php

External-Combustion Engine

External-combustion engines include steam engines and Stirling-cycle 
engines. Stirling engines may be a promising technology for HypercarSM 
vehicles. They burn their fuel in a single chamber or furnace, with the 
heat from this combustion driving the pistons up and down in their 
cylinders to rotate the crankshaft. This arrangement allows for 
increased efficiency over internal-combustion engines because the 
combustion takes place continuously, instead of in a series of 
explosions (which inevitably leave more unburned fuel). Efficiencies of 
up to 60 percent are theoretically possible, compared to 45 percent for 
diesel engines and not much more than 30 percent for standard Otto engines.

Although Stirling engines haven't yet been used in any production 
vehicles, most early problems of bulk and reliability appear to have 
been ironed out, and current prototypes offer the prospect of lower 
mass, fewer parts, less noise, and much lower emissions than diesel 
counterparts


http://www.howstuffworks.com/engine1.htm

There is such a thing as an external combustion engine. A steam engine 
in old-fashioned trains and steam boats is the best example of an 
external combustion engine. The fuel (coal, wood, oil, whatever) in a 
steam engine burns outside the engine to create steam, and the steam 
creates motion inside the engine. It turns out internal combustion is a 
lot more efficient (takes less fuel per mile) than external combustion, 
plus an internal combustion engine is a lot smaller than an equivalent 
external combustion engine. This explains why we don't see any cars from 
Ford and GM using steam engines.

   ------------------------------------------------------------

It seems to have to do with where the combustion takes place relative to 
the engine. All engines must be open to the outside at least part of the 
time, or else they would explode.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - External vs. Internal combustion engines

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-12T15:36:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211121536.3b0ffc54@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0211112359.3563b310@posting.google.com> <s54A9.1635$vM1.127899@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3DD15BAD.1000305@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote 

> >>Combustion does not occur outside the chamber, or outside the rocket.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >whether it is within the boundry of the unit.
 
> That's not what various sites around the Web say:
 
> External-combustion engines include steam engines and Stirling-cycle 
> engines. ...

This makes no exclusions, nor does it claim to be an exhaustive list. 
Discussing stirling engines makes no contribution to the issue at
hand.

> increased efficiency over internal-combustion engines because the 
> combustion takes place continuously, 

Rockets are continuous too.

> There is such a thing as an external combustion engine. A steam engine 
> in old-fashioned trains and steam boats is the best example of an 
> external combustion engine. 

As I said.  Whether the combustion is within the unit or not is
irrelevant.

> It seems to have to do with where the combustion takes place relative to 
> the engine. All engines must be open to the outside at least part of the 
> time, or else they would explode.

An internal combustion engine is not open to the outside at the time
that the combustion takes place, thus the _combustion_ is not open to
the outside.
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-11-12T15:19:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0211121519.269190a1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e7794975.0210300704.3e4aba67@posting.google.com> <01c28157$2b3310e0$abc2f943@chottel> <3dc538dd_6@Usenet.com> <aq5mjg$kt8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <b3638c46.0211041200.ebec68c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211042026.3442d221@posting.google.com> <3dc9d3ac_1@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0211112359.3563b310@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0211112359.3563b310@posting.google.com>...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote 
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> whether it is within the boundry of the unit.

Hmmm...so you would argue that at the moment of combustion in a car
engine (for example), because all the valves are closed, it is
"internal combustion", even though the chamber itself is vented to the
outside via the inlet and exhaust manifolds?

You may be right; I don't know enough about engineering to know
whether this is the formal definition of an internal combustion engine
or not...However, this lack of formal qualification hasn't stopped me
maintaining and repairing cars all my life <G>

I'll recap the point I was trying to make...(which had very little to
do with internal combustion or rocket engines for that matter)

New Technology is replacing the old just as horsedrawn buggies were
replaced by cars. We have two choices:

1. Buy a car (for serious travel, but maybe use the buggy at weekends
for fun...)

2. Decide that cars are noisy and dangerous and difficult to
understand so we'll stick with what we know thank you very much and
pass the nosebag...
(The drawback with this approach is that when we set out to go
somewhere we find that the people with cars got there before us. If it
is just an outing with the kids to the beach, this may not be so
important; if it is for a job interview or to get around our customers
and sell our product, it DOES matter.)

I used engines in the previous analogy because the engines my team use
to develop commercial computer systems are as far removed from COBOL
as a rocket is from the internal combustion engine.

And that's where we came in...<G>

Pete.
```

<a id="t-1428178ec4"></a>
## Thread: Cobol EXTERNAL question — 6 msgs

### Cobol EXTERNAL question

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T04:02:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2815b$7f4aa2c0$abc2f943@chottel>`

```
I have not really used EXTERNAL before.  I coded the following:

     FD  STDIN-FILE EXTERNAL
           LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
           DATA RECORD IS STDIN-REC.
       01  STDIN-REC.
           05  STDIN-CHAR          PIC X     OCCURS 80 TIMES
                                             INDEXED BY STDIN-INDEX.

I was suprised to find STDIN-INDEX does not have the EXTERNAL attribute.  

The only way I can think of to do what I want is to code:

     FD  STDIN-FILE EXTERNAL
           LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
           DATA RECORD IS STDIN-RECORD.
       01  STDIN-RECORD         PIC X(80).

WORKING-STORAGESECTION.
       01  EXTERNAL-DATA IS EXTERNAL.
             03  STDIN-REC
                  05  STDIN-CHAR          PIC X     OCCURS 80 TIMES
                                                      INDEXED BY
STDIN-INDEX.

and then do READ STDIN-FILE INTO STDIN-REC

I would prefer to not have to do a READ ... INTO  does anyone have any
alternative suggestions.

Before you say "don't use EXTERNAL" or "why are you using "EXTERNAL" let me
explain.  I am reading  a book on compilers which has a simple one pass
compiler program written in C and I am trying to do the equivalent program
in Cobol.  At first I was going to use nested programs and GLOBAL but three
of the CALLed programs have to be RECURSIVE and nested programs cannot be
RECURSIVE.  That seems like a silly restriction.  Of course EXTERNAL data
items cannot have value clauses and that also seems silly.  This project is
for self-traing/learning only.

I want to share the file among the programs and I want to simulate C's one
character at a time I/O.  Any program which reads STDIN will PERFORM a
paragraph, 1100-GETCHAR, which will be the same in each program and will
use STDIN-INDEX which must be the same field in each program and hence
EXTERNAL.  Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol EXTERNAL question

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T14:00:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qOvw9.3677$mN6.1056446@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<01c2815b$7f4aa2c0$abc2f943@chottel>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c2815b$7f4aa2c0$abc2f943@chottel...
> I have not really used EXTERNAL before.  I coded the following:

>      FD  STDIN-FILE EXTERNAL
...
>        01  STDIN-REC.
>            05  STDIN-CHAR          PIC X     OCCURS 80 TIMES
>                                              INDEXED BY STDIN-INDEX.
>
> I was suprised to find STDIN-INDEX does not have the EXTERNAL attribute.

It works that way because that's the way EXTERNAL works: It only shares the
file buffer. Datanames are different.

Usual way to handle this is with the same copylib in each program...

FILE SECTION.
    COPY  'FD_FOR_FILE_TO_BE_SHARED' .

... or by using READ INTO in each Program


In your case..
>I want to share the file among the programs and I want to simulate C's one
>character at a time I/O.  Any program which reads STDIN will PERFORM a
>paragraph, 1100-GETCHAR, which will be the same in each program and will
>use STDIN-INDEX which must be the same field in each program and hence
>EXTERNAL.

Instead of the same 1100-GETCHAR paragraph in each program, you could use a
copylib for a complete nested program or a CALL to a completely separate
module which accepts the buffer and the number of characters valid as
parameters....although this is bass-ackwards from what I've seen and done...
the separate module would normally be a common I-O module which would 'get a
record from file' on command.


MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol EXTERNAL question

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T15:20:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c281ba$369aa5c0$5c95f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c2815b$7f4aa2c0$abc2f943@chottel> <qOvw9.3677$mN6.1056446@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```

shortsighted.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol EXTERNAL question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T11:47:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<apuet2$lsg$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<01c2815b$7f4aa2c0$abc2f943@chottel>`

```
Possibly not what you want, but try,

      FD  STDIN-FILE EXTERNAL
            LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
            RECORDING MODE IS F
            BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
            DATA RECORD IS STDIN-REC.
        01  STDIN-REC.
            05  STDIN-CHAR          PIC X     OCCURS 80 TIMES
                                              INDEXED BY STDIN-INDEX.

          ...
   Working-Storage Section.
   01  Saved-Index   Usage Index   External.
        ...
   Read ....
   Set Saved-Index to STDIN-INDEX
   Call ...


An "index" is not truly a data-item (although some/most implementations may
treat it as such). Therefore, if you want to "externalize" an index, you
need to use an INDEX-DATA-(ITEM.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol EXTERNAL question

- **From:** philhickling@compuserve.com (Phil Hickling)
- **Date:** 2002-11-07T03:00:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a371be2.0211070300.e76991@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<01c2815b$7f4aa2c0$abc2f943@chottel>`

```
I would have thought that your original code should work, so long as
you have the file select and FD statements in every program that wants
access to the data.  (It is obviously best therefore to put it in a
copy library).

I use EXTERNAL a lot, both for files and data items.  It is easier to
code than using LINKAGE sections to pass data, and seems to work well.
 Your original question hinted that it isn't good practice - why?

Phil Hickling
www.willowtreecomputing.co.uk
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol EXTERNAL question

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-11-08T01:14:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c286c4$3f66aa00$9293f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c2815b$7f4aa2c0$abc2f943@chottel> <6a371be2.0211070300.e76991@posting.google.com>`

```
Even if every program  had the file SELECT and FD statements, I believe
each program would have its own local index item pointing to the next
character to be read.  Updating and index in one program would not change
the indexes in the other programs. I have not yet reached the testing stage
as an avalanche of interruptions of various types has distracted me
temporarily.  

As to good practice: Many people whose religious affiliation is "orthodox
software engineering" claim that globally accessible data is bad i.e. a
mortal sin. It violates the principle of encapsulation and information
hiding. Data should be passed by parameters and never by reference.  The
same people will tell you that the only good PERFORM statement is an inline
PERFORM.  All other PERFORM should be replaced by CALL statements.

I personally trust myself to only modify data appropriately. I find global
data can greatly reduce parameter passing. I try to avoid GOTO but if I can
simplify higly complicated nested IF I will occassionally GOTO an exit
paragraph.  Also when simulating recursion some people use a GOTO to remove
tail recursion. I don't mind other than inline PERFORM but writting these
programs using CALL has reminded me of the benefits of this choice for
providing Cobol equivalents of C library routines. The larger the program
that you write the more you will benefit from the othodox approach. 

Phil Hickling <philhickling@compuserve.com> wrote in article
<6a371be2.0211070300.e76991@posting.google.com>...
> I would have thought that your original code should work, so long as
> you have the file select and FD statements in every program that wants
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-1d4b7e93f2"></a>
## Thread: TOD Clock — 2 this month / 8 total

_Thread started [2002-10](2002-10.md#t-1d4b7e93f2)._

#### ↳ Re: TOD Clock

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T05:54:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DC21726.9090806@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<905b937a.0210301212.7a98ae0c@posting.google.com>`

```
simon wrote:
> I am looking for way to use TOD CLOCK in my application. Any idea?

 From COBOL?  Probably not a good idea.  Hardware TOD CLOCK values are 
ill suited to manipulation in COBOL (52-bit integer, scaled in a double 
word) and will wrap sometime relatively soon (2043?), possibly 
introducing failures in applications that use it directly and expect it 
to be monotone increasing.

Unless there is an LE subroutine that will return and interpret TOD 
CLOCK values, this would take an Assembler subroutine to invoke the 
special hardware instructions to store the value, and possibly more 
assembler code to trace through the system control blocks to get the 
doubleword integer local time offset and leap-second values and perform 
required arithmetic if the real object is high resolution local time. 
Because of the 2043 exposure mentioned above, it's probably not a good 
idea to introduce new direct TOD CLOCK dependencies in Assembler code 
either.

Newer processors have a new extended length (128-bit) hardware TOD CLOCK 
to resolve both the 2043 problem and the need for finer resolution on 
faster processors, but this would also be a format that doesn't mesh 
well with COBOL.

If your object is to get date and time, you want to stick to support 
provided in standard COBOL interfaces and subroutines.
```

#### ↳ Re: TOD Clock

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T21:56:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DC2F863.4020708@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<905b937a.0210301212.7a98ae0c@posting.google.com>`

```
simon wrote:
> I am looking for way to use TOD CLOCK in my application. Any idea?

How do you propose to get access to it in a COBOL program?

BTW, what are you planning to use it for?
```

```
Robert Graham wrote:
> I used the Caldera Open Linux for a while as an experiment, but it kept 
> crashing and I'd have to reload it.  May be I'll try SuSE next.

Follow up:

Some other folks recommended the Mandrake distro, so I downloaded the 
9.0 ISO images, burned the CD's & tried to install. I had install 
problems both trying a dual boot install (with W2K) on my desktop and as 
a standalone system on my laptop (ThinkPad). Someone suggested trying 
the previous version, 8.2. That wouldn't install on my laptop at all.

I decided it was time to change my approach, so I downloaded RedHat's 
8.0 distribution and that installed without a glitch, on my laptop 
first, then a dual boot setup with W2K on my desktop (24 hours ago).

In both cases I was online less than an hour after the install routines 
completed, tougher to figure out how to define my laser printer plugged 
into my router, but done. I'm unable to play mp3 files and I'd like to 
map or define the Windows HDD's to Linux so I have access to everything.

It's like 30 years ago, I'm on the flat part of the learning curve with 
Linux; but at least I understand what I need to master here, e.g., the 
new terminology and how to navigate Linux. I figure I need a book or two 
to get the "Concepts & Facilities" level info and to start looking into 
the Linux magazines, I've learned a lot from the PC magazines over the 
years.

My $.02 anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux (was Re: OT : Nigerian Email Scams)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-10T07:49:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3D87A5EC.E1CC274E@shaw.ca> <3D87CBBE.6C919885@sprynet.com> <3DC2070F.F5A8B5B0@sbcglobal.net> <3DC2FC5E.3080405@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DC32E68.A5755B64@sprynet.com> <3DC36E7F.1060608@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DCBCF5F.2070807@nycap.rr.com> <3DCCC2C8.4060803@optonline.SPAMNOT.net>`

```
Liam Devlin wrote:

> I'd like to
> map or define the Windows HDD's to Linux so I have access to everything.

Samba does that.  It provides an SMB server and also has a client file
system for mapping to Windows shares:  mount -f smbfs //winmachine/C
/mnt/winmachine
```

```
I managed to figure this out yesterday, needed to add the "ntfs" 
definition to the kernel. Now I can mount the Window's HDD's RO, e.g.:

mount -r ntfs /dev/hdb1 /mnt/wind

where I've previously defined a "/mnt/wind" directory.

Does Samba permit updating? (not that I'm planning anything at this point)

I've seen lots of posts concerning Samba, is it new or troublesome?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux (was Re: OT : Nigerian Email Scams)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-11T11:05:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DCF8EF7.BBC6E69E@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3D87A5EC.E1CC274E@shaw.ca> <3D87CBBE.6C919885@sprynet.com> <3DC2070F.F5A8B5B0@sbcglobal.net> <3DC2FC5E.3080405@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DC32E68.A5755B64@sprynet.com> <3DC36E7F.1060608@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DCBCF5F.2070807@nycap.rr.com> <3DCCC2C8.4060803@optonline.SPAMNOT.net> <3DCE0F9A.4013FF8F@Azonic.co.nz> <3DCEBFC5.8070000@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
Liam Devlin wrote:
>
> >>I'd like to
> >>map or define the Windows HDD's to Linux so I have access to everything.

> mount -r ntfs /dev/hdb1 /mnt/wind
> 
> where I've previously defined a "/mnt/wind" directory.
> 
> Does Samba permit updating? (not that I'm planning anything at this point)

You are wanting to map local HD partitions to Linux, while I thought you
wanted to map networked machines.  Samba is for networking.

NTFS partitions are particularly troublesome because NT is designed that
way.  If it were easy to access an NTFS partition from another system
then the whole idea of MS's security goes out the window ;-)

Actually there have been ways of getting at NTFS partitions for some
time, such as from OS/2.  MS changes it at each release though just to
make it difficult for others.
 
> I've seen lots of posts concerning Samba, is it new or troublesome?

It's been around for years.
```

```
Thanks for the info on compilers.  About networking - I have a very 
basic network (actually the 4 machines go thru a router to get to the 
internet ((Road Runner Broadband Cable))).  I've got them to share files 
  (windows is not as friendly as Bill Gates makes it out to be).  Two 
machines are Windows ME and two are Windows XP.  They all share files 
well, but the XP wont recoginze the printer on an ME machine.  They did 
recognize the printer I had hooked to the router (SMC Barcade) but that 
laser needs a new drum unit which I don't want to spring for just now.

The question is, if I put a Linux machine on the network, what problems 
will I have talking to Windows machines beyond file share (which I was 
able to do in the Caldera version, including file updating, although it 
was cleaner when I had Star Office on both ops.  However, as mentioned 
before, I found Caldera unstable.)

Thanks,

Bob
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux (was Re: OT : Nigerian Email Scams)

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-11T13:11:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211111311.6e4deb02@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3D87A5EC.E1CC274E@shaw.ca> <3DC2FC5E.3080405@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DC32E68.A5755B64@sprynet.com> <3DC36E7F.1060608@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DCBCF5F.2070807@nycap.rr.com> <3DCCC2C8.4060803@optonline.SPAMNOT.net> <3DCE0F9A.4013FF8F@Azonic.co.nz> <3DCEBFC5.8070000@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DCF8EF7.BBC6E69E@Azonic.co.nz> <3DCED9BF.2030703@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <qfCz9.6790$hK4.625309@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3DCF3236.5020704@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DCFCBE6.1020904@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote 

> The question is, if I put a Linux machine on the network, what problems 
> will I have talking to Windows machines beyond file share (which I was 
> able to do in the Caldera version, including file updating, although it 
> was cleaner when I had Star Office on both ops. 

The main problem I had using Samba was related to the Windows machines
password encryption.  Once fixed (years ago) it has mostly just
worked.  Actually I do have another problem with a Windows machine
that has a CD drive and a DVD/CDR. The DVD is accessed fine by the DVD
player, and is F: to a DOS prompt, but it doesn't show up in Windows
Explorer so I can't make it sharable.  If there was a command line
access to sharing still (there was in W4W 3.11) then I could share F:
drive.

'Beyond filesharing' you can set up Linux with Apache as an IntraNet,
and PostgreSQL (or MySQL) and unixODBC as a Databse server. With these
then you will have a shared database resource accessible from most
languages (Cobol, Python, CobolScript, PERL, PHP) and from Star
Office/OpenOffice.org via web browsing, ODBC or directly.

Also you can use VNC to put a Windows screen on your Linux machine so
that you can use both at the same time from one keyboard/monitor.
```

```
You're not by any chance running Windows ME or something 
other than XP?  I've had so much trouble with two optical 
divices (either CD and CD-RW, or CD and DVD) that the 
machine has been getting as much service as one of those old 
British LeLand cars.  Usually the DVD plays everything fine, 
and the CD will read data CD's but the CD will not play 
music.  Often times the system locked up when using the 
secondary CD, requiring a cold boot.  Other times, the sound 
drivers mysteriously disapear.  So..., if you are not using 
XP consider upgrading, it seems to have fixed lots of 
ME/2000/98/95 problems (not that it doesn't come with its 
own undocumented bugs, I mean "enhancements".)
```

```
Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote 
 
> Well, until Linux runs windows programs as seemlessly as 
> Windows, I'll have to hope the Gates people get it as right 
> as possible.

I keep the old '98 machine to cover the residual Windows stuff I
require. Actually I do have Win4Lin which runs an actual copy of Win98
in a Linux window, but it is just as easy on a separate machine as I
have a few machines networked together. If I am too lazy to go to the
other screen/keyboard I can just use VNC to bring it to me.
 
> TigerDirect.com is selling a "Lindows" computer that is 
> supposed to include Windows drivers to allow Windows 
> programs to work.  Anyone know about this Lindows Op?  It 
> has as a core Linux but I don't know who is behind it.

It uses an enhanced WINE that includes support for DirectX.  It can
run many Windows programs, even some games.  It may be useful if there
are specific requirements for actual Windows programs - but not enough
to justify Win4Lin or VMWare or Plex (the last two run a Windows OS
_and_ Linux at the same time on one machine).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux (was Re: OT : Nigerian Email Scams)

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-18T12:41:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211181241.20e033b5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3D87A5EC.E1CC274E@shaw.ca> <3DCF3236.5020704@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DCFCBE6.1020904@nycap.rr.com> <217e491a.0211111311.6e4deb02@posting.google.com> <3DD15D97.4050402@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <217e491a.0211121934.15c64be7@posting.google.com> <ar0tfa0ov6@enews4.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0211141827.7381eb9c@posting.google.com> <3DD4F846.1080501@nycap.rr.com> <217e491a.0211151425.4ade814b@posting.google.com> <arav24$6ve$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote

> > No, I will not be using XP, ever.
> 
> A definite statement such as that probably means you don't like its registration policy. 

With previous versions of Windows, agreeing to the EULA granted MS, or
its agents, the _right_ to enter your premises and inspect any and all
your computers and require that you supply all material required to
prove correct licencing. It is not that I couldn't do this, but I have
inadequate filing systems.

By not registering I avoideed giving MS, and the Spanish Inquisition,
the address of the premises they thought that they should have the
right to enter.

With XP SP1, and other later EULAs that MS insist on, they now have
the _right_ to connect to your machine, change any and all programs,
upgrade or delete, examine data and block any operation.  This is
irrespective of whether you have turned off 'auto update' or not.

I won't give that right to anyone.

> And that you are self-employed.
 
And I also work for clients who have tried XP and will be dumping it.

> Objection to its registration policy is a political choice.  

Is 'non-objection' also a 'political choice' ?  Did you not object
because it is good for the US economy ?  Did you not object because
that is 'one in the eye' of the DoJ ?

Or is it only 'objection' that is a political choice ?

> XP is technically superior to any non NT windows 

Well, MS would say that.  However, if you wish to argue 'technical
superiority' then why restrict the comparison to Win 3.11-Win98 ?  In
many ways XP is technically inferior to WinNT (for example the changes
needed to allow games to run compromises the system).

For the vast majority of Windows users 'technical superiority' is just
more marketing goobledegook.  They neither know nor care what that is
about, but buy XP or not based on the same reason they choose their
cars - how many sparkly things it has, or says it has in the brochure
(little cries of joy when they discover that the new model has cup
holders that can adjust to _two_ sizes of cups).

For most users Win98 is 'good enough' for what they want to do.  MS is
trying to prove that it is 'hated' and 'insecure' and is, well, crap
actually.  In fact a decent firewall and _not_ using outlook or IIS
(there are alternatives) is enough to provide sufficient security.

If 'technical superiority' was a decision factor then Windows wouldn't
get on the list. For example: FAT, FAT32, NTFS ?  only MS could
consider these to be adequate as file systems, technically they are
rather poor.
On another note, I hate the condescending paternalism of Windows, the
fact that 'MS knows best' and will reset changes that I make to try to
get the machine to work the way that _I_ want it.  Perhaps Americans
are brought up to like that sort of thing (it sure seems so from the
television programs and news we get from the US).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux (was Re: OT : Nigerian Email Scams)

```
On 18-Nov-2002, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> > And that you are self-employed.
>
> And I also work for clients who have tried XP and will be dumping it.

If my employer chooses XP, I will use XP on their machines.  My objection to it
doesn't further than making a recommendation when I go outside of my home.

> > Objection to its registration policy is a political choice.
>
> Is 'non-objection' also a 'political choice' ?  Did you not object
> because it is good for the US economy ?  Did you not object because
> that is 'one in the eye' of the DoJ ?

Certainly "non-objection" can be a political choice.

> Or is it only 'objection' that is a political choice ?
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> needed to allow games to run compromises the system).

You were the person who first mentioned that XP was not an upgrade over W98.   I
think that almost anything is technically superior to W98, including XP.


> For the vast majority of Windows users 'technical superiority' is just
> more marketing goobledegook.

Unless they experience the blue screen of death with W98 and their friends do
not with WXP.   For the mast majority of Windows users 'technical superiority'
means that they can get their work and playing done.


> If 'technical superiority' was a decision factor then Windows wouldn't
> get on the list. For example: FAT, FAT32, NTFS ?  only MS could
> consider these to be adequate as file systems, technically they are
> rather poor.

The vast majority of users don't care about that.  They just want to plug in
their programs of choice and have them work.   Also they want to be able to plug
in their USB device and have it work.    If they can do that with XP and can't
do it with W95 or WNT, then to them, WXP is technically superior.

One of the characteristics you condemn with XP though should not be turned off -
all Windows versions have so many serious flaws that it can be dangerous to not
keep caught up with fixes.  The average user won't do this on his own,
automating the procedure is recommended.   This is a damning indictment of
MicroSoft - but I am only discussing the comparison you made - that between W98
and WXP.
```

```
> Exactly my point.  The vast majority don't care about
superiority or
> inferiority, as long as it is 'good enough'.

I wouldn't try to convince you to go to XP -- I can respect
your reasons for not doing so.  As an adjunct, though, everyone
I've spoken with, that has moved from Win32 (95/98/ME) to WinNT
(4.0/2000/XP) has been very happy with the step "up."  Part of
this would have to be the associated boost in hardware, but
there is a genuine appreciation of the operating system
improvements as well.  These users range the spectrum of
technical savvy -- from my code buddies down to my mother.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT : Nigerian Email Scams

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-05T15:01:39
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aq8mkm$vjn$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3D87A5EC.E1CC274E@shaw.ca> <3D87CBBE.6C919885@sprynet.com> <3DC2070F.F5A8B5B0@sbcglobal.net>`

```
Just got one in the past few minutes:

Text follows.............................................

FROM: MR Edward Adeleye

FAX:234.1759.6254.

ATTN: dear sir

REQUEST FOR URGENT BUSINESS RELATIONSHIP


Firstly, I must solicit your strictest confidentiality
in this transaction. This is by virtue of its nature
as being utterly CONFIDENTIAL and "TOP SECRET." Though
I know that a transaction of this magnitude will make
anyone apprehensive and worried, but I am assuring you
that all will be well at the end of the day.

We have decided to contact you first by your email due
to the urgency of this transaction, as we have been
reliably informed that it will take at least two to
three weeks for a normal post to reach you. So we
decided it is best using the email. Let me start by
first introducing myself properly to you.I am MR EDWARD ADELEYE
a director general in NATIONAL ELECTRIC POWER AUTHORITY
and I head a Three-man tender board
appointed by the Government of the Federal Republic of
Nigeria to award contracts to individuals and
companies and to approve such contract payments in the
National Electric Power Authority . The duties of the
committee also include evaluation, vetting and
monitoring of work done by foreign and local
contractors in the ministry.

I came to know of you in my search for a reliable and
reputable person to handle a very confidential
business transaction which involves the transfer of a
huge sum of money to a foreign account requiring
maximum confidence. In order to commence this
business, we solicit for your assistance to enable us
transfer into your account the said funds.

The source of this funds is as follows: Between 1999
and 2000, this committee awarded contracts to various
contractors for engineering, procurement, supply of
electrical equipments such as Transformers, and some
rural electrification projects.

However, these contracts were over-invoiced by the
committee, thereby, leaving the sum of US$14.3million
dollars (Fourteen Million Three Hundred thousand
United States Dollars) in excess. This was done with
the intention that the committee will share the excess
when the payments are approved.

The Federal Government have since approved the total
contract sum which has been paid out to the companies
and contractors concerned which executed the
contracts, leaving the balance of $14.3m dollars,
which we need your assistance to transfer into a safe
off-shore and reliable account to be disbursed amongst
ourselves.

We need your assistance as the funds are presently
secured in an escrow account of the Federal Government
specifically set aside for the settlement of
outstanding payments to foreign contractors. We are
handicapped in the circumstances as the civil service
code of conduct, does not allow us to operate
off-shore accounts, hence your importance in the whole
transaction.

My colleagues and I have agreed that if you or your
company can act as the beneficiary of this funds on
our behalf, you or your company will retain 20% of the
total amount of US$14,300,000.00 (Fourteen Million
Three Hundred Thousand United States Dollars), while
70% will be for us (members of this panel) and the
remaining 10% will be used in offsetting all
debts/expenses incurred (both local and foreign) in
the cause of this transfer. Needless to say, the trust
reposed on you at this juncture is enormous. In return
we demand your complete honesty and trust.

It does not matter whether or not your company does
contract projects of this nature described here, the
assumption is that your company won the major contract
and sub-contracted it out to other companies.  More
often than not, big trading companies or firms of
unrelated fields win major contracts and subcontract
to more specialized firms for execution of such
contracts. We are civil servants and we will not
want to miss this once in a life time opportunity.

You must however, NOTE that this transaction will be
strictly based on the following terms and conditions
as we have stated below, as we have heard confirmed
cases of business associates running away with funds
kept in their custody when it finally arrive their
accounts. We have decided that this transaction will
be based completely on the following:

(a). Our conviction of your transparent honesty and
diligence.

(b). That you would treat this transaction with utmost
secrecy and confidentiality.

(c). That upon receipt of the funds, you will promptly
release our share (70%) on demand after you have
removed your 20% and all expenses have been settled.

(d). You must be ready to produce us with enough
information about yourself to put our minds at rest.

Please, note that this transaction is 100% legal and
risk free and we hope to conclude the business in Ten
Bank working days from the date of receipt of the
necessary information and requirement from you.

Endeavour to acknowledge the receipt of this letter
using my above number . I will bring you into the
complete picture of the transaction when I have heard
from you.

Your urgent response will be highly appreciated as we
are already behind schedule for this financial
quarter.

Thank you and God bless.

Yours faithfully,

MR EDWARD ADELEYE
```

```
Robert Jones wrote:
> 
> I got the following a few days ago, I didn't answer till a day after

It was most likely a spam message trying to solicit responses to confirm
that your EMail address was active.  Other ways of doing this are to
have a 'remove me from this list' option that does exactly the opposite.

By responding you have ensured that your EMail address will be burned
into a CD that will be sold to thousands of new spammers as 'confirmed
active'.

My horoscope reading for you this day is:

  Expect many new friends to write to you in the very near future.
```

<a id="t-3d30d8ea86"></a>
## Thread: Call or Link — 4 this month / 7 total

_Thread started [2002-10](2002-10.md#t-3d30d8ea86)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Call or Link

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T22:09:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DC2FB6C.7070209@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<zfednd5ZN-vIEFygXTWcoA@comcast.com> <1st3su0vvb413bhqdlv59i4bp1c3epekk4@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> On Thu, 31 Oct 2002 15:09:24 -0500 "Jim Eanes" <jimeanes@hotmail.com> wrote:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> If you can issue a CICS LINK you should not issue a dynamic call.

I would disagree on a blanket statement basis.

I don't know what the original poster's performance criteria are, i.e., 
why he's concerned with "more efficient"; but the most heavily used 
program in my former application was the file handler, to which people 
linked more than 30 million times/day (roughly 18 hours online). We 
never saw any performance hits.

OTOH, IBM has published a white paper in which they state that the 
dynamic CALL is slightly more efficient. I'm a big fan of dynamic CALLs, 
and all new code, i.e., after 1992, uses them. It's a simpler and more 
natural way for one COBOL program to CALL another, plus it supports 
multiple parameters in the USING phrase, so you're not limited to 
DFHCOMMAREA for passing data. Starting with COBOL II, there's no longer 
any restriction on issuing CICS commands in a CALLed program, providing 
the first 2 parameters are the EIB and DFHCOMMAREA.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Call or Link

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-01T17:04:31-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<apv1a1$3dj$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<zfednd5ZN-vIEFygXTWcoA@comcast.com> <1st3su0vvb413bhqdlv59i4bp1c3epekk4@4ax.com> <3DC2FB6C.7070209@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
To follow up on Liam's post, the following information (among other
performance hints) is from the IBM performance paper at:

 http://www-1.ibm.com/support/docview.wss?uid=swg27001475&aid=1


"CICS
...

Enterprise COBOL, COBOL for OS/390 & VM, COBOL for MVS & VM, and COBOL/370
support static| and dynamic calls to Enterprise COBOL, COBOL for OS/390 &
VM, COBOL for MVS and VM, COBOL/370, and VS COBOL II subprograms containing
CICS commands or dependencies. Static calls are done with the CALL literal
statement and dynamic calls are done with the CALL identifier statement.
Converting EXEC CICS LINKs to COBOL CALLs can improve transaction response
time and reduce virtual  storage usage. Neither Enterprise COBOL, COBOL for
OS/390 & VM, COBOL for MVS & VM, nor COBOL/370 supports calls to or from
OS/VS COBOL programs in a CICS environment. In this case, EXEC CICS LINK
must be used.

 Note: : When using EXEC CICS LINK under Language Environment, a new
run-unit (enclave) will be  created for each EXEC CICS LINK. This means that
new control blocks will be allocated and subsequently  freed for each LINKed
to program. This will result in an increase in the number of storage
requests. When  using ALL31(ON), there is at least one storage request for
each enclave. When using ALL31(OFF), there  are two to three storage
requests for each enclave. If storage management tuning has not been done,
you  may experience more storage requests per enclave. As a result of a new
enclave being created for each EXEC  CICS LINK, the CPU time performance
will also be degraded when compared to VS COBOL II. If your  application
uses many EXEC CICS LINKs, you can avoid this extra overhead by using COBOL
CALLs
 whenever possible."

    ***

I think the original note in this thread talked about using VS COBOL II.
If so, you might want to review the CICS information at:

 http://www-1.ibm.com/support/manager.wss?rt=4&org=SW&doc=7001473&aid=1

Although I think that paper pre-dates LE - and "as we all know" running VS
COBOL II programs under the VS COBOL II run-time library is now totally
unsupported - unlike running such programs under the LE run-time library
which is still supported (even if the compiler isn't).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Call or Link

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-02T21:23:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<en19su0erpqtjkk9htjjjf6rvnqd0utq26@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<zfednd5ZN-vIEFygXTWcoA@comcast.com> <1st3su0vvb413bhqdlv59i4bp1c3epekk4@4ax.com> <3DC2FB6C.7070209@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
On Fri, 01 Nov 2002 22:09:29 GMT Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
wrote:

:>Binyamin Dissen wrote:

:>> On Thu, 31 Oct 2002 15:09:24 -0500 "Jim Eanes" <jimeanes@hotmail.com> wrote:
 
:>> :>I work on an IBM mainframe running Cobol II.  Which is more efficient for a
:>> :>CICS task which makes multiple calls to the same sub-program.  A dynamic
:>> :>"call" or a CICS "Link".?
 
:>> If you can issue a CICS LINK you should not issue a dynamic call.

:>I would disagree on a blanket statement basis.

:>I don't know what the original poster's performance criteria are, i.e., 
:>why he's concerned with "more efficient"; but the most heavily used 
:>program in my former application was the file handler, to which people 
:>linked more than 30 million times/day (roughly 18 hours online). We 
:>never saw any performance hits.

:>OTOH, IBM has published a white paper in which they state that the 
:>dynamic CALL is slightly more efficient. I'm a big fan of dynamic CALLs, 
:>and all new code, i.e., after 1992, uses them. It's a simpler and more 
:>natural way for one COBOL program to CALL another, plus it supports 
:>multiple parameters in the USING phrase, so you're not limited to 
:>DFHCOMMAREA for passing data. Starting with COBOL II, there's no longer 
:>any restriction on issuing CICS commands in a CALLed program, providing 
:>the first 2 parameters are the EIB and DFHCOMMAREA.

Does that mean that CICS now "handles" the dynamic calls?
```
