<a id="t-2531475e97"></a>
## Thread: Code Help — 3 msgs

### Code Help

- **From:** "Dan J" <dali@cogeco.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-19T23:52:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LrEC9.17122$L47.2794151@read2.cgocable.net>`

```
I tried writing the code for the task below can somebody proof it, please!

Thanks,
dan j

Question:
WRITE A PROGRAM TO PROCESS TWO
FILES. ONE OF THE FILES IS A TRANSACTION FILE AND IT WILL BE USED TO
UPDATE THE SECOND FILE WHICH IS A MASTER FILE. THE UPDATED MASTER FILE
RECORDS WILL BE WRITTEN AS A REPORT INSTEAD OF A NEW MASTER FILE AND
THE ERRORS WILL ALSO BE WRITTEN AS A SEPARATE REPORT. THE REPORT
LAYOUTS ARE LEFT UP TO YOU BUT SHOULD HAVE PROPER SIZING AND LOOK
PROFESSIONAL. MAIN LOGIC OF THIS PROCESS WILL BE DISCUSSED DURING ONE
OF THE LABS AND IS DISCUSSED IN CHAPTER 11 OF TEXT.

THE TWO INPUT FILES ARE:

ITEM MASTER FILE (CM2310S)
     ITEM NO              1  -  3
     ITEM DESCRIPTION     4  - 19
     UNIT PRICE          22  - 26  (2 DECIMALS)
     UNIT COST           27  - 31  (2 DECIMALS)
     ON HAND QUANTITY    32  - 36  (0 DECIMALS)
     STATUS              46  - 46  (A - ACTIVE, D - DELETED)
======================================================================
SALES ORDER DETAIL (CM2300S)
     QUANTITY SOLD       26  - 29
     ITEM DESCRIPTION    30  - 45
     ITEM NUMBER         48  - 50
     TRANSACTION CODE    67
======================================================================

THE TWO FILES MUST BE MATCHED ON ITEM NO AND THE MASTER RECORDS
UPDATED ACCORDING TO THE FOLLOWING SPECIFICATIONS:

  1) IF THE MASTER IS ACTIVE AND THERE IS NO MATCHING TRANSACTION,
     THEN WRITE THE MASTER RECORD TO THE REPORT

  2) IF THE MASTER IS ACTIVE AND THERE IS A MATCHING TRANSACTION
     RECORD, THEN UPDATE THE MASTER AS FOLLOWS:

        A)  IF THE TRANS CODE IS "S", SUBTRACT QTY SOLD FROM THE ON
            HAND QUANTITY
        B)  IF THE TRANS CODE IS "C", ADD QTY SOLD TO THE ON HAND
            QUANTITY

  3) WHEN ALL TRANS RECORDS HAVE BEEN PROCESSED AGAINST A MASTER
     RECORD, PERFORM THE FOLLOWING CALCULATIONS AND INCLUDE ON DETAIL

        EXTENDED PRICE   = ON HAND QUANTITY TIMES UNIT PRICE
        EXTENDED COST    = ON HAND QUANTITY TIMES UNIT COST
        PROFIT/LOSS      = EXTENDED PRICE - EXTENDED COST

  4) ALL OTHER NORMAL ERROR CONDITIONS MUST BE CAUGHT AND REPORTED ON
     THE ERROR REPORT.

  5) IF THE MASTER STATUS IS "D", THEN AN ERROR MESSAGE SHOULD APPEAR
     SAYING "DELETED-MASTER", IF THE MASTER STATUS IS "D" AND HAS
     CORRESPONDING TRANSACTIONS, THEN THESE TRANSACTIONS ARE IN ERROR
     AND SHOULD BE REPORTED ON THE ERROR LISTING.


Answer:
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. ASSIGN3.
      **********************************************************
      *
*
      *    *
      *DATE WRITTEN NOV 2002                                   *
      **
      *             *
      **********************************************************
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

       FILE-CONTROL.
  SELECT SALES-ORDER-DETAIL ASSIGN TO DISK 'C:\COBOL\CM2300S.DAT'
            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
            SELECT ITEM-MASTER-FILE ASSIGN TO DISK 'C:\COBOL\CM2310S.DAT'
            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
  SELECT OUTPUT-FILE ASSIGN TO DISK 'C:\COBOL\REPORT1.RPT'
            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.


       DATA DIVISION.

       FILE SECTION.

       FD   ITEM-MASTER-FILE
            RECORD CONTAINS 45 CHARACTERS
            DATA RECORD IS IN-SALS-REC.

       01   MR-MASTER-RECORD.
            05 MR-REC-TYPE  PIC X.
   88 SR-TRANSCODE  VALUE "S".
   88 SR-TRANSCODE  VALUE "C".

   10 ITM-NUM              PIC X(2).
   10 ITM-DES   PIC X(15).
   10 UNT-PRC   PIC 9(2)V99.
   10 UNT-CST   PIC 9(1)V99.
   10 HND-QNT   PIC X(4).

  FD  SALES-ORDER-DETAIL
           RECORD CONTAINS 84 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS PRINT-REC.

 01 SALES-RECORD
  10 QTY-SOL   PIC X(3).
  10 ITM-DES   PIC X(15).
  10 ITM-NUM   PIC X(2).

 FD  OUTPUT-FILE
  RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS



       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

 01-WORK-AREAS.
  05  WS-ACTION-CODE      PIC (X).
  05 WS-SALES-ORDER-DETAIL PIC X(100).

 01 PROGRAM-COUNTERS       COMP.
  05 WS-OLD-SALES-COPIED-CTR  PIC S999.
  05 WS-OLD-RECORD-DELETED-CTR  PIC S999.
  05 WS-NEW-RECORD-CTR  PIC S999.
  05 WS-OLD-RECORD-CHANGED-CTR  PIC S999.
  05 WS-NEW-MASTER-FILE-CTR PIC S999.

      01  REPT-OUTPUT.
           05 REPT-EXT-PRICE-TOTL       PIC S9(6)V99.
           05 REPT-EXT-COST-TOTL        PIC S9(6)V99.
           05 REPT-PRF-LOS-TOTL         PIC S9(6)V99.

       01  DETL-LINE.
           05 FILLER                    PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.
           05 DETL-INT-NUM              PIC XX.
           05 FILLER                    PIC X(9) VALUE SPACES.
           05 DETL-INT-DES             PIC X(15).
           05 FILLER                    PIC X(4) VALUE SPACES.
           05 DETL-UNT-PRC              PIC X(4).
           05 FILLER                    PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
           05 DETL-UNT-CST              PIC $ZZ,ZZ9.99-.
           05 FILLER                    PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.
           05 DETL-HND-QNT              PIC $$$,$$9.99-.
           05 FILLER                    PIC XX VALUE SPACES.

       01  TOTL-LINE.
           05 FILLER                    PIC X(45) VALUE SPACES.
           05 EXT-PRICE-TOTL       PIC $ZZ,ZZ9.99-.
           05 FILLER                    PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.
           05 EXT-COST-TOTL        PIC $ZZ,ZZ9.99-.
           05 FILLER                    PIC X(1) VALUE SPACES.
           05 PRF-LOS-TOTL PIC      $ZZ,ZZ9.99-.

01 HEADINGS-1.
05 PIC X(32)      VALUE SPACES
05 PIC X(7)       VALUE "ITEM NO"
05 PIC X(15)      VALUE "ITEM DESCRIPTION"
05 PIC X(10)      VALUE "UNIT PRICE"
05 PIC X(12)      VALUE "UNIT COST"
05 PIC X(15)      VALIE "ON HAND QUANTITY"
05 PIC X(32)      VALUE SPACES

01 HEADING-2.
05 PIC X(100)      VALUE SPACES
05 PIC X(12)      VALUE "QUANTITY SOLD"
05 PIC X(15)      VALUE "ITEM DESCTIPTION"
05 PIC X(10)      VALUE "ITEM NUMBER"

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.


 100-PROCUDE ITEM-MASTER-FILE.
  PERFORM 200-START-UP
  PERFORM 210-PROCESS-UPDATE
   UNTIL NO-MORE-RECORDS AND NO MORE-DETAIL-RECORDS.
  PERFORM 220-FINISH-UP.
  STOP RUN.

 200-START-UP.
  OPEN INPUT SALES-ORDER-DETAIL
   OLD-MASTER-FILE
     OUTPUT OUTPUT-FILE
  PERFORM 300-INITALIZE-ITEMS.
  PERFORM 310-WRITE-REPORT-HEADERS.
  PERFORM 400-READ-SALES-ORDER-DETAIL.
  PERFORM 410-READ-DETAIL-RECORD.

 210-PROCESS-UPDATE.
  MOVE SPACES TO ADUDIT-LINE
  IF OLD-MR-NAME = DETAIL;-NAME
   THEN
    IF UPDATE-RECORD
     PERFORM 320-UPDATE-OLD-MASTER-RECORD
    ELSE
   PERFORM 350-DELETE-OLD-MASTER-RECORD
 ENDIF

ELSE
    IF OLD-MR-NAME ( DETAIL-NAME
     PERFORM 330-COPY-OLD-MASTER-RECORD
    ELSE
     PERFORM 340-ADD-NEW-RECORD
   END-IF

  ENDIF
  PERFORM 360-PREPARE-AUDIT-LINE.

 220-FINISH-UP.
  PERFORM 370-PREPARE-TOTALS.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM END-LINE.
  AFTER ADVANCING 5 LINES.
  CLOSE DETAIL-LINE
  SALES-ORDER-DETAIL
  ITEM-MASTER-FILE
  OUTPUT-FILE.

 300-INITIALIZE-ITEMS.
  ACCEPT WS-DATE FROM DATE.
  MOVE WS-DATE TO O-DATE.
  INITIALIZE PROGRAM-COUNTERS.

 310-WRITE-REPORT-HEADERS.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM MASTER-FILE
   AFTER ADVANCING PAGE.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM REPORT-NAME-1
   AFTER ADVACNING 3 LINES.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM REPORT-NAME-2
   AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FORM HEADINGS1
   AFTER ADVANDCING 2 LINES.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM HEADINGS2.
   AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
  MOVE 2 TO WS-PROPER-SPACING.

 320-UPDATE-OLD-MASTER-RECORD.
  MOVE "S" TO WS-ACTION-CODE.
  MOVE OLD-ITM-NO TO NEW-ITM-NO.
  MOVE DETAIL STATUS TO NEW-DETAIL-STATUS.
  MOVE OLD-ITM-DES TO NEW-ITM-DES.
  MOVE OLD UNT-PRC TO NEW-UNT-PRC.
  MOVE OLD ON-HND-QTY TO NEW-HND-QTY.
  ADD 1 TO WS-SALES-ORDER-DETAIL-CTR
     ITEM-MASTER-FILE.
  PERFORM 400-READ-SALES-ORDER-DETAIL.
  PERFORM 410-READ-DETAIL-RECORD.

 330-COPY-SALES-ORDER-DETAIL.
  MOVE "C" TO WS-ACTION-CODE.
  MOVE OLD-MR-RECORD TO NEW-MR-RECORD.
  WRITE NEW-MR-RECORDS.
  ADD 1 TO WS-OLD-RECORD=-COPIED-CTR.
  PERFORM 400-READ-OLD-MASTER-RECORD.

 340-ADD-NEW-RECORD.
  MOVE "A" TO WS-ACTION-CODE.
  MOVE DETAIL-ITM-NO TO NEW-ITM-NO.
  MOVE DETAIL STATUS TO NEW-DETAIL-STATUS.
  MOVE DETAIL-ITM-DES TO NEW-ITM-DES.
  MOVE DETAIL-UNT-PRC TO NEW-UNT-PRC.
  MOVE DETAIL-ON-HND-QTY TO NEW-HND-QTY.
  WRITE NEW-MASTER-RECORD
  ADD 1 TO WS-SALES-ORDER-DETAIL-CTR
     ITEM-MASTER-FILE.
  PERFORM 410-READ-DETAIL-RECORD.

 350-DELETE-STATUS-ITEM-MASTER-FILE
  MOVE "D" TO WS-ACTION-CODE.
  MOVE OLD-ITEM-MASTER-FILE TO WS-OL=MR-RECORD.
  ADD 1 TO WS-OLD-RECORD-DELETED-CTR.
  PERFORM 400-READ-OLD-MASTER-RECORD.
  PERFORM 410-READ-DETAIL-RECORD.

 370-PREPARE-TOTALS.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM SUMMARY-HEADER
   AFTER ADVANCING 5 LINES.
  MOVE WS-OLD-RECORD-COPIED-CTR TO O-OLD-RECORD-COPIED-CTR.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM SUMMARY-LINE-1.
   AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
  MOVE WS-OLD-RECORD-DELETED-CTR TO O-OLD-RECORD-DELETED-CTR.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM SUMMARY-LINE-2.
   AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
  MOVE WS-NEW-RECORD-CTR TO O-NEW-RECORD-CTR.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM SUMMARY-LINE-3.
   AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
  MOVE WS-NEW-MASTER-FILE-CTR TO O-RECORD-CHANGED-CTR.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM SUMMARY-LINE-4.
  MOVE WS-NEW-MASTER-FILE-CTR TO O-NEW-MASTER-FILE-CTR.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FROM SUMMARY-LINE-5.
   AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
  MOVE WS-AUDIT-CTR TO O-AUDIT-CTR.
  WRITE PRINTLINE FORM SUMMARY-LINE-6.
   AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
  EXD-PRICE = WS-ON-HND-QTY * UNT-PRC.
  EXD-COST = WS-ON-HND-QTY * PRC.
  PRF-LOSS = EXD-PRICE - EXT-CST

 400-READ-OLD-MASTER-RECORD.
  READ OLD-MASTER-FILE NEXT RECORD
   AT END
    MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO OLD-ITM-NO.

 410-READ-DETAIL-RECORD.
  READ DETAIL-FILE-RECORD
   AT END
    MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO DETAIL-ITM-NO.
```

## Thread: help file I/O — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help file I/O

- **From:** "hello" <sfdsa@jhk.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T00:52:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ddb22b3_1@news.eol.ca>`

```
if the input file include two types of records, how to read it?

example:
   xxx  9999
   xxx  9999
   ..
(first 10 lines)
   ..
   xxx  9999
   999  xxx xxxxxx
   999  xxx xxxxxx
   ...
( 20 lines)
   999  xxx xxxxxx
```

#### ↳ Re: help file I/O

- **From:** "Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T12:03:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<arfqee$hoq$1@reader12.wxs.nl>`
- **References:** `<3ddb22b3_1@news.eol.ca>`

```
Usually these differrent records are coded with some extra bytes indicating
the record type. If not, you should use the VLI to determine what record
type you have read from your input file.

Or check on the contents of the bytes 5-7 and 9-14 to know what record you
got. Before the READ move spaces to the entire record.....

Regards, Danny.

### Fujitsu 6.1 COPY support

- **From:** "Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T12:09:17+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<arfqou$i6d$1@reader12.wxs.nl>`

```
When building one of my projects in Fujitsu COBOL 6.1 (RM/85 mode) I get the
following error message :

d:\project\copyfile\STROUTINES.PRD 204: JMN5525I-S  IDENTIFIER SPECIFIED
JUST AFTER INVOKE MUST BE OBJECT IDENTIFIER. INVOKE STATEMENT IS IGNORED.

The error points to a line in a copyfile where the following is coded :

033960 MELD                            SECTION.
033970*
033980 ME-00.
033990*
                  INVOKE POW-SELF "DisplayMessage"
                         USING    B-SCHAP
                          "Systeemmelding".
034030*
034040 ME-900.
034050*
034060     EXIT.

The same section I use in another project (without copyfiles however) and it
works fine there.

Any clou ?

Kind regards, Danny.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu 6.1 COPY support

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T07:57:10-08:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3DDBB0D6.10907@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<arfqou$i6d$1@reader12.wxs.nl>`

```
The problem has nothing to do with "copy".  It has to do with the fact 
that the identifier specified
just after the invoke (clue ... that is the word POW-SELF) is not an 
object identifier. Maybe you
should ask the person whose code you copied.

Donald

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu 6.1 COPY support

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T17:33:14+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<argdjv$nqe$1@mailnews.kub.nl>`
- **References:** `<arfqou$i6d$1@reader12.wxs.nl>`

```
Danny,

The POW-SELF object refers to the current form you're working on, and
apparently the compiler can't find this form. Are you using this code in
PowerCOBOL, or in COBOL97? The latter cannot work with the object POW-SELF.

One thing to remember is that PowerCOBOL uses a pre-compiler in which it
renames stuff like form-names, control-names etc. The COPY statement is
compiler after the pre-compiling by PowerCOBOL. Try using INCLUDE in stead
of COPY since the INCLUDE statement IS used by the pre-compiler.

Kind regads,

Rik Hendriks

"Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com> wrote in message
news:arfqou$i6d$1@reader12.wxs.nl...

the
> following error message :
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…

it
> works fine there.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-af29778c9b"></a>
## Thread: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390? — 28 this month / 57 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T07:24:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com>`

```
I was implementing a simple routine to walk through a tree of trees
and display the data contained therein.  The routine works properly
but when it exits the last activation of the Iterate-Nodes paragraph
it goes into an endless loop.  The instead of returning to the
statement after the "Perform Iterate-Nodes" in the procedure division
it endlessly returns to the recursive "Perform Iterate-Nodes" call
inside Iterate-Nodes.

Does anyone have an idea of how to correct this problem?  For such a
simple routine I'd rather not write a separate program with the "is
recursive" option.  Can this work at all?  Thanks.

BTW This is the IBM Cobol compiler for OS/390.

Working-Storage Section.
  05  Child-Ndx               binary PIC S9(8) value 0.      
  05  Child-Stack-Area.                                    
    10                        occurs 99.                   
      15  Child-Collection    Pointer.                
      15                      Pic X(01).              
        88  Children-Not-Done           value ' '.    
        88  Children-Done               value 'Y'.
	....
Linkage Section.

01  This-Node.
  05  This-Node-Name        Pic X(255).
  05  This-Node-Children    Pointer.

Procedure Division.
 Move low-values       to Child-Stack-Area
 Move 1                to Child-Ndx
 Set  Child-Collection to Root-Collection           
 Perform Iterate-Nodes
 Goback.

/------------------------------*
 Iterate-Nodes.                                               
*------------------------------*
     Perform Get-First-Child                                      

     Perform until Children-Done (Child-Ndx)

       Display This-Node-Name

       Add 1 to Child-Ndx                                           
       Set Child-Collection (Child-Ndx) to This-Node-Children
       Set Children-Not-Done (Child-Ndx) to true

       Perform Iterate-Nodes                                  

       Subtract 1 from Child-Ndx                                    
       If Child-Ndx > 0
         Perform Get-Next-Child      
       Else
         Move 1 to Child-Ndx
       End-If
                                
     End-Perform                  
                                  
     Exit.
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T11:43:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<arghks$kdb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com>`

```
I assume that you no that the
    EXIT
statement at the end of the paragraph is "basically" a no-op.  (It is a
"marker" for an exit - not a performing of an exit).  Therefore, I *think*
that the following PROCEDURE division "revision" *might* work. (Normally I
am not a great fan of "GO TO" - but I think for this type of "escape" - and
until IBM supports the EXIT PARAGRAPH feature of the 2002 Standard - I think
this may be your best solution.)

 Procedure Division.
  Move low-values       to Child-Stack-Area
  Move 1                to Child-Ndx
  Set  Child-Collection to Root-Collection
  Perform Iterate-Nodes
Exit-Para.
  Goback.

  Iterate-Nodes.
 *------------------------------*
      Perform Get-First-Child

      Perform until Children-Done (Child-Ndx)

        Display This-Node-Name

        Add 1 to Child-Ndx
        Set Child-Collection (Child-Ndx) to This-Node-Children
        Set Children-Not-Done (Child-Ndx) to true

        Perform Iterate-Nodes

        Subtract 1 from Child-Ndx
        If Child-Ndx > 0
          Perform Get-Next-Child
        Else
          Move 1 to Child-Ndx
        End-If
      End-Perform

      Go To Exit-Para
         .
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T13:27:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com>`

```
As far a I am aware recursive performs are not currently permitted,
according to the draft new standard, the behaviour is unpredictable,
presumably because the exit, as translated by the compiler, of the
perform points to the place that it is supposed to return to, which in
your case will have been altered by the second perform of the same
paragraph.

You don't have to write a recursive subroutine, the equivalent to
recursion can be achieved by rearranging the way the procedure is
written and using PERFORM UNTIL a specific suitably devised condition.

Additionally, your code never sets children-done to true.

Hope this helps,

Robert

jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote in message news:<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com>...
> I was implementing a simple routine to walk through a tree of trees
> and display the data contained therein.  The routine works properly
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>      Exit.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T08:00:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com>`

```
robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote in message news:<fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com>...
> As far a I am aware recursive performs are not currently permitted,
> according to the draft new standard, the behaviour is unpredictable,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> written and using PERFORM UNTIL a specific suitably devised condition.

Yes, but it looks awfully ugly when compared to the clean recursive
approach.

> Additionally, your code never sets children-done to true.

Oops, that line was in the "Get-Next-Child" sequence that I compressed
for readability...

I managed to use Bill's suggestion and altered it as so:

    - The initial invokation was done using Perform X thru X-Exit (an
actual use for that twisted construct -- whoda thunk it).

    - A special check for Children=-Done (1) did a "Go To X-Exit".

Set CS-coll to root-tree
Move 1 to CS-Ndx
Set Not-Done (CS-Ndx) to true 
                                                  
Perform Iterate-CS-Nodes thru Iterate-CS-Nodes-Exit

Iterate-CS-Nodes.                                               
    Perform First-CS-Child                                      
    Perform until CS-Done (CS-Ndx)                              
      Perform Handle-CS-Child                                   
                                                                
      Add 1 to CS-Ndx                                           
      Set CS-Coll (CS-Ndx) to CS-Coll-Kids              
      Set CS-Not-Done (CS-Ndx) to true                          
      Perform Iterate-CS-Nodes
      Subtract 1 from CS-Ndx                                  
      If CS-Ndx = 0                                             
        Go To Iterate-CS-Nodes-Exit                             
      End-If                                                    
      Perform Next-CS-Child 
    End-Perform             
    Exit.                   
Iterate-CS-Nodes-Exit.      
    Exit.                   

As a side comment, I use "Exit" only to keep the SQL, DLI and CICS
tranlators from screwing up my programs.  If you use the following:


Para-Name-One.
    ..statements..
    Exec XXXX
       somecommand
    End-Exec
    .

The IBM translators consume the terminal period and read it as
"End-Exec.".  This results in code that falls through to the next
paragraph and really bothers me.  I'm very much a single '.' per
paragraph sort of programmer

The easy fix is to make it "Exit." instead of just ".", though
"Continue." would work as well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T16:29:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com>`

```

On 26-Nov-2002, jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> The IBM translators consume the terminal period and read it as
> "End-Exec.".  This results in code that falls through to the next
> paragraph and really bothers me.  I'm very much a single '.' per
> paragraph sort of programmer

I've mentioned this type of programmer to people a few times.  So far I haven't
come across anybody who understands it.  Maybe this is because I have worked
primarily in IDMS shops where we use pre-compilers and full stop periods are
very useful when we don't see the code within.  Maybe it is because we work in
shops with code that was written before END-IF was part of the language.   Maybe
it is because I have worked in shops where the standard was to end a sentence
with a period.

But maybe not.  We just don't see any advantage in having only one period per
paragraph.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T20:16:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote 

> I've mentioned this type of programmer to people a few times.  So far I haven't
> come across anybody who understands it. 

What's to 'understand' ?

> But maybe not.  We just don't see any advantage in having only one period per
> paragraph.

What is the 'advantage' of having more than one ?

Sometimes it is necessary to change code so that it is only executed
in certain circumstances.  This is easily done by bounding it with an
IF and an END-IF - except that all the bloody full stops have to be
removed.  If later the IF-END-IF is removed do you really go back and
add all the useless full-stops ?

Sometimes I move code into an in-line PERFORM - and have to remove all
the stops,  if this gets too large I move it to a new paragraph and
make it an out-of-line PERFORM - do you go and put all the stops back
in ?

Really ?
```

completely.  Or at the very least dropped as a required syntax element.
What purpose do they serve, other than to make the compiler writer's job
easier?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T14:30:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE4D8F6.6080105@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <wb4F9.63070$e%.990887@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


db2ims wrote:
> I think that 'full stops' (aka periods) should be dropped from the language
> completely.  Or at the very least dropped as a required syntax element.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Well if they were dropped completly then that would be OK, 
but it appears that they are here for the duration.  That 
being said, I have found that less mistakes happen when they 
are used all the time (except in "in line performs"). 
Although I suppose if COBOL had "END MOVE" along with all 
the other "END ..." then we wouldn't need periods.  It is 
usually with the MOVE's that problems with periods arise, 
and usually caused by someone who is modifying the program 
written by someone else.

Eliminating their need might make mistakes in code less 
likely to happen, but so would more disciplined and careful 
programmers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T15:08:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<as2n6a$58l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <wb4F9.63070$e%.990887@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

On 27-Nov-2002, "db2ims" <elitistN@netscape.net> wrote:

> I think that 'full stops' (aka periods) should be dropped from the language
> completely.  Or at the very least dropped as a required syntax element.
> What purpose do they serve, other than to make the compiler writer's job
> easier?

With pre-compilers they serve a nice function.

The lines without sequence numbers were created by my IDMS pre-compiler.

051100*    OBTAIN CALC IABRCAA
051200*        ON DB-REC-NOT-FOUND
                 MOVE 7 TO DML-SEQUENCE
                 CALL 'IDMS' USING SUBSCHEMA-CTRL
                         IDBMSCOM (32)
                         SR1406
                         IDBMSCOM (43)
                 IF NOT DB-REC-NOT-FOUND PERFORM IDMS-STATUS;
                 ELSE
051300             DISPLAY 'SKIPPING ' FINDER-ID ' NOT FOUND'.

I could have coded an END-IF to the IF that is not in my source code.

But in general, I think they make maintenance easier.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "db2ims" <elitistN@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T10:44:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sL5F9.3453$Nm.368420@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <wb4F9.63070$e%.990887@news20.bellglobal.com> <as2n6a$58l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:as2n6a$58l$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 27-Nov-2002, "db2ims" <elitistN@netscape.net> wrote:
>
> > I think that 'full stops' (aka periods) should be dropped from the
language
> > completely.  Or at the very least dropped as a required syntax element.
> > What purpose do they serve, other than to make the compiler writer's job
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> But in general, I think they make maintenance easier.

<topic drift>

Not being an IDMS guy, my opinion has to be 'humble', but that is odd
behaviour for a precompiler, to leave a dangling 'ELSE' like that!
Shouldn't precompiler generated code be self contained?  As in:

                  IF NOT DB-REC-NOT-FOUND PERFORM IDMS-STATUS
                  END-IF

This way the programmer doesn't *have* to code anything to satisfy the
'ELSE' clause, and there is less risk of screwing up the subsequent logic.

</topic drift>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T16:27:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<as2rot$7rp$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <wb4F9.63070$e%.990887@news20.bellglobal.com> <as2n6a$58l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <sL5F9.3453$Nm.368420@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

On 27-Nov-2002, "db2ims" <elitistN@netscape.net> wrote:

> Not being an IDMS guy, my opinion has to be 'humble', but that is odd
> behaviour for a precompiler, to leave a dangling 'ELSE' like that!
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'ELSE' clause, and there is less risk of screwing up the subsequent logic.

The pre-compiler is old, from before END-IF was available.   I imagine there
exists a lot of code that depends upon it working the way it does.

Notice that they inverted the " On DB-REC-NOT-FOUND" into "IF NOT
DB-REC-NOT-FOUND" to pull my conditions into an else clause.   I could have
dozens of commands after the else that have to be terminated by a period or an
end-if.

If I know how the code expands, I can do:
    OBTAIN CALC IABRCAA
        ON DB-REC-NOT-FOUND
           DISPLAY 'SKIPPING ' FINDER-ID ' NOT FOUND
        END-IF

But we don't work with listings anymore.  I will assume that the person doing
maintenance is not as sure as I am what gets inserted by the pre-compiler.  So I
don't nest IDMS commands, and I finish the commands with a full stop period.

That way whomever comes after me will be very sure about what happens.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3de56beb_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <wb4F9.63070$e%.990887@news20.bellglobal.com> <as2n6a$58l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:as2n6a$58l$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 27-Nov-2002, "db2ims" <elitistN@netscape.net> wrote:
>
> > I think that 'full stops' (aka periods) should be dropped from the
language
> > completely.  Or at the very least dropped as a required syntax element.
> > What purpose do they serve, other than to make the compiler writer's job
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> But in general, I think they make maintenance easier.

Interesting...it bears out the point that Richard has made here many
times...what is familiar to us is pleasing to us.

See, I reckon that this...

> 051100*    OBTAIN CALC IABRCAA
> 051200*        ON DB-REC-NOT-FOUND
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 051300             DISPLAY 'SKIPPING ' FINDER-ID ' NOT FOUND'
   051400     END-IF

...looks more elegant and is less likely to be missed , than your full stop.
You can also "cut and paste" this code more safely, because the scope of the
IF is clearly delimited.

But I also believe it doesn't matter...if I had to maintain it, I'd have no
problem either way...<G>

Pete.

>With pre-compilers they serve a nice function.

Do they?  

You coded in the original source

OBTAIN CALC IABRCAA
    ON DB-REC-NOT-FOUND
       DISPLAY 'Skipping' FINDER-ID 'NOT FOUND' .

Which works.


But what if:

IF Weekday = 'Friday' AND Day-of-month = 13 THEN
   DISPLAY 'Today is Friday the 13th, let''s try an OBTAIN'
   OBTAIN blah1 blah2
     ON DB-REC-NOT-FOUND
        DISPLAY 'Ooops - I knew it, Friday 13 syndrom'
  END-OBTAIN   (Which would be good here, wouldn't it)
  DISPLAY 'End of Friday 13th processing'
END-IF


If you _need_ to use dots/periods, there is a flaw in your
precompiler, methinks.....

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <wb4F9.63070$e%.990887@news20.bellglobal.com> <3DE5C8E7.C18D9CA7@Azonic.co.nz> <41qcuu4qteno54rd9jku3o6pui6p0sd0na@4ax.com>`

Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote

> >There are places where the full stop is required in order to resolve
> >ambiguity.
> 
> 
> An example, please....

A the end of a pargraph:

             MOVE A   TO B
             C

The compiler cannot tell whether C is variable to move A to or a paragraph label.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T15:05:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com>`

```

On 26-Nov-2002, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> > I've mentioned this type of programmer to people a few times.  So far I
> > haven't
> > come across anybody who understands it.
>
> What's to 'understand' ?

Why.

> > But maybe not.  We just don't see any advantage in having only one period
> > per
> > paragraph.
>
> What is the 'advantage' of having more than one ?

Full stop periods are sure (especially when you use a pre-compiler).   Periods
are English.

> Sometimes it is necessary to change code so that it is only executed
> in certain circumstances.  This is easily done by bounding it with an
> IF and an END-IF - except that all the bloody full stops have to be
> removed.  If later the IF-END-IF is removed do you really go back and
> add all the useless full-stops ?

It is easy to edit text, including indenting it and making it easy for the next
person to read it clearly.

So you leave out periods to save a couple of seconds of editing time.   I add
editing time to make sure that the code is easy to read.

> Sometimes I move code into an in-line PERFORM - and have to remove all
> the stops,  if this gets too large I move it to a new paragraph and
> make it an out-of-line PERFORM - do you go and put all the stops back
> in ?

Yes, it only takes a couple of seconds.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T12:31:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211271231.6d66ba87@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote

> > What is the 'advantage' of having more than one ?

> Periods are English.

When I program I write in Cobol.

> So you leave out periods to save a couple of seconds of editing time.   I add
> editing time to make sure that the code is easy to read.

Being 'easy to read' is a purely subjective view based on what you are
used to.  I find excessive useless full stops to be irritating since I
weaned myself off them a couple of decades ago.

While adding or removing full stops may only be 'a few seconds', the
effects from missing just one may be somewhat longer.
 
> Yes, it only takes a couple of seconds.

What I do find amazing is that full stop proponents will insist on
having a stop on the end of each 'sentence' and will claim that it is
difficult to read without them, but is quite content to have that same
block without them when indented (even going as far as removing and
adding them).

I suspect that there is also quite a difference in overall design
approaches too.  Do you tend to use sections with an exit- paragraph ?
 Rather than indent code in a IF .. END-IF (and thus remove stops) do
you IF NOT .. GO TO exit and leave code unindented ?

Programming styles tend to a complete package. At one extreme (IMHO)
is the 'heavyweight' performed section/goto exit style where sections
are numbered in a hierarchical style and code order is strictly
controlled by number.

At the other extreme is the 'lightweight' paragraphs-only, no stops,
as-you-find-it style (that I have developed into).

The 'heavyweight' style seems more suited to batch programs that have
a logical hierarchy, batch editors and lineflow listings.  In user
driven interactive programs (CICS eg is 'batch') there tends to be
less hierarchical dependancies, code may be executed as required by
the user, not as ordained by a structure chart. These interactive
programs tend to be controlled by a large event loop.

There is little useful middle ground between the heavyweight and
lightweight styles. There are several distinct differences in approach
to the styles, taking them one at a time and trying to apply them
simply does not make sense.

If you are using a 'heavyweight' style then doing 'minimum full stops'
or 'no goto' or 'paragraphs only' simply don't fit (nor make sense).

If one is using a 'lightweight' style then 'adding full-stops' or
using a goto or hierarchy numbers on paragraphs doesn't work.
```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0211271231.6d66ba87@posting.google.com...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > So you leave out periods to save a couple of seconds of editing time.
I add
> > editing time to make sure that the code is easy to read.
>
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…

Don't strongly disagree with any of the above, Richard, although it is
speculative.

I guess I must be "middleweight"... I DO write sectioned code (with a single
entry and exit point) but, in every other regard, my code would be what you
defined as "lightweight". (Like you, I dropped full stops 20 years ago;
however, I retained sections for a number of reasons which we will simply
have to disagree on.)

I believe that there are almost as many "styles" of writing COBOL as there
are programmers.

What is "good" or "bad" is really just subjective opinion.

A real "programmer" should be able to cope with it all...We either know the
language or we don't.

Pete.

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> So you leave out periods to save a couple of seconds of editing time.   I
add
> editing time to make sure that the code is easy to read.

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, it only takes a couple of seconds.

While I totally respect your right to code it however you like, Howard, I am
simply amazed by your position on this. Could it be that this is a hangover
from the days when COBOL HAD to be in sentences and was supposed to be as
English-like as possible?

Whether an editor that highlights them is used or not, experience has shown
that using full stops in COBOL simply introduces another opportunity for
error. One of the main reasons for the introduction of "COBOL 2" was to
encourage people NOT to use them, by providing a structured alternative in
the form of scope delimiters.

I can't believe you actually put them back after having removed them...<G>

Still, "Chacune a son gout..."

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-11-29T11:22:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On 26-Nov-2002, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> 
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, it only takes a couple of seconds.

I use full stops and scope delimiters together. I would rather put up
with a few compiler errors arising from a unintentional full stop than
the run-time consequences of a missing one when someone has
inadvertently failed to use all the appropriate scope delimiters in
what are sometimes complex compound conditional constructs.  I know
that code should be properly tested, but it isn't always and use of
the full stop limits the amount of code that can be accidentally
executed because of a missing scope delimiter.  Even when finding such
errors during testing, I think it also makes testing easier as not so
much is done wrong during overruns.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-29T16:01:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211291601.75b80f58@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com>`

```
robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote 

> I use full stops and scope delimiters together. I would rather put up
> with a few compiler errors arising from a unintentional full stop than
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> much is done wrong during overruns.

You seem to ignore the possibilty that there may be a missing scope
delimiter, say on an inner set of IFs, that won't give a compiler
error and will be masked by the full stop while still not giving the
correct results.

Certainly the use of scope delimiters will give a compiler error if
there are spurious full stops, and thus you should always use
delimiters, but 'limiting' the amount of code 'accidentally executed'
because of incorrect contructs simply makes these errors
_more_difficult_ to find.

If I left off an END-IF in the midst of a large construct I want the
whole program to blow up so that I know something is wrong and not
just have one or two minor invalid results that may, or may not, show
up in testing.
That is: if I get an 'overrun' I want _everything_ to go wrong, the
problem with 'not much' going wrong is that you may never notice it
(until 2am one dark and stormy night when the CEO wants results on
desk at 8am).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T11:33:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211261133.5024c25d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com>`

```
jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote
                                              
> Perform Iterate-CS-Nodes thru Iterate-CS-Nodes-Exit
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Exit.                   

You might want to complete the transition by changing 'PERFORM
Iterate-CS-Nodes' to a GO TO removing all pretence that this is actual
recursion.  The issue is that if this code was moved to a compiler
that actually supported recursion you may get different results - such
as overflowing the perform stack.

It will also be very confusing to the next programmer for this code
because it now relies on side effects in the code. Essentially the
'next' programmer must understand that the EXIT is being used as a GO
TO to the SUBTRACT 1 statement which is back inside the in-line
PERFORM and it relies on the END-PERFORM re-iterating this PERFORM
after it has already completed.
```

Bill,

HOW or WHETHER Fujitsu make a profit is not the issue. The issue is whether
the user must pay a licence fee.

Fujitsu users don't.

Your implication that Fujitsu are not a "COBOL oriented" company ("MF is
COBOL oriented. BIG [your emphasis...] difference" between them and Fujitsu)
is simply a matter of opinion, and is irrelevant to the issue anyway.

I'm sure Fujitsu would argue that the millions they have spent on Compiler
research and development qualifies them as a COBOL-oriented company. You
certainly can't argue that at least one division of their Company is COBOL
oriented... Their COBOL products are of the highest quality.

If we wanted to have an argument abut this <G>, I would remind people that I
started out as a staunch MF supporter...used their products (COBOL and
DIALOG) and was quite happy with them. Then we had the VISOC fiasco where
they shafted the user base, then various takeovers (are they Merant or not
Merant?) complete instabilty and consternation amongst their own staff due
to a lack of Management direction, and the introduction of a licence fee (it
wasn't always there), on the reasoning that the company couldn't stay viable
without it. Whether they are "COBOL oriented" or not, their track record has
hardly been one of stability.

(Micro Focus today are not the same company and I accept that they are doing
their best to forget the past and  simply get on with marketing Net Express.
However, it is early days yet and, while NE is an excellent product, it
still requires a run time fee.)

Fujitsu meanwhile have simply picked up the user base by offering
(initially) a free COBOL compiler and then providing regular releases that
improved the product. Whether you believe they are "COBOL oriented" or not
the fact remains that they are stable, and their COBOL products are simply
superb. They have also proved themselves responsive to the Market Place,
implementing OO COBOL (for instance) while J4 were still sunning themselves
in Florida and deciding where to have the next meeting...<G>

Your remark is unfair and unworthy (and I expect better from you <G>).

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MS Cobol 4.5 How to do?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-22T07:32:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDDDD74.E7A8BEA2@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<BIOC9.9$NS2.1157@newsb.telia.net> <argh58$i6l$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3ddc1619_5@Usenet.com> <arhare$48t$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ddc9f6d_4@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:

> are they Merant or not

No.

> and the introduction of a licence fee (it wasn't always there), 

Actually runtime licences were 'there' from the earliest days of CIS
Cobol and Level II Cobol.  These products could not create executables
that did not rely on a runtime which had to be purchased. 

It was only later, with Cobol/2 on DOS and Windows, that they added the
facilty of building a complete .EXE that incorporated the runtime
modules and thus didn't require separate licences for the stand-alone
runtime.  This also required that the program only used facilities from
the basic compiler package, if any 'Toolkit' or 'OSX' components were
used, or the program was built as .INT or .GNT, then a run-time licence
was required.

So MF runtime licences _were_ always there, but were not required under
specific circumstances for some products.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MS Cobol 4.5 How to do?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T17:25:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211201725.32001954@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<BIOC9.9$NS2.1157@newsb.telia.net> <argh58$i6l$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
"Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote

> MS COBOL is actually from Micro Focus and is no longer supported. 

MS Cobol 4.5 was a rebadged and repackaged version of MF Cobol 2.5.

> And no - you can't destribute license free code.

Wrong.  The MicroSoft licence for their product allowed the royalty
free distribution of compiled programs incorporating any and all
run-time facilities provided by the package.  See actual licence and
manuals for details.

I quote: 

"Additional grant of license (language software) - ... you have the
royalty-free right to reproduce and distribute executable files
created using the software. ... royalty-free right to reproduce and
distribute the runtime modules of the software provided blah, blah."

In fact MF Cobol version 2.5 and later including 3.4 and 4.0 also
allowed free distribution of compiled programs as long as certain
facilities were not included.


> Compiler vendors would go out of business if
> you did.

Wrong. Fujitsu allows free run-time distribution (with some
exceptions)*, though it could be argued that their compiler
development may be subsidised by other interests.
Other compiler vendors, mostly for other languages, don't go out of
business when allowing free runtime distribution.

*NOTE the free Fujitsu version 3 download prohibits distribution
entirely, it is necessary to buy a compiler to get a licence to
distribute runtimes for free. MF student versions are much the same as
this.
```

#### ↳ Re: MS Cobol 4.5 How to do?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-21T07:56:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDC91BB.57F074A5@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<BIOC9.9$NS2.1157@newsb.telia.net>`

```
Fim Wï¿½stberg wrote:
> 
> I am trying to use MS Cobol 4.5, but I have som problems.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cobol run time library not installed

As a guess you are using Windows NT/2000/XP.  Try using 'FORCEDOS cobol
...', Windows is getting confused.  

Basically the Cobol compiler can run in DOS or OS/2 version 1 and thinks
that in a protected mode environment it should be OS/2.

> Is this Copier free, or do I have to pay som $$ to Bill G?

MS Cobol 4.5 originally sold for many hundreds of dollars a decade ago. 
It allows free distribution of the compiled application (no run-time
fees at all) to licenced users.
```

…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

Wah?  No.  The transaction would be assigned to no terminal at all. 
EIBTRMID would be set to LOW-VALUES.  Doing a SEND would get you a
NOTALLOC abend.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What happens if START is used without a TERminal id

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-23T12:15:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E9F2C77B45804F93.65064FAC75BFC864.4EE933342608497A@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<2068459.1037810641@dbforums.com> <6B62AB1FCE6886FF.0EAC9D9938643FE2.153A998A5FA1FEC3@lp.airnews.net> <3DDEF464.5D9FEEA5@sprynet.com>`

```
On Fri, 22 Nov 2002 20:22:12 -0700, Frank Swarbrick
<infocat@sprynet.com> enlightened us:

>SkippyPB wrote:
>> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>NOTALLOC abend.

Are you sure?  Most CICS transactions have task storage assigned to
them and that storage is usually assigned to some terminal.  Even at
startup, the storage used to open tables and such is assigned
internally to the console as I recall.  Unless the transaction is
communicating with DCT entries or doing some internal work, there is a
terminal assigned to it somewhere in the chain of things (maybe the
TCT entry).  While it is possible the EIBTRMID is non existent, the
task is still logically attached to the terminal that was running the
task that issued the start.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Real Signs:

Hotel in Zurich:  

BECAUSE OF THE IMPROPRIETY OF ENTERTAINING
GUESTS OF THE OPPOSITE SEX IN THE BEDROOM, 
IT IS SUGGESTED THAT THE LOBBY BE USED FOR THIS PURPOSE
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What happens if START is used without a TERminal id

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-23T17:23:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDFBB7F.4030900@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<2068459.1037810641@dbforums.com> <6B62AB1FCE6886FF.0EAC9D9938643FE2.153A998A5FA1FEC3@lp.airnews.net> <3DDEF464.5D9FEEA5@sprynet.com> <E9F2C77B45804F93.65064FAC75BFC864.4EE933342608497A@lp.airnews.net>`

```

>>
>>Wah?  No.  The transaction would be assigned to no terminal at all. 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> task that issued the start.



Usually the first program that is started with a transaction 
will have the termid of the terminal where the user typed in 
the transaction, and it would be stored in EIBTRMID.  It is 
then passed to any subsequent programs called or transferred 
to by the main program.  If (as I discovered) the "CICS" 
program is actually started by a batch program (yes it is 
strange, but I've seen it done) then the EIBTRMID would be 
assigned from a working storage field an explicit literal move.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> On Fri, 22 Nov 2002 20:22:12 -0700, Frank Swarbrick
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> task that issued the start.

Why do you think storage has been assigned to some terminal?  I've been
doing TCP/IP programming where I have a long running task which I
currently start up using CECI (it's not in production yet -- it will be
started automatically when CICS comes up).  I do "CECI START
TRAN(IP01)".  The task then runs the entire time CICS is up.  I am free
to run anything else I want on my terminal, the terminal that started
the transaction.  I don't see why you would think it was attached to my
terminal, nor any reason it should be.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What happens if START is used without a TERminal id

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T09:29:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE09BE9.FDA1B033@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<2068459.1037810641@dbforums.com> <6B62AB1FCE6886FF.0EAC9D9938643FE2.153A998A5FA1FEC3@lp.airnews.net> <3DDEF464.5D9FEEA5@sprynet.com> <E9F2C77B45804F93.65064FAC75BFC864.4EE933342608497A@lp.airnews.net> <3DE07962.5B2064C@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> SkippyPB wrote:
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> "I'm very seldom naughty" --Willow Rosenberg, 'Buffy the Vampire Slayer'

In the CICS regions I program for, there are dummy terminals defined
for specific tasks.  This allows us to make certain long-running tasks
single-threaded, such as an asynchronous file-writer, for example.  In
older releases of CICS, the dummy terminals seemed to require special
DD statements in the startup deck for readers and punches.

Starting transaction which already has a terminal (including a dummy
terminal) will queue up a message to that task.  Most of the started
tasks I work with have no user interface screen.  We have some tasks
that used to have VTAM/SNA network interfaces, but have been heavily
modified to use TCP/IP.  They still have dummy terminals.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What happens if START is used without a TERminal id

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T18:05:51+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<241120021805514194%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<2068459.1037810641@dbforums.com> <6B62AB1FCE6886FF.0EAC9D9938643FE2.153A998A5FA1FEC3@lp.airnews.net> <3DDEF464.5D9FEEA5@sprynet.com> <E9F2C77B45804F93.65064FAC75BFC864.4EE933342608497A@lp.airnews.net> <3DE07962.5B2064C@sprynet.com> <3DE09BE9.FDA1B033@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <3DE09BE9.FDA1B033@worldnet.att.net>, Arnold Trembley
<arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> 
> In the CICS regions I program for, there are dummy terminals defined
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> modified to use TCP/IP.  They still have dummy terminals.

You can achieve the same function by queuing a message to a DCT that is
"Logically Recoverable", has a trigger level and a tranid. This will
ensure that only one transaction is run against the queue at a time.

There are many tricks to getting CICS to serialize on some functions.

By the way, CICS will NOT assign a TERMID to any transaction started
with EXEC CICS START without a terminal.

As an aside, it is not good design to have permanent CICS transactions.
CICS is not designed to support permanently running transactions.
If there is any way to do it. I would try to design the trigger event
for running the work that a permanent transaction is waiting for via
some other CICS mechanism. START TRAN, WRITEQ TD, Terminal Input, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What happens if START is used without a TERminal id

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T04:46:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE1AAFA.333F85D4@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<2068459.1037810641@dbforums.com> <6B62AB1FCE6886FF.0EAC9D9938643FE2.153A998A5FA1FEC3@lp.airnews.net> <3DDEF464.5D9FEEA5@sprynet.com> <E9F2C77B45804F93.65064FAC75BFC864.4EE933342608497A@lp.airnews.net> <3DE07962.5B2064C@sprynet.com> <3DE09BE9.FDA1B033@worldnet.att.net> <241120021805514194%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```
Joseph Katnic wrote:
> 
> In article <3DE09BE9.FDA1B033@worldnet.att.net>, Arnold Trembley
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> ensure that only one transaction is run against the queue at a time.

We tried the DCT and trigger level trick with the destination in
another CICS region on another CEC.  It worked pretty well, but didn't
provide the throughput in transactions per second that we were looking
for.  But it's certainly nice to have multiple tools in the toolbox.

> 
> There are many tricks to getting CICS to serialize on some functions.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Joe.

Thanks for the advice.  Our long-running tasks exist only to service
the network interface.  They use HANDLE ABEND to perform a clean-up
(no GETMAINs outside the startup initialization paragraphs), and
restart the long-running task.  Once it has a message, it is STARTed
over to an application transaction.

Anything that uses a 3270 screen must be pseudo-conversational.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What happens if START is used without a TERminal id

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T22:40:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE1B7E7.84F50DE9@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<2068459.1037810641@dbforums.com> <6B62AB1FCE6886FF.0EAC9D9938643FE2.153A998A5FA1FEC3@lp.airnews.net> <3DDEF464.5D9FEEA5@sprynet.com> <E9F2C77B45804F93.65064FAC75BFC864.4EE933342608497A@lp.airnews.net> <3DE07962.5B2064C@sprynet.com> <3DE09BE9.FDA1B033@worldnet.att.net> <241120021805514194%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```
Joseph Katnic wrote:
> 
> In article <3DE09BE9.FDA1B033@worldnet.att.net>, Arnold Trembley
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> some other CICS mechanism. START TRAN, WRITEQ TD, Terminal Input, etc.

Umm, what about the CICS TCP/IP "Listener" transaction?  We don't use it
(don't have TS yet), but you can't tell me that it's not running all the
time...  It has to, because it's always listening for TCP/IP connection
requests.

This, among other things, is what my program does.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What happens if START is used without a TERminal id

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T20:24:00+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<251120022024002205%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<2068459.1037810641@dbforums.com> <6B62AB1FCE6886FF.0EAC9D9938643FE2.153A998A5FA1FEC3@lp.airnews.net> <3DDEF464.5D9FEEA5@sprynet.com> <E9F2C77B45804F93.65064FAC75BFC864.4EE933342608497A@lp.airnews.net> <3DE07962.5B2064C@sprynet.com> <3DE09BE9.FDA1B033@worldnet.att.net> <241120021805514194%josephk@iinet.net.au> <3DE1B7E7.84F50DE9@sprynet.com>`

```
In article <3DE1B7E7.84F50DE9@sprynet.com>, Frank Swarbrick
<infocat@sprynet.com> wrote:

> Umm, what about the CICS TCP/IP "Listener" transaction?  We don't use it
> (don't have TS yet), but you can't tell me that it's not running all the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> This, among other things, is what my program does.

Well, there's the exception that proves the rule! ;)

When you think about it though, the listener transaction does nothing
but accept packets and dispatch transactions. Probably does not even
use any CICS resources to speak of (except memory and cpu).
So it's probably ok.

I never did like any of the new "TCP/IP" implementations in CICS.
It should have been more like VTAM. These messy protocol things should
be hidden from the user. I should be able to define a set of "TCP/IP"
CICS terminals and auto-install them. They should accept HTTP requests
WITHOUT USER PROGRAMMING. I shoudl be able to define sockets to listen
on without user programming etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What happens if START is used without a TERminal id

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T20:22:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE2E902.BAC880F7@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<2068459.1037810641@dbforums.com> <6B62AB1FCE6886FF.0EAC9D9938643FE2.153A998A5FA1FEC3@lp.airnews.net> <3DDEF464.5D9FEEA5@sprynet.com> <E9F2C77B45804F93.65064FAC75BFC864.4EE933342608497A@lp.airnews.net> <3DE07962.5B2064C@sprynet.com> <3DE09BE9.FDA1B033@worldnet.att.net> <241120021805514194%josephk@iinet.net.au> <3DE1B7E7.84F50DE9@sprynet.com> <251120022024002205%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```
Joseph Katnic wrote:
> 
> In article <3DE1B7E7.84F50DE9@sprynet.com>, Frank Swarbrick
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> on without user programming etc.

Sounds great, in theory, but probably wouldn't work as well as you'd
think.  Sure, you could have it do what you said -- communicate via
HTTP.  But not all clients speak HTTP.  In fact, not all of the "TCP/IP
terminals" are clients.  Here are the TCP/IP devices I need to
communcate with and their attributes:

Racal Host Security Module.  Functions as a server.  Messages are
prefixed by a two-byte binary (network byte order) length header. 
Messages are in EBCDIC or ASCII depending on how you've chosen to
configure it.  Also has a UDP option.

VisaNet USMG.  Functions as a server.  Messages are prefixed by a
four-byte header.  The first two bytes are the message length in binary
(network byte order).  Bytes 3 and 4 are ignored.  Messages are in
EBCDIC.

MasterCard.  Functions as a server.  Messages are prefixed by a two-byte
binary (network byte order) length header.  Messages are in EBCDIC.

NCR ATM.  Functions as a client.  Messages are prefixed by a four-byte
ASCII numeric header.  Messages are in ASCII.  Header can be configured
to be three-byte ASCII numeric.  Header can be configured to be two-byte
binary in network byte order.  Header can be configured to be two-byte
binary in Intel byte order.

VisaNet connection sends a 'heart beat' message after 90 seconds of
inactivity.  This message is basically just a header with the length set
to zero.  MasterCard also supports this as an option.  They also both
support the host sending these messages at a smaller interval.  The
others do not support this option.

All four device types use 'always on' connections.  At CICS startup I
will connect to all server-type devices.  I also start up a 'listener'
socket to allow connections from the client-type devices.  Each
connection stays established the entire time CICS is up (barring
problems, of course!).

My point here is that there's no way IBM could possibly anticipate all
of these different 'protocols', no matter how similar they may be.  The
"TCP/IP Communcations Manager" that I am writing handles all of them. 
Each "terminal" has its options configured in a VSAM file.  But
certainly we may get yet another 'device' type with some protocol
specifications that I did not anticipate, so at that point I would have
to add more features to my program.

As much as I wish all of this had been done for me already I can
certainly see why it was not.
```

>terminal, nor any reason it should be.

Others have pointed out what I've been trying to say.  You are
confused between physical assignment and a logical one.  Even your
CECI started transaction would have a terminal logically tied to it
and that does not imply it would lock that terminal out of doing
something else.  By the way, I sure hope you find a better way than
CECI of starting your IP01 transaction once it goes into production.
I know from just a security standpoint, it is an evil, unforgivable
gaff to start anything in a production environment using CECI.

Regards,

IT IS SUGGESTED THAT THE LOBBY BE USED FOR THIS PURPOSE
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What happens if START is used without a TERminal id

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T15:02:33+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i4b4uug4c1dv21sq938didnili1ankqj0c@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<2068459.1037810641@dbforums.com> <6B62AB1FCE6886FF.0EAC9D9938643FE2.153A998A5FA1FEC3@lp.airnews.net> <3DDEF464.5D9FEEA5@sprynet.com> <E9F2C77B45804F93.65064FAC75BFC864.4EE933342608497A@lp.airnews.net>`

```
On Sat, 23 Nov 2002 12:15:38 -0500, SkippyPB
<swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>Are you sure?  Most CICS transactions have task storage assigned to
>them

make that "all CICS transactions"

>and that storage is usually assigned to some terminal.

No.  Some tasks have a terminal as a "primary facility", and they have
an associated TCTTE (plus more terminal related storage).  But there
is no need for that at all


>While it is possible the EIBTRMID is non existent, the
>task is still logically attached to the terminal that was running the
>task that issued the start.

No.  There may not even _be_ such a terminal.  Just assume that
terminal T123 does

EXEC CICS START TRANSID('X123') INTERVAL(120000) END-EXEC

Then the terminal logs off from CICS.  12 hours later transaction X123
starts - It surely is not associated with any terminal at all.

I ran into that on an IBM VM system which took the word 
"virtual" to limits never dreamed of.  "Virtual" machines 
were put to sleep and a batch CICS "transaction" would wake 
them up so they could access a file on another virtual 
machine, do some processing and then go back to sleep.  This 
was an undocumented system and it made it tough to figure 
out what was happening, when and by what program(s).
```

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [2002-08](2002-08.md#t-9cbd7113bb)._

#### ↳ Re: PPT entry for Statically/Dynamically called subprograms in CICS.

- **From:** naveen.masade <naveen.masade@eds.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T17:00:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2068475.1037811621@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0208280517.79feeb85@posting.google.com>`

```

Hi

 to me , your assumptions sounds good.
  But i have not seen a CICS program using static cobol calls...
  if u have seen your assumption is true..
 please do let me know.

QUOTE]Originally posted by Shyam Nivas 
Hi,
First of all sorry for putting CICS question on COBOL
group.
My assumptions are as follows:
1. For Statically called module no PPT entry is required because
   called module is part of main routine.
2. For Dynamically called module PPT entry is required as called
   module is loaded at run time.Simultaneously we have to put called
   module in CICS linked pack area.


Please reply me whether my assumptions are correct or not.


Regards.
Shyam 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PPT entry for Statically/Dynamically called subprograms in CICS.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-20T11:49:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<argi01$ut$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0208280517.79feeb85@posting.google.com> <2068475.1037811621@dbforums.com>`

```
All of this is pretty well documented in

  "3.1.1.4 Calling to or from COBOL programs"

at:
 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/3.1.1.4

and YES you need a PPT entry for dynamically called subroutines and do NOT
need it for statically linked routines
```

<a id="t-3b62698d35"></a>
## Thread: CBL_ALLOC_MEM — 2 msgs

### CBL_ALLOC_MEM

- **From:** pa@schaubroeck.be (Peter Alluyn)
- **Date:** 2002-11-21T09:06:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ddca16e.154899604@news.eunet.be>`

```
Hello,

Our programmers developped a client-server application using
NetExpress, in some programs they use CBL_ALLOC_MEM, in normal
situation this memory is given free with CBL_FREE_MEM.  For some
strange reason every nom and then the CBL_FREE_MEM isn't executed and
the memory stays allocated.  Is there a possibility to get an overview
off all allocated memory within a run-unit ?

Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: CBL_ALLOC_MEM

- **From:** Ethan Schultz <ethans@RosebudUSA.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T07:38:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE0C840.4C9C0846@RosebudUSA.com>`
- **References:** `<3ddca16e.154899604@news.eunet.be>`

```
Peter, 

I use these calls extensively and haven't run across this problem.
When you say the _FREE didn't 'execute', I assume you mean that
you did the call, but the memory was still allocated ?

Let me ask, how are you sure the memory is 'allocated' ?   Are 
you looking at TaskManager or something ?   I assume since you
mentioned looking for tools to view allocated that this is the 
closest you might have come to actually knowing for sure if the 
memory is allocated or not.

If you're simply accessing the memory that you _FREE'ed and not 
getting a 114 this doesn't necessarily mean the memory is still
allocated this is expecially true in multi-threaded programs.

The biggest issue I've run across with using DYN memory is making
sure the POINTER fields are reset to NULL when the _FREE is done,
also make sure you check RETURN-CODE after ALL you DYN calls.

Hope this helps.



Peter Alluyn wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Peter
```

Q: How many internet mail list subscribers does it take to 
change a light bulb?

A: 1,331:

1 to change the light bulb and to post to the mail list that 
the light bulb has been changed

14 to share similar experiences of changing light bulbs and 
how the light bulb could have been changed differently.

7 to caution about the dangers of changing light bulbs.

27 to point out spelling/grammar errors in posts about 
changing light bulbs.

53 to flame the spell checkers

156 to write to the list administrator complaining about the 
light bulb discussion and its inappropriateness to this mail 
list.

41 to correct spelling in the spelling/grammar flames.

109 to post that this list is not about light bulbs and to 
please take this email exchange to alt.lite.bulb

203 to demand that cross posting to alt.grammar, 
alt.spelling and alt.punctuation about changing light bulbs 
be stopped.

111 to defend the posting to this list saying that we are 
all use light bulbs and therefore the posts **are** relevant 
to this mail list.

306 to debate which method of changing light bulbs is 
superior, where to buy the best light bulbs, what brand of 
light bulbs work best for this technique, and what brands 
are faulty.

27 to post URLs where one can see examples of different 
light bulbs.

14 to post that the URLs were posted incorrectly, and to 
post corrected URLs.

3 to post about links they found from the URLs that are 
relevant to this list which makes light bulbs relevant to 
this list.

33 to concatenate all posts to date, then quote them 
including all headers and footers, and then add "Me Too."

12 to post to the list that they are unsubscribing because 
they cannot handle the light bulb controversy.

19 to quote the "Me Too's" to say, "Me Three."

4 to suggest that posters request the light bulb FAQ.

1 to propose new alt.change.lite.bulb newsgroup.

47 to say this is just what alt.physic.cold_fusion was meant 
for, leave it here.

143 votes for alt.lite.bulb.

<a id="t-4543c4450e"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL file open error — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL file open error

- **From:** "David Thomas" <d.m.thomas@swan.ac.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-11-21T17:25:52
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<arj4qt$pbf$1@news.swman.net.uk>`

```
I'm getting the following error while trying to open a data file.

JMP0310I-I OPEN ERROR, FILE=EMPMAS.DAT, 'ACC-METHOD', PGM=TESTWRITE
ADR=004011F0

The organization of the file is indexed, the access method is sequential.

The program works prefectly under MicroFocus Netexpress, so the data and the
program code should be OK.

I've tried moving the programs and data into the Fujitsu 'FSC' directory, in
case it was a paths problem, but that had no effect.


Can anybody help a confused newbie?

Thanks guys.


Here's the specification of the data file:
*---------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL file open error

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-11-21T18:59:15+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<arj716$sdh$1@mailnews.kub.nl>`
- **References:** `<arj4qt$pbf$1@news.swman.net.uk>`

```
quote: The organization of the file is indexed, the access method is
sequential.

That's your problem. You can't access an indexed file sequentially. The
error msg also states a problem 'ACC-METHOD' which refers to the method you
use to acces the file. Sequential can only be used on Sequential of
Line-sequential files.

Rik

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL file open error

- **From:** "Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-21T19:33:59+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<arj96q$bc6$1@reader14.wxs.nl>`
- **References:** `<arj4qt$pbf$1@news.swman.net.uk> <arj716$sdh$1@mailnews.kub.nl>`

```
Oops! Is this a restriction within the Fujitsu environment ? Because it is
an ANSII compliant way to read data sequential from an indexed file. This
was used in the old days to increase performance. At those days the indexed
files consisted of two seperate files (or data area's in one file)
containing data and index entries. Which means two physical reads when
accessing one record (is still something like that but modern disks are so
fast it does'nt matter anymore (usually)).

To increase performance we accessed indexed files sequentially when possible
(could be done only when the order of the records being processed did'nt
matter).

Kind regards, Danny.

    I may be wrong, but it sounds like he was talking about
reading in PHYSICAL order.  This is not possible with std cobol.

    Doing so is often possible, but it would be highly non portable.

    A new version runtime will frequently break that sort of operation.

the

in

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

*---------------------------------------------------------------------------
> --
>        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>       *-------------------------------------------------------

You most certainly can have ACCESS SEQUENTIAL for an ISAM file - both in
Fujitsu and the rest of the world.

Unfortunately, both in Fujitsu and the rest of the world, you cannot open a
file as either input or I-O if the file does not exist. Does it exist?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL file open error

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-22T14:01:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDE38B9.4ECF9498@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<arj4qt$pbf$1@news.swman.net.uk> <5bKdnYyICcM31ECgXTWcpA@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 
> Unfortunately, both in Fujitsu and the rest of the world, you cannot open a
> file as either input or I-O if the file does not exist. Does it exist?

Yes you can.

Specify SELECT OPTIONAL .. and it will create the file if it doesn't
exist when OPEN I-O.  If it is OPEN INPUT and doesn't exist the file
will be treated as if it does exist but is empty (ie first READ will
follow AT END).  In both cases the file will be 'opened'.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL file open error

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-21T21:33:11
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ddd4ed0_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<arj4qt$pbf$1@news.swman.net.uk>`

```
David, I am staggered at the arrant nonsense you have received in response
to this. Only Jerry Mouse gave you correct information, from the posts I
have seen.

Here are the facts:

1. Fujitsu, like all standard COBOL, DOES support sequential access of an
indexed file.

2. The definition you have posted is fine. (I assume you have a
complementary FD?)

3. If you created the file under Net Express you will not be able to read it
with Fujitsu. The internal ISAM formats are completely different.

I suspect that the last is causing your problem.

Pete.





David Thomas <d.m.thomas@swan.ac.uk> wrote in message
