#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL file open error

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-21T14:45:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211211445.257d3861@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<arj4qt$pbf$1@news.swman.net.uk>`

```
"David Thomas" <d.m.thomas@swan.ac.uk> wrote

> JMP0310I-I OPEN ERROR, FILE=EMPMAS.DAT, 'ACC-METHOD', PGM=TESTWRITE
> ADR=004011F0
> 
> The program works prefectly under MicroFocus Netexpress, so the data and the
> program code should be OK.

What is the OPEN ?  If it is OPEN INPUT and you are trying to read a
file that was created under NetExpress then the problem is that the
file format will need conversion.  Fujitsu and MF indexed file
structures are different.

If you are expecting to create the file and it is OPEN I-O then you
may need to add OPTIONAL to the SELECT.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL file open error

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-22T08:29:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDE5B4E.3040201@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<arj4qt$pbf$1@news.swman.net.uk>`

```
If this code ran under MS, then I suspect you are going to have to code 
in a declaratives section,
even if it is only a dummy that ignores everything.  The biggest 
difference beteween the two compilers
seems to be error handling.  MS will continue, returning a status code 
on error (for example, if the
file does not exist, MS will return a status "35").  With Fujitsu, the 
same program will abort with
a message such as you have.  If you put in a declaratives, informing the 
run time that you want to
ignore the standard run-time error returns, the run time will behave 
like MS.

Donald


David Thomas wrote:

>I'm getting the following error while trying to open a data file.
>
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL file open error

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-23T11:37:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDF6873.9D53652@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<arj4qt$pbf$1@news.swman.net.uk> <3DDE5B4E.3040201@Sympatico.ca>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> If this code ran under MS, then I suspect you are going to have to code
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> like MS.

No, that is wrong.  Fujitsu does not need declaratives in order to
return file status values.  I never use declaratives, and always get
file status values returned with Fujitsu, exactly as for MF - except 9x
values are different.  Of course if there is no file status in the
select then a declarative will be required.

The 'secret' of getting rid of the messages is to have
@MessOutFile=filename or @NoMessage in the cobol85.cbr file and then the
program runs 'correctly'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL file open error

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-23T11:42:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDFDA39.3040504@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<arj4qt$pbf$1@news.swman.net.uk> <3DDE5B4E.3040201@Sympatico.ca> <3DDF6873.9D53652@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:

>Donald Tees wrote:
>  
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, you can override with a CBR file as well.  With a declaratives, you 
do not need one.  I just do not
like an external file that determines how the program runs.  Too easy 
for problems down the line.

Donald


Donald.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-23T14:55:59
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Hmmm... waiting to get "payed" with "baited" breath...

Maybe people are skeptical of taking lessons from someone who spells the way
he talks...(the corollary to that being that he probably programs the way he
thinks, and we all know how lethal that can be...<G>)

While it doesn't really matter in a Newsgroup, it certainly matters in the
world of commerce.

I receive about  12 letters a week from various agencies and consultancies
offering me services. I immediately bin any that have spelling or (serious)
grammatical errors (I am happy to accept colloquialisms) in them.

My reasoning goes that if they haven't got the time to check mail before
they send it, they probably haven't got time to check code before they
install it either. Of course, if they DID check it, and still didn't spot
the errors, then they are simply incompetent and there is no point in
dealing with them. Apart from that, I need an excuse to filter them, and
this is as good as any <G>

So, you are asking, what do I do with the 1 that (sometmes) survives this
check? (yes, it is a sad reflection on our times...both of the languages I
love the best  (English and COBOL) are in decline for different reasons...)

Well, at least they are in with a chance... I usually note what they are
offering and file or circulate them until such time as I actually need the
service offered. Of course, once a relationship has been established, then
the tendency is to deal in future with the devil you know.

So track record and service focus win every time.

Pete.



Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
```

```
> > That is Australia, a completely different country.  We have no accent
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

Yep, but we aint ockers.

We're Kiwis.  We do have an accent, and the closest it comes to is an
Australian News announcer.  - in other words, we sound like Aussies trying
to imitate English RP (received pronunciation).

We sound a bit like Aussies, I guess - in the same sort of way that
Canadians sound like Southerners for outsiders (you all drawl).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T21:36:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE14983.3A9C2BC4@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <3DDFB9CC.2060204@nycap.rr.com> <217e491a.0211231650.59bc119d@posting.google.com> <%34E9.2668$Kw6.1573758@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <arr896$475$1@hermes.nz.eds.com>`

```


Tim Scrivens wrote:

> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
> news:%34E9.2668$Kw6.1573758@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Canadians sound like Southerners for outsiders (you all drawl).

I agree you do have an accent. It's fifty years ago in Egypt but I could tell
the difference between the accents of Wg. Cdr. Jim Lombard RAAF and Sqn. Ldr.
Gott RNZAF. To put it 'delicately', Kiwi isn't so harsh.

Correct observation about Yanks and Canucks - but there is a distinct
difference, including slight variations as you move from the West to East
Coast of Canada.  Perhaps the most distinctive sound to an American ear is
when Canadians use words with the 'out' sound - they tend to come out 'oot' or
'ouwt'. Canucks say 'roof' like you whereas our American brethren say 'rough'.
Somehow, "Let's tile the rough", doesn't sound quite kosher.

Richard implied you don't have accents, having admitted that he is tone deaf
anyway.
I betcha you could tell that Richard has an 'accent'. What's your Kiwi
equivalent of pommie bastard ?

And as regards accents - my dear wife, born in UK like moi and  works in
retail. "I love your accent", say the customers. She smiles sweetly and
replies, "I don't have an accent, but you Canucks do".

Not now, because they've more or less switched to 'Estuary English', but back
in our days in Somerset, ('63 - '75),  a local yokel would say, "Tell Oi where
'urs at, an' Oi'll come where urs to" Just in case - translation - "Tell me
where you are and I'll come there" <G>.

It's got to be several years ago but a girl working with Eileen and studying
English literature at the U of C said she had a real problem reading Bronte's
'Wuthering Heights'. Eileen looked at one of the offending passages, "Pin back
your lug 'oles !", said the character with his Yorkshire accent. <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
> I agree you do have an accent. It's fifty years ago in Egypt but I could
tell
> the difference between the accents of Wg. Cdr. Jim Lombard RAAF and Sqn.
Ldr.
> Gott RNZAF. To put it 'delicately', Kiwi isn't so harsh.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> when Canadians use words with the 'out' sound - they tend to come out
'oot' or
> 'ouwt'. Canucks say 'roof' like you whereas our American brethren say
'rough'.
> Somehow, "Let's tile the rough", doesn't sound quite kosher.

Aah - we have a very similar thing.

Aussies reckon Kiwis have a problem with the ee sound.

You will often get an Aussie attempting a Kiwi accent by saying "Fush and
Chups" for fish and chips, and "Sudney Brudge" for the coathanger by the
Opera house.

Of course, we reckon they say "Seeedney Breeedge"

> Richard implied you don't have accents, having admitted that he is tone
deaf
> anyway.
> I betcha you could tell that Richard has an 'accent'. What's your Kiwi
> equivalent of pommie bastard ?

Nope, we do "Pommie Bastard" as well.

We also do (derisively)  "Bronzed Ocker", and "Struckenfalian" for the
people from West Island (Australia).


> And as regards accents - my dear wife, born in UK like moi and  works in
> retail. "I love your accent", say the customers. She smiles sweetly and
> replies, "I don't have an accent, but you Canucks do".
>
> Not now, because they've more or less switched to 'Estuary English', but
back
> in our days in Somerset, ('63 - '75),  a local yokel would say, "Tell Oi
where
> 'urs at, an' Oi'll come where urs to" Just in case - translation - "Tell
me
> where you are and I'll come there" <G>.
>
> It's got to be several years ago but a girl working with Eileen and
studying
> English literature at the U of C said she had a real problem reading
Bronte's
> 'Wuthering Heights'. Eileen looked at one of the offending passages, "Pin
back
> your lug 'oles !", said the character with his Yorkshire accent. <G>

Just been reading "McAuslan in the Rough" by George MacDonald Fraser
(recommended).  It has Glaswegian patois in it - If Eileen can't cope with
"Pin back your lug'oles", I wonder how she'd go with "See you, Jimmy, yon's
a swithering bamfpot, him"  (Look at that person of little worth).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-11-30T05:42:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20021130004217.00349.00000140@mb-fn.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE14983.3A9C2BC4@shaw.ca>`

```
>From: "James J. Gavan" jjgavan@shaw.ca 
>Date: 11/24/02 4:36 PM Eastern Standard Time

>Correct observation about Yanks and Canucks - but there is a distinct
>difference, including slight variations as you move from the West to East
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Quite correct Jimmy :)  But I don't think you Canucks sound the least bit like
our Southeners.  I do find that the 'oot' to be stronger from those in the West
though. I also saw in another of your posts that you called yourself 'Jimmy the
Sassneach'.  The only time I've seen that term was in several books by Diane
Gabeldon referring to the English woman taken captive by the Scotts.  Isn't the
Canadian accent a watered down version of the Scottish with a bit of language
drift thrown in for good measure?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-30T07:18:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE8646F.E4361720@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3DE14983.3A9C2BC4@shaw.ca> <20021130004217.00349.00000140@mb-fn.aol.com>`

```


YukonMama wrote:

> >From: "James J. Gavan" jjgavan@shaw.ca
> >Date: 11/24/02 4:36 PM Eastern Standard Time
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> drift thrown in for good measure?

"Sassenach" (Gaelic) is a Scottish derisive term for all them damn southerners
below the Scottish border. Alistair or one of the other Scots will have to confirm
it's true meaning.

Now as to the Canadian accent - that's a difficult one for me - have to get
confirmation from the people actually born here. I came across from UK in '75 and
went back for the first time in 2000 for the J4 meeting where I met Bill. Comment
from a local in a pub, I hadn't lost my English (S.E.) accent. It's a guess, but as
the general southern accent has become nasal, at least to my ear, I suspect to
somebody who is observant, there is a slight difference with the 25 year gap,
coupled of course with the fact that without trying, I use 'Americanisms'.

As I said Canadian accents do differ. Slightly harsh, to my ear, in Vancouver B.C..
probably because it is a big city. Calgary, a very general sort of accent,
flavoured with A US influence, all them Lone Star folks up here in the oil patch,
BUT - I can discern what I call a rural Alberta accent, laid back but pleasing.
Then east, Saskatchewan, from those here in Alberta not too dissimilar from the
rural Albertan. Then I get foggy as we move further east, picking up Ontario
'sounds' from the national TV stations. Difficult to explain, but Donald might
latch on, national broadcasters like Lloyd Robertson do have a distinctive sound to
my ear - very different from what I hear in Calgary.

Quebec - well naturally "I 'ave a slighte French acc-ccent if I am a Quebecois" The
East coast is primarily a rural (fishermen) sound. When I first came over, within a
couple of weeks I was invited to a social do and drinks. I asked this charming and
amusing man, 'Which part of Ireland are your from ?". His reply, "Newfoundland"
<G>.

Historically of course, the East Coast was settled by French, Scots and Irish - so
yes there's probably a mixture of them all. Haven't visited, and could be wrong,
but the sounds I hear from the East Coast are more Irish than Scottish. And as I
write this I can't think of that Scottish "brrrr" sound appearing in Canadian
speech - others might be tuned in differently.

Do you recall the series on TV, 'The History of English" about 20 years ago ?.
Actually Scottish, (or Scots gaelic) is closer in sound and words to Dutch/German -
kirk/kerk/kirche. My time in the RAF in N. Germany was in Westphalia and the locals
were a little humourless and considered to be 'stur' in German. The Scots word is
'dour'.

"Now yew all wan' I spek like a'is frum Kentucky",  or whatever, I could do a damn
site better job than even attempting to do a Canuck accent. (I'm amazed at the
number of English actors being used in the States and putting on American accents,
some very good and some disastrous like Larry Olivier playing Douglas McArthur)..

Just a footnote - our friend Guy proudly telling us he's from Norse(Viking)/Franc
stock. That's the bloody trouble with the French they do everything to excess. Not
content with beating the Anglo-Saxons in 1066 at Hastings and taking over perfide
Albion, the buggers shipped west and pestered the poor Irish for 1,000 years <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-30T10:01:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE8FD04.27AA@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE14983.3A9C2BC4@shaw.ca> <20021130004217.00349.00000140@mb-fn.aol.com> <3DE8646F.E4361720@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> Just a footnote - our friend Guy proudly telling us he's from Norse(Viking)/Franc
> stock. That's the bloody trouble with the French they do everything to excess. Not
> content with beating the Anglo-Saxons in 1066 at Hastings and taking over perfide
> Albion, the buggers shipped west and pestered the poor Irish for 1,000 years <G>

We've probably all got a bit of everything.  I was surprised to find a few years ago 
that we also have a bit of "the blood of Abraham".  My sister and I were checking out 
ancestors in the old records but couldn't find great-great-grandma in the baptismal 
register of the village where we KNEW she was born.  We suddenly realised why - her 
maiden name was SARAH RUBIN -  Oy Veh!!!  Luckily they didn't pass all the traditions 
down, or I might have found myself "culturally modified".
```

```
>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Maybe people are skeptical of taking lessons from someone who spells the

Hahaha! Not bad...

Paul Hogan is Australian so I make no further comment. If you check your
Atlas you will see there is 1400 miles of ocean between them and us. In
terms of culture it is much further...<G>

Yes, I am a New Zealander but I have spent the last 30 years travelling
around the World and as a result my accent was once memorably described as
"Mid-Atlantic Antipodean" <G>

This mail (and the mail you quoted) is being written in the Midlands of the
United Kingdom.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T22:55:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE15AC5.3040602@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```


Alistair Maclean wrote:
> In article <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospa
> m.enternet.co.nz> writes
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

And U.S. English is evolving the fastest.  We are more than 
happy to take words from anyone's language and make it our 
own.  We're economical as well.  Look at all the U's we've 
dropped, like in "color" as opposed to "colour", etc.

We've even had movements to change to a phonetic form of 
spelling - "kat" for "cat".  Think of what that would do to 
your spell checkers!  If you look at old writing from 200 
years ago, great people had very bad spelling.  Sometimes in 
the same document they would spell a word several different 
ways.
```

```
>
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> ways.

We do that now, and never even notice.

OK and Okay, for example.

And from memory, there are 4 acceptable spellings for "panatella", depending
on which dictionary you use.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T05:20:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE1B144.89DB4F0F@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3DE15AC5.3040602@nycap.rr.com> <arro4u$lri$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3DE18E0F.40502@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham wrote:

> Tim Scrivens wrote:
> > "Robert Graham" <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> Can they even use "R.S.V.P." any more???

You are dead wrong about "irregardless"; it's in the same category as "I 'aven't
got none" a sort of double negative, beyond regard and regard-less. In sheer
despair at the abuse of the mother tongue in N. America, I believe I'm correct in
saying that the venerable Oxford Dictionary introduced 'irregardless' as, sigh,
being acceptable. Did that about the same time they agreed it was OK for the
Chairman of the Board to sing "Nooh York ! Nooh York !".

PS: Netscape spellchecker, (using either the US or English versions),  queried
'irregardless' but offered no alternatives <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan

```
In article <3DE15AC5.3040602@nycap.rr.com>, Robert Graham
<rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> writes
>
>And U.S. English is evolving the fastest.  We are more than 
>happy to take words from anyone's language and make it our 
>own.  We're economical as well.  Look at all the U's we've 
>dropped, like in "color" as opposed to "colour", etc.

I believe that your spelling of color may be rooted in history a little
further back than our spelling of colour.

>
>We've even had movements to change to a phonetic form of 
>spelling - "kat" for "cat". 

We did that, but only for people with learning difficulties.

> Think of what that would do to 
>your spell checkers!  

I'm still correcting my spell checker to use the -ize rather than -ise
ending to words (recognize, etc.).

>If you look at old writing from 200 
>years ago, great people had very bad spelling.  Sometimes in 
>the same document they would spell a word several different 
>ways.

William Shakespeare had something like 24 different ways that he spelled
his name.

>
```

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)

- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message news:<OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>...
> In article <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospa
> m.enternet.co.nz> writes
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Email left unread due to misspelling of "sometmes".

Hahaha! Well done, Alistair <G>

(I DID say that it doesn't matter in a Newsgroup...my mail here is
certainly not commercial <G>)

> 
> ps English is mutating/evolving and expanding not declining.

Agreed. It has been doing so since Chaucer's time. I guess what
saddens me is the fact that it is not being taught properly and we see
the result in our everyday correspondence. There was a recent poll
here in the UK (in fact, the final was last night) to find the
Greatest Briton. Shakespeare made it to the top 10 but it was
painfully apparent in the debates that most kids are not being exposed
to him, or, if they are, they are not being helped to appreciate the
beauty and timelessness of his writing. (I'm NOT saying that
Shakespeare IS English; just one of the many indicators...)
Anyway, don't start me on another off-topic rant <G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T01:29:05
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan

- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <3DE21BB6.4010504@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
news:3DE21BB6.4010504@nycap.rr.com...
>
> Well, Shakespeare may not be so far off topic.  One time, I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'll never forget the ICL COBOL manual, written sometime in the late
60s/early 70s, which said:

"Future implementations of the DIVIDE verb will permit the use of the
REMAINDER construct. "'Tis a consummation devoutly to be wished." "

(It was obviously written by some poor English Lit. graduate who was
DETERMINED to get MacBeth into COBOL.

Now, see, if that had been me, I would have used another of MacBeth's
soliloquys: "We are but teachers preaching bloody instructions, which, being
taught, return to plague th' inventor...This even handed justice commends
our poisoned chalice to our own lips." Now doesn't that sum up programming
and debugging perfectly?  And good ol' Willy wrote it 400 years before
computers were invented! Don't tell me the man wasn't a genius...<G>)

Pete.
```

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> Now, see, if that had been me, I would have used another of MacBeth's
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Well now, as to them returning to "plague th' inventor", I 
don't know about that, at least not many that "I" have 
invented.  Most of my projects were as a consultant who 
moved on to other clients after the system was installed, so 
any plagues that arose were inflicted on the maintenance 
programmer (lucky dog).  I like a Scottish proverb which 
says something like "The moving hand, having writ, moves on 
never to return."  Although being imperfect, I have had on 
occasion been handed the "poisoned chalice" because a 
mistake on my part brought the system to a screeching halt. 
  Not often mind you, and not recently, but in the early 
days when I once received 6 boxes of 8-1/2 by 11 dump.  Boy 
did I quickly learn how to suppress dumps in my JCL jobs. :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-28T00:03:20
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<3de55e52_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <3DE21BB6.4010504@nycap.rr.com> <3de42205_1@Usenet.com> <3DE4C833.7000300@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
news:3DE4C833.7000300@nycap.rr.com...
> I like a Scottish proverb which
> says something like "The moving hand, having writ, moves on
> never to return."

Arrrghhhh! No, I simply can't stand to see one of my favourite pieces
mis-quoted and claimed for Scotland...<G>

The quotation comes from "The Rubaiat of Omar Khyaam" and was written around
400 years ago (I think...it's late and I am too tired to check the
reference) by an uneducated Arab tent maker  named Omar Khyaam (As far as I
know, he never visited Scotland...<G>)

"The moving finger writes
  And, having writ,
  Moves on.
  Nor all your piety or wit
  Shall lure it back to cancel half a line.
  Nor all your tears
  Wash out a word of it."

The translation into English was done by Richard Burton, the famous explorer
who competed with Speke to find the source of the Nile in the 19th Century.

If you like Omar's thoughts on the inevitability of the passage of Time, you
might enjoy his description of morning...(another of my favourites <G>)

"Awake!
 For morning in the bowl of night
 Has flung the stone
 that puts the stars to flight. (the Sun...)
And Lo!
The hunter of the East (Sun again)
Has caught the Sultan's turret (the Sky)
In a noose of light."

I make no apology whatsoever for the above off-topic and totally irrelevant
post. When you look at the drivel that gets posted here, it is about time
the quality of the drivel was raised a bit...<G>

Pete

```

- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <3DE21BB6.4010504@nycap.rr.com> <3de42205_1@Usenet.com> <3DE4C833.7000300@nycap.rr.com> <3de55e52_5@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
> news:3DE4C833.7000300@nycap.rr.com...
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete


Well, Pete, I quoted it based upon the memory of a book 
about Edenburgh that my mother had.  It has also been a long 
time since I read the Rubaiyat.  Two of my favorite parts are:

(This one is perhaps the most well known?)

A book of verses underneath the bough,
A jug of wine, a loaf of bread - and thou
Beside me singing in the wilderness -
Oh, wilderness were Paradise now!
  (Stanza XII - Fitzgerald Version)

My personal favorite is this:

Today is thine to spend, but not tomorrow,
Counting on 'morrows breedeth naught but sorrow;
Oh! Squander not this breath that Heaven hath lent thee,
Nor make too sure another breath to borrow!
   (Stanza XVIII - Whinfield Version)

However, when it comes to programming by imperfect 
programmers, one can only hope that the manager remembers 
that "Mercy was made for sinners" (Stanza XXVII, Whinfield 
Version).

All my programs are not works of poetry, but I try! :)
```

```
Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
news:3DE5930B.9090603@nycap.rr.com...
>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Er... I recall the last line as:
"And Wilderness were Paradise enow..." ("enow" being a poetic form of
"enough")

As you can see, it changes the meaning subtly... (Could be differences in
various translations; I know the Fitzgerald one is the most popular.)


> My personal favorite is this:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yeah, that's a great one... didn't have it in my memory but will from now
on...<G>

My father's favourite (he died in 1957 and introduced me to Omar Khyaam when
I was eight) was:

"Passing by the Marketplace one day
 I saw a potter
 Thumping his wet clay.
 And with its all obliterated tongue
 It murmured "Gently Brother, gently pray..." "


> However, when it comes to programming by imperfect
> programmers, one can only hope that the manager remembers
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It is my belief that anyone who appreciates poetry is capable of writing
great COBOL...but of course, I can't prove it...<G>

Pete.
```

```
had a translation from someone else, as you say,

Kind of brings things into focus, doesn't it.

I'd be glad to prove it.  If you are ever in a situation 
where you need another COBOL programmer I'm sure I can be 
available (did you say you were over in England?)  I have 
20+ years experience - IBM mainframe mostly, with some 
MicroFocus & RM COBOL.  I'd be willing to travel.  I almost 
got an assignment in Edinburgh but mad cow / hoof in mouth / 
or something caused it to fall through.  Bummer. :(

A love of poetry got me through high school.  With Hamlet 
and MacBeth causing me to be willing to actually attend 
instead of dropping out, heading to Woodstock and becoming a 
Hippie. (I was in High School in the late 60's.)  <GM> [as 
in "Groovie Man"] ;)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-30T11:02:46
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<3de89975_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <3DE21BB6.4010504@nycap.rr.com> <3de42205_1@Usenet.com> <3DE4C833.7000300@nycap.rr.com> <3de55e52_5@Usenet.com> <3DE5930B.9090603@nycap.rr.com> <3de7f767_1@Usenet.com> <3DE8153C.9080804@nycap.rr.com>`

```

Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
news:3DE8153C.9080804@nycap.rr.com...
> >
> > It is my belief that anyone who appreciates poetry is capable of writing
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It is a cause of great sadness to me that I am currently unable even to use
COBOL on our site. (I simply can't make an effective Business Case for doing
so.) (Mind you, we don't use Java either...)

Everything is moving to Siebel (personally, I don't think this is a good
move but the decision was taken before I came.)

There is nothing wrong with Siebel on a small to medium scale, but for a
large Enterprise I have severe reservations. The Version we have is a
two-tier model and it is very rigid and unbelievably inefficient as more
terminals log in. (Each screen will generate its own DB
connections...(Horrors!)). Siebel are suggesting we upgrade to the "new"
three tier model (but it is still tied to the same rigid DB design). We are
not rushing to do this as it will cost millions...and we already spent
millions on it...

The really sad thing is that our "legacy" systems are performing fairly well
now <G>. These systems are not mainframe based (as you might expect when you
hear "legacy"; with the exception of one application which IS mainframe
based and written in PL/1, and will be decommissioned within the next 6
months...), they are all client/server and Web enabled. The heart of them is
SQL Server and ORACLE with XML transports to the presentation layer. There
is large use of components and we expect many of these to be pulled through
to the Siebel solution.

The main part of my brief is to keep the Business running while we effect
the transfer to Siebel. This means maintaining our legacy systems and really
ensuring there is a fall-back position if the Siebel implementation should
be delayed. We are trying to avoid doing major enhancements to the legacy
systems but my people are so good they can very often do stuff in a couple
of days that would take weeks using procedural code. Seeing how they do
things has really made me sit up and take notice.

The Web is king for presentation and it is XML that gets stuff there.

DB is written using stored procedures and components (we have SQL Server and
ORACLE tables with over 50,000,000 rows...it frightens me to death, but it
performs OK. We are running most of it on the top of the range COMPAQ server
with 8 processors and terabytes of storage. There are a total of 12 servers
in the Domains I manage, but that is the main one.

Data flow is accomplished with DTS (Data Transformation Services) which is
just a drag and drop tool. (This achieves moving  and connecting data
streams, sorting them, and converting their formats if required, with a few
mouse clicks... it is really awesome. Days of work with procedural code, in
seconds)

It is for all these reasons that I am on record as believing the days of
procedural code are numbered. I have talked to other managers in similar
businesses. None of them are still using procedural code and they smile at
the idea ...(Oh yes, the "good old days"...COBOL, PL/I, VB...).

I believe the role of COBOL will become more and more as maintenance for
existing legacy code and (certainly in my personal case) for the development
of Business components. I can't see major systems development being
undertaken in COBOL, or any other procedural language, in the
not-too-distant future.

That is how I perceive it at the moment. It may change. Meantime, I would
urge all of you who make a living out of COBOL to expand your skill set.

Pete.






 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
Yes, I agree.  I went through a VB learning phase. Now I am

so many "flavors of the month" that it is hard to keep up. 
Worse is the job situation around here where, if there are 
any jobs, they are asking for "the sun, the moon and the

of us who can swim in anything are out of luck.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T13:07:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com>, Peter E.
C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> writes
>Agreed. It has been doing so since Chaucer's time. I guess what
>saddens me is the fact that it is not being taught properly and we see
>the result in our everyday correspondence.

I wan't taught proper at school either. I was taught French grammar but
not English grammar. Just checked the spelling of Grammar (it's ok). My
OED doesn't list Grammer.

> There was a recent poll
>here in the UK (in fact, the final was last night) to find the
>Greatest Briton.

Got it on the video. I'm glad that Winnie won. Shame that the rest were
(with the exception of Ollie Cromwell) charlatans, ne'er do wells or
born/married to their greatness.

> Shakespeare made it to the top 10 but it was
>painfully apparent in the debates that most kids are not being exposed
>to him, or, if they are, they are not being helped to appreciate the
>beauty and timelessness of his writing. 

I was tortured at school by having to do Romeo and Juliet, Merchant of
Venice, MacBeth (a clansman of mine) and Two Gentlemen From Verona. I
plan on self-abusing myself by doing Hamlet sometime, just so that I can
see how Guildenstern and Rosencrantz fit in. BTW, I love MacBeth, even
though the story is an English propaganda exercise (should that be
exercize?).


>(I'm NOT saying that
>Shakespeare IS English; just one of the many indicators...)
>Anyway, don't start me on another off-topic rant <G>
>
>Pete.
```

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<cpGdnfLO8vvN2n-gXTWcpg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
"Alistair Maclean" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
>
> I was tortured at school by having to do Romeo and Juliet, Merchant of
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Then you'll appreciate:

"Is it thy tiny sword in front I'm glimpsing,
With its blunt bit pointing to my wrists? I wish to touch it:
I find I'm no good doing it, but I spy it still..."

This is but a part of Macbeth in which all "a's" and "e's" have been
excised.

Or:

"To be, or not to be; that's the query:
Whether you would be nobler to suffer mentally
The stones and arrows of outrageous fortune,
Or to take arms to oppose a sea of troubles,
And through combat end them? To pass on, to sleep;
No more..."

for five acts until:

"The rest be hush-hush."

Never having uttered the letter "i"

Currently work is afoot on Othello without the o's. It isn't easy, but
what's art without suffering?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T17:51:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE2661E.3BC021C2@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```


Alistair Maclean wrote:

>
> I was tortured at school by having to do Romeo and Juliet, Merchant of
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Certainly sympathize with you on the choice of plays. I was more fortunate.
We did MacBeth, Julius Caesar and Henry V. I think you could say that my
three had more appeal to snotty nosed young boys. Coupled with that the
college put on a live version of Richard II - can still visualize it.
Somebody was courageous enough to use Gustav Holst's 'Planet Suite' as
background music. Still brings tears of emotion to my eyes when I recall the
4th Movement, Jupiter, the Bringer of Jollity which contains that beautiful
piece of music with the words, "And I vow to thee, my country....". And that
used as background as the ailing John of Gaunt says his famous, "This
sceptred Isle, this demi paradise....." - Zowee !. (I know that isn't a
correct quote, but can't be bothered to dig out my Shakespeare).

No, haven't got an encyclopaedic memory - just so happens I was playing
'Planet Suite' two days ago, and have the CD on my desk.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<3de4220a_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```

Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> In article <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com>, Peter E.
> C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> writes
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think you'll find she's your Father or Mother's Mother...<G>

> > There was a recent poll
> >here in the UK (in fact, the final was last night) to find the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Perhaps a little hard on Nelson, Darwin, Brunel, and Newton, don't you
think, Alistair? (Agree about Diana, Elizabeth the First (although you have
to admire her courage), and John Lennon (admire his ideals but still
basically a ne'er-do-well <G>). Of course they missed the real British
heroes who are loved by millions, Sooty, Basil Brush, TeleTubbies, Bob the
Builder, Postman Pat, and Mr Blobby...

> > Shakespeare made it to the top 10 but it was
> >painfully apparent in the debates that most kids are not being exposed
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is little opportunity for self abuse in Hamlet. Ophelia is a mournful
cow who ends up drowning herself (not in the least erotic). I saw Diana Rigg
naked as Lady MacBeth many years ago on the West End stage and the image
haunts me to this day. (For those of you who get re-runs of the original
Avengers with Steed and Mrs Peel, she was Mrs Peel...I can assure you she
was even more devastating without the leather outfit than she was with
it...<G>) I believe that Nicole Kidman did the same role recently (but I
might be confusing that with the Blue Room). Anyway, for my money, Lady
MacBeth is the sexiest role in Shakespeare...

Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are inserted into Hamlet for no apperent reason
other than to make Hamlet look smart when juxtaposed against their
stupidity...<G>

Pete.

```

```
Robert Graham wrote:

> Donald Tees wrote:
> > Robert Graham wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> like COBOL programs, "legacy". ;)

The thing about 'legacy' is that it is proven and resilient and lasts. Now
hum 10 Beatles songs for me. Now do the same for the other mob fronted by
that Neanderthal man !

Not my theory but one proposed by a Londoner who used to be over here in
Calgary. The four cheeky chappies took Britain by storm back in 1963. But
they were from the 'provinces', although Liverpool has a place in its own
right. Somebody in the nation's capital felt 'affronted'. "Gor blimey mate.
We can't 'ave these bleedin' Scousers takin' over. We betta form a group of
Londerners".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T19:23:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE51DC3.5050407@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3de4220a_1@Usenet.com> <3DE4C95E.9010504@nycap.rr.com> <3DE4F592.6060406@Sympatico.ca> <3DE4D685.5080801@nycap.rr.com> <3DE50995.691611EF@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Robert Graham wrote:
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I was visiting Scotland in 1962 when the Beatles were making 
  all kinds of waves.  I saw them on a program (or in 
British - a programme??) called Make It Or Break It, where a 
pile driver was used to smash the loosing record (looked 
like a variation on American Bandstand with construction 
gear thrown in).  Needless to say, the Beatles won.  The 
song?  She Loves You, which everyone on the plane back was 
singing.

Instead of humming, can I at least put in "ONE" and let John 
  & Co. do the singing? :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T19:39:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<as3728$e8f$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3de4220a_1@Usenet.com> <3DE4C95E.9010504@nycap.rr.com> <3DE4F592.6060406@Sympatico.ca> <3DE4D685.5080801@nycap.rr.com> <3DE50995.691611EF@shaw.ca>`

```

On 27-Nov-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> The thing about 'legacy' is that it is proven and resilient and lasts. Now
> hum 10 Beatles songs for me. Now do the same for the other mob fronted by
> that Neanderthal man !

Lots of music isn't designed to be hummable.   In fact, the social need to be
able to whistle has gone down as there are fewer melodies in modern music.

My mother's era had more tunes, and much better dance music than my era.  But
even in my era, people had practice writing tunes.  Musical theatre had several
songs that were singable by just about anybody.

But artists would rather be different than to try to improve on established
media, so music has changed.   I like some music without melodies - and listen
to Music From The Hearts of Space on NPR.  But in general, I prefer melodies.  I
do so even with classical music.    That isn't to say today's music is wrong. 
Just like all other generations, I am a product of my times, listening to fogie
music instead of contemporary once I no longer was a youth.

Hum two songs from a Broadway musical not designed for children - from maybe the
last 20 years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:as3728$e8f$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 27-Nov-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
> > The thing about 'legacy' is that it is proven and resilient and lasts.
Now
> > hum 10 Beatles songs for me. Now do the same for the other mob fronted
by
> > that Neanderthal man !
>
> Lots of music isn't designed to be hummable.   In fact, the social need to
be
> able to whistle has gone down as there are fewer melodies in modern music.
>
> My mother's era had more tunes, and much better dance music than my era.
But
> even in my era, people had practice writing tunes.  Musical theatre had
several
> songs that were singable by just about anybody.
>
> But artists would rather be different than to try to improve on
established
> media, so music has changed.   I like some music without melodies - and
listen
> to Music From The Hearts of Space on NPR.  But in general, I prefer
melodies.  I
> do so even with classical music.    That isn't to say today's music is
wrong.
> Just like all other generations, I am a product of my times, listening to
fogie
> music instead of contemporary once I no longer was a youth.
>
> Hum two songs from a Broadway musical not designed for children - from
maybe the
> last 20 years.

Dum dummm, de-dum de-dum dum dummmm

Dee Dee, dum dum.

(Phantom of the Opera)

Deeee-deee dee dee dum de dum-de-dum

(Memories, from Cats).

It's a plot by Andrew Lloyd Webber.  No, I'm wrong - Lloyd-Webber isn't
responsible for the plots.
```

```
>I had heard that she did a naked stage performance, but I

maybe 1970. She plays the role of the philospher's wife; and has a fleeting
fully nude scene while charading Norman Mailer's _The Naked and the Dead_

for c.l.c.]
```

```
In article <3de4220a_1@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospa
m.enternet.co.nz> writes
>
>Just checked the spelling of Grammar (it's ok). My
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I think you'll find she's your Father or Mother's Mother...<G>

I asked for that one.

>
>> > There was a recent poll
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Perhaps a little hard on Nelson, 
Committed suicide by wearing a uniform with medals which marked him out
to the snipers. An officer normally wore a plain coat and hat and not
their medals. Also a philanderer (one of my pet hates). Deserted his
wife and child.


>Darwin, 
Although he did formulate the theory of evolution something like 50
years before anybody else, he did not publish his theory until Wallace
(I think, and an Aussie) sent him a copy of a manuscript independently
formulating the same theory. Then Darwin published. Why give him the
credit when his work had not been in the public domain before Wallace
published his work?


>Brunel,
A builder of large and unused boats. And bridges. I wish I had seen
Jeremy Clarkson's documentary as that must have been a magnificent piece
of tv for Brunel to do so well.


> and Newton, 
As the credited inventor of Differential Calculus he had been beaten to
it by a Frenchman by at least 25 years. What Newton did do was to
conduct the first experiments using truly rigorous scientific methods. 

>don't you
>think, Alistair? (Agree about Diana, Elizabeth the First (although you have
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Builder, Postman Pat, and Mr Blobby...

What about Pugh, Pugh, Barley, McGrew, Cuthbert, Dibble and Grub? Windy
Miller? Roobarb and Custard? Parsley? Muffin?...

>
>> > Shakespeare made it to the top 10 but it was
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>naked as Lady MacBeth many years ago

Lucky beggar!

> on the West End stage and the image
>haunts me to this day. (For those of you who get re-runs of the original
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>stupidity...<G>

Have you seen the film Rosencrantz and Guildenstern? Well worth
watching.

>
>Pete.
```

```
Great response, Alistair. You certainly take no prisoners...<G>

Some comments below...

Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:39ke6fAP8T59Ewwd@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> In article <3de4220a_1@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospa
> m.enternet.co.nz> writes
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I can't fault your knowledge of history. But if it weren't for him, we'd be
having this conversation in (Ugh!) ...French. (Come to think of it, it's a
pity the Canucks didn't have someone like him; they wouldn't be having all
this nonsense with Quebec... (if you are Canadian, please ignore
deliberately inflammatory comment <G>))


>
> >Darwin,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
He delayed publication for 20 years, but mainly because he realised the
terrible effect his "Origin of Species" would have on Society. It is
interesting that Wallace withdrew completely in favour of Darwin. (I believe
he had been a student of Darwin's, but I'm not sure about that...)

>
> >Brunel,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Clarkson's program was superb. I believe he is a genuine admirer of Brunel.
The Bookies stopped taking bets when it was rumoured that students from
Brunel University had hacked into the telephone system to rig the voting in
Brunel's favour. He certainly led for quite a while at the start of the
competition.
>
> > and Newton,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not sure who you are referring to here. Certainly there is evidence that one
of Newton's greatest rivals, the German Mathematician and Astronomer,
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, may have pipped him to the Calculus.

Despite his thoroughly disagreeable personality, and the overruling of his
work by Relativity and Quantum Mechanics, we cannot deny that it was
Newtonian physics that put men on the Moon and gave us the first true
insight into the "nature of things". (Even if that insight required
subsequent amendment...)

> >don't you
> >think, Alistair? (Agree about Diana, Elizabeth the First (although you
have
> >to admire her courage), and John Lennon (admire his ideals but still
> >basically a ne'er-do-well <G>). Of course they missed the real British
> >heroes who are loved by millions, Sooty, Basil Brush, TeleTubbies, Bob
the
> >Builder, Postman Pat, and Mr Blobby...
>
> What about Pugh, Pugh, Barley, McGrew, Cuthbert, Dibble and Grub? Windy
> Miller? Roobarb and Custard? Parsley? Muffin?...
>
Well, that's an assortment I am not familiar with...<G>

> >
> >> > Shakespeare made it to the top 10 but it was
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> plan on self-abusing myself by doing Hamlet sometime, just so that I
can
> >> see how Guildenstern and Rosencrantz fit in. BTW, I love MacBeth, even
> >> though the story is an English propaganda exercise (should that be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >There is little opportunity for self abuse in Hamlet. Ophelia is a
mournful
> >cow who ends up drowning herself (not in the least erotic). I saw Diana
Rigg
> >naked as Lady MacBeth many years ago
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are inserted into Hamlet for no apperent
reason
> >other than to make Hamlet look smart when juxtaposed against their
> >stupidity...<G>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, I haven't. I'll try and hire a DVD.

Pete.
```

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-28T18:28:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan

- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3de4220a_1@Usenet.com> <39ke6fAP8T59Ewwd@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3de5662b_5@Usenet.com> <tfQaWFAXHq59EwAe@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:

> >I can't fault your knowledge of history. But if it weren't for him, we'd be
> >having this conversation in (Ugh!) ...French. (Come to think of it, it's a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

THey did.  His name was James Wolfe and he beat the French under Montcalme at the Battle 
of the Plains of Abraham.

However, you forget that the French DID conquer England in 1066 and the language known 
today as English is one of the results.  From the old records it looks as if that was 
when my ancestors showed up in the UK. 

They love me in Quebec when I talk about how my ancestors licked the Anglo Saxons at 
Hastings.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-11-29T11:44:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<mAooRHA3M159EwRs@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3de4220a_1@Usenet.com> <39ke6fAP8T59Ewwd@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3de5662b_5@Usenet.com> <tfQaWFAXHq59EwAe@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3DE6D0CE.6AA2@paralynx.com>`

```
In article <3DE6D0CE.6AA2@paralynx.com>, Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
writes
>However, you forget that the French DID conquer England in 1066 
Definitely wrong. Normans were not French as there was no such thing at
that time (apart from some vague notional concept). 


>and the language 
>known 
>today as English is one of the results. 

It is debatable as to how much English owes to the Normans but it is
definitely rooted in Anglo-Saxon with elements of Norse, Gaelic (chiefly
Welsh) and Danish. However, this is really an argument for another
newsgroup. Unless you are arguing that Cobol is intrinsically
French/Norman?

> From the old records it looks as if 
>that was 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The Japanese have a word: Gekokujo. The low overcoming the high. In
Japan it refers to the act of the low breed ashigaru (peasant foot
soldiers) displacing their high breed samurai masters in armies and
society. In our context it would refer to the success of the Anglo-Saxon
population in assimilating their new 'masters'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Have you always dreamed of become a high payed consultant?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-29T08:59:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dfl,comp.lang.dylan
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE79D07.64BE@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3de4220a_1@Usenet.com> <39ke6fAP8T59Ewwd@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3de5662b_5@Usenet.com> <tfQaWFAXHq59EwAe@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3DE6D0CE.6AA2@paralynx.com> <mAooRHA3M159EwRs@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
> 
> In article <3DE6D0CE.6AA2@paralynx.com>, Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> that time (apart from some vague notional concept).

True, the Normans were the "North Men", having invaded and taken over as rulers of that 
part of France.  However, as was made clear in a recent TV series "Blood of the 
Vikings", a large number of their troops were ethnically French (i.e. longer time 
dwellers in what we now call France).  "Guy" is a French name (derived from an old 
German one, not Norse) and there plenty of "Guys" with the Normans.

There were even some Scots returning to Britain.  A friend of mine is named "Lamont", 
which he says was derived from "Lomond" and that his ancestors went from Scotland to 
France, francisied their name and returned in 1066.

> >and the language
> >known
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Welsh) and Danish. 

We were taught that the language shows how Normans were the ruling class.  The names of 
foods in English often come from the French (e.g."Beef") while the names of the animals 
("Cow") come from Anglo Saxon - because the Saxons looked after the animals while the 
Normans only ate them.

> However, this is really an argument for another
> newsgroup. Unless you are arguing that Cobol is intrinsically
> French/Norman?

It's a linguistic issue.  We were taught Latin to give us an idea of the operation of a 
highly structured language in order to better understand grammar. COBOL hadn't been 
invented then. While I was learning COBOL in the mid 1960s I often thought that it 
should replace Latin in the schools fro that purpose.


> > From the old records it looks as if
> >that was
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> population in assimilating their new 'masters'.

In this case they apparently married them.  I was born on the East coast of England - 
which was dominated by Vikings at one time.  I knew from Domesday Book that the Guys 
were there in the late 11th century (direct line records to me only from 1580, though - 
the Church started keeping them then).  I was surprised to see in the same TV program 
that Viking blood carries a genetically linked problem (Dupuyton's Contracture) - which 
my father had, and so did my grandmother.  So I guess I'm also the result of 
assimilation.
```

```
Damn, I knew I should have watched that rerun.

>a large number of their troops were ethnically French (i.e. longer 
>time 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
contractor, right <G>?

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<aJ7D9.24808$lj.546954@read1.cgocable.net> <3DDD2381.7030808@nycap.rr.com> <3DDD62CA.4040801@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3DDD7119.70809@nycap.rr.com> <3ddf96d3_6@Usenet.com> <OnrSoHA$kS49Ewux@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b3638c46.0211250239.38efb1d3@posting.google.com> <iXpZEjA3Bi49EwIn@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3de4220a_1@Usenet.com> <39ke6fAP8T59Ewwd@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3de5662b_5@Usenet.com> <tfQaWFAXHq59EwAe@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3DE6D0CE.6AA2@paralynx.com> <mAooRHA3M159EwRs@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3DE79D07.64BE@paralynx.com> <3de7f771_1@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> That was fascinating, Ed. I knew that Guy was a very old name and I watched
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> contractor, right <G>?

Was once (now officially "retired", but still developing software and flogging it over 
the web).  I worked with a Ukrainian chap whose ancestors were Cossacks.  We used to 
joke that his family were raping and pillaging across Eastern Europe while mine were 
doing the same across Western Europe.  ("Guy" is derived from the Germanic "Wido" which 
means "From the Woods" - which explains my interest in family trees - half my family 
were in them!)
```

```
Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote

> >However, you forget that the French DID conquer England in 1066 
> Definitely wrong. Normans were not French as there was no such thing at
> that time (apart from some vague notional concept). 

The people in most of, what is today, France were Francs, but the
Normans were not these, as you say, they were Normans: men from the
North, Vikings, Danes and Norweigens mostly, who had taken root in
many places around Europe including Yorkshire and Northumberland.

William was cousin to Edward the Confessor and of Harald Hardrada of
Norway (who invaded England a couple of weeks before William and lost
the Battle of Stamford Bridge).

The Francs never conquered England, it was just more Vikings.
 
> >They love me in Quebec when I talk about how my ancestors licked the Anglo 
> >Saxons at Hastings.

Yeah, well the Francs are like that. I'd better not elaborate.  ;-)
```

```
>see it (and it's better that way, too).

That's what I figured from the quotes of it that I read in other 
postings... not much sleep was lost over it, at least in my chamber.

>
>Greetings from comp.lang.dylan to the neighbour newsgroup,

Greets from comp.lang.cobol... how's the weather over there?  Here it 
is... the same, it seems it rarely rains on this part of the UseNet.

DD
```

```
of the good products on the market... Therefore, I've been trying to find an
evaluation copy (demo) and I really can't find it anywhere that I know of.

thanks for your help!

Martin
```

<a id="t-ec77920485"></a>
## Thread: Off topic message (is it?) — 27 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Off topic message (is it?)

- **From:** "Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-22T11:24:32+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<arl0t1$c90$1@reader12.wxs.nl>`

```
Hi all,

Being a programmer since 1985 I noticed that programmers, just like
mathematical scientists, have the ability of recognizing and creating
patterns. And music is all about patterns. Most of my colleague programmers
are musicians in their spare time and so am I.

I'll keep it short. Just take a look at www.rocketnetwork.com and learn how
to make music with people all over the world using the Internet as a medium.
I am into this scene since 1998 (www.resrocket.com in those days) and must
say I have heared some impressive music created by techies with musical
capabilities.

Regards, Danny.

(First and Last off topic message from me)
```

#### ↳ Re: Off topic message (is it?)

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-22T13:37:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HqqD9.2045$Kw6.1271725@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<arl0t1$c90$1@reader12.wxs.nl>`

```
"Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com> wrote in message
news:arl0t1$c90$1@reader12.wxs.nl...
>
> Being a programmer since 1985 I noticed that programmers, just like
> mathematical scientists, have the ability of recognizing and creating
> patterns. And music is all about patterns. Most of my colleague
programmers
> are musicians in their spare time and so am I.. and must say I have heared
some impressive
> music created by techies with musical capabilities.

I first read something about this relationship of programmers and musicians
about thirty years ago. While the correlation of interests was noted - just
as have you - at the time it was ascribed (erroneously, but those were the
times) to what we might euphemistically call "better living though
chemistry."

We know better now.

MCM
```

```
There was no mention of music. I only add this, because I qualify for
Chess and Bridge but lack any grasp of music (I consider Philip Glass,
Steve Reich and Bartok to be rather good).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Off topic message (is it?)

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T22:38:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE156EB.40202@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<arl0t1$c90$1@reader12.wxs.nl> <HqqD9.2045$Kw6.1271725@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <B3ZRMLA2oS49Ewve@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```


Alistair Maclean wrote:
> When I started out as a programmer you had to play Chess and/or Bridge.
> There was no mention of music. I only add this, because I qualify for
> Chess and Bridge but lack any grasp of music (I consider Philip Glass,
> Steve Reich and Bartok to be rather good). 
> 

Are you suggesting you are the fourth in your list of Bridge 
players, along with Glass, Reich and Bartok.  Or are they 
members of your chess club? <G>

Now in regards to programmers/musicians/philosophers 
combined, I would submit John Cage (not that I like his 
compositions).  Besides, he doesn't/didn't write COBOL, nor 
make music (never did, and can't any longer).  May be he 
might have qualified as a philosopher, or perhaps as a 
Bridge/Chess player.

However, I do like Bartok, but I prefer J.S. Bach.  I never 
could grasp Bridge, and Chess is too much like war and there 
  is enough of that already.
```

#### ↳ Re: Off topic message (is it?)

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-22T11:03:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211221103.f719ffa@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<arl0t1$c90$1@reader12.wxs.nl>`

```
"Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com> wrote

> Being a programmer since 1985 I noticed that programmers, just like
> mathematical scientists, have the ability of recognizing and creating
> patterns. And music is all about patterns. Most of my colleague programmers
> are musicians in their spare time and so am I.

Your correlation is probably caused by selective statistics.  The
programmers that I know, and especially myself, are completely tone
deaf and wouldn't know which end of a piano to blow through.

Music (AFAIK) is about repitition and rhythm. Programming isn't.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Off topic message (is it?)

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-22T20:48:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDE9A1D.3070503@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<arl0t1$c90$1@reader12.wxs.nl> <217e491a.0211221103.f719ffa@posting.google.com>`

```


Richard wrote:
> "Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com> wrote
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Music (AFAIK) is about repitition and rhythm. Programming isn't.

Since when is programming "not" about repitition?  As far as 
"rhythm", well, one definition of it is:

"In the widest sense, a dividing into short portions by a 
regular succession of motions, impulses, sounds, accents, 
etc., producing an agreeable effect, as in music, poetry, 
the dance, or the like."

In the widest sense, that sounds like a program to me.

By the way, I play the electric bass, anyone want to jam? ;)
```

```
>By the way, I play the electric bass, anyone want to jam? ;)

sax.  Of course none of us has touched an instrument in the last 20 years or so
(gotta get that piano tuned).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Off topic message (is it?)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-23T11:37:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDF6896.C1C597DF@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<arl0t1$c90$1@reader12.wxs.nl> <217e491a.0211221103.f719ffa@posting.google.com> <3DDE9A1D.3070503@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham wrote:

> >
> > Music (AFAIK) is about repitition and rhythm. Programming isn't.
> 
> Since when is programming "not" about repitition? 

It does seem to me that some programmers endlessly write the same
program over and over, with only a few differences (such as file and
report layout), and have done so for decades.

Personally I avoid that as much as possible.  For example I have one
program that prints most reports using a script file that specifies the
file(s) (accessed via a called module into a standard linkage area), the
selection criteria (based on dictionary names that decode to field
position in the linkage and type/length), the sort order and control
breaks, and the print line layout.

The scripts take a couple of minutes and there is a 'report writer' that
even a user can use to create a report.

If your programming is repitition then it is probably because the system
design is poor.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Off topic message (is it?)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-22T23:53:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDEC57C.1050606@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<arl0t1$c90$1@reader12.wxs.nl> <217e491a.0211221103.f719ffa@posting.google.com> <3DDE9A1D.3070503@nycap.rr.com> <3DDF6896.C1C597DF@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:
> Robert Graham wrote:
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> design is poor.

As a consultant for 20+ years at about one new client site 
per year, I have discovered that even with the variety of 
industries, there is not much originallity in the programs I 
am asked to maintain or fix.  I do try to make my original 
programs as creative and fresh as possible.  I look at 
writing programs like writing a speach or a short story, one 
that is trying to solve a problem in a way that whoever has 
to maintain it in the future (which is most likely NOT me) 
will have an easy time with it.

I admire your ability to create a program that a user can 
get to work without causing the lights to go out.  Most 
"users" I've run into don't have a clue about using a 
computer program beyond the Windows card games.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Off topic message (is it?)

- **From:** "Tim Scrivens" <tim.scrivens heavy mung@nz.invalideds.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T10:59:20+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<arri3q$fi2$1@hermes.nz.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<arl0t1$c90$1@reader12.wxs.nl> <217e491a.0211221103.f719ffa@posting.google.com> <3DDE9A1D.3070503@nycap.rr.com>`

```

"Robert Graham" <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
news:3DDE9A1D.3070503@nycap.rr.com...
>
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> By the way, I play the electric bass, anyone want to jam? ;)

As a fellow bass player and very part-time composer of music, I have just
this second developed a theory.

A parallel could be drawn between the different styles of music over the
years, imposed in part by the society the composer was in at the time, and
the "styles" of programming, imposed in part by the language.

Very structured and complex stuff - Bach and Assembler
Structured and as complex as you want - Mozart and Cobol
And developing the theme further, to compare composers to code

OO-Cobol - Hooked on Classics :-)
C++  - Penderecki  (atonal rubbish, but it's good for SOMETHING, even if it
is only keeping the neighbours dogs off your lawn).

OK, finished being rude about every language more complex than Cobol
now.....
```

## Thread: Getting a float return value from a c function — 2 msgs

### Getting a float return value from a c function

- **From:** mrt@columbus.rr.com (Tony Riddle)
- **Date:** 2002-11-22T12:34:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f9d8348.0211221234.410fbbaa@posting.google.com>`

```
Below is my code. I am trying to call a c function that returns a
float value. I have called all other functions successfully. I get a
value in the variable PDF-TEXT-LENGTH that is equal to the value in
PDF, I don't know if this is important or not. PDF should hold a
handle to a pdf file and PDF-TEXT-LENGTH should have the length of the
text in pixels in the specified font. This is calling a dll that works
fine when used with a c program, all functions return the values they
are supposed too. Int values seem to be returned fine and all values
are passed to the functions properly. The dll functions as it should
and everything works ok except the float return value is incorrect.
Any suggestions?




      $SET ALIGN"8"
      $SET IBMCOMP

        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
        PROGRAM-ID.      TESTPDF.
        AUTHOR.          AJR 05/02.

        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
        CONFIGURATION SECTION.
        SOURCE-COMPUTER.                 PIII-500.
        OBJECT-COMPUTER.                 PIII-500.

        SPECIAL-NAMES.
            CALL-CONVENTION 64   IS MICROSOFT.

        INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

        FILE-CONTROL.
        DATA DIVISION.
        FILE SECTION.

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01 PTR-PROCEDURE                USAGE IS PROCEDURE-POINTER.

       01 POINTERS.
           02 PDF                      USAGE IS POINTER.

       01 VARIABLES.
           02 RETURN-VALUE             PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
           02 MAJOR-VALUE              PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
           02 PDF-DATA                 PIC X(500).
           02 PDF-FILENAME             PIC X(50).
           02 PDF-INFO-NAME            PIC X(20).
           02 PDF-INFO-VALUE           PIC X(40).
           02 PDF-PAGE-WIDTH           USAGE COMP-1.
           02 PDF-PAGE-HEIGHT          USAGE COMP-1.
           02 PDF-FONT                 PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
           02 PDF-FONT-NAME            PIC X(20).
           02 PDF-FONT-HOST            PIC X(10).
           02 PDF-TEXT                 PIC X(300).
           02 PDF-FONT-SIZE            USAGE COMP-1.
           02 PDF-FONT-X               USAGE COMP-1.
           02 PDF-FONT-Y               USAGE COMP-1.
           02 PDF-TEXT-WIDTH           USAGE COMP-1.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       OPEN-FILES.

           SET PTR-PROCEDURE TO ENTRY "PDFLIB".
           DISPLAY PTR-PROCEDURE.

       MAINLINE.

           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_new"
                       RETURNING PDF.

           MOVE "TEST.PDF"         TO PDF-FILENAME.
           INSPECT PDF-FILENAME REPLACING ALL SPACES BY LOW-VALUES.
           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_open_file"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF
                       BY REFERENCE PDF-FILENAME
                       RETURNING RETURN-VALUE.
           DISPLAY "RETURN VALUE " RETURN-VALUE.

           MOVE "Creator"          TO PDF-INFO-NAME.
           MOVE "GCSStatement"     TO PDF-INFO-VALUE.
           INSPECT PDF-INFO-NAME REPLACING ALL SPACES BY LOW-VALUES.
           INSPECT PDF-INFO-VALUE REPLACING ALL SPACES BY LOW-VALUES.
           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_set_info"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF
                       BY REFERENCE PDF-INFO-NAME
                       BY REFERENCE PDF-INFO-VALUE.

           MOVE "Author"           TO PDF-INFO-NAME.
           MOVE "TonyRiddle"       TO PDF-INFO-VALUE.
           INSPECT PDF-INFO-NAME REPLACING ALL SPACES BY LOW-VALUES.
           INSPECT PDF-INFO-VALUE REPLACING ALL SPACES BY LOW-VALUES.
           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_set_info"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF
                       BY REFERENCE PDF-INFO-NAME
                       BY REFERENCE PDF-INFO-VALUE.

           MOVE "Title"            TO PDF-INFO-NAME.
           MOVE "HelloWorld"       TO PDF-INFO-VALUE.
           INSPECT PDF-INFO-NAME REPLACING ALL SPACES BY LOW-VALUES.
           INSPECT PDF-INFO-VALUE REPLACING ALL SPACES BY LOW-VALUES.
           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_set_info"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF
                       BY REFERENCE PDF-INFO-NAME
                       BY REFERENCE PDF-INFO-VALUE.

           MOVE 612                TO PDF-PAGE-WIDTH.
           MOVE 792                TO PDF-PAGE-HEIGHT.
           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_begin_page"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF
                       BY VALUE PDF-PAGE-WIDTH
                       BY VALUE PDF-PAGE-HEIGHT.

           MOVE "Helvetica-Bold"   TO PDF-FONT-NAME.
           MOVE "winansi"          TO PDF-FONT-HOST.
           INSPECT PDF-FONT-NAME REPLACING ALL SPACES BY LOW-VALUES.
           INSPECT PDF-FONT-HOST REPLACING ALL SPACES BY LOW-VALUES.
           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_findfont"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF
                       BY REFERENCE PDF-FONT-NAME
                       BY REFERENCE PDF-FONT-HOST
                       BY VALUE 0
                       RETURNING PDF-FONT.

           MOVE 24                 TO PDF-FONT-SIZE.
           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_setfont"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF
                       BY VALUE PDF-FONT
                       BY VALUE PDF-FONT-SIZE.

           MOVE 50                 TO PDF-FONT-X.
           MOVE 700                TO PDF-FONT-Y.
           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_set_text_pos"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF
                       BY VALUE PDF-FONT-X
                       BY VALUE PDF-FONT-Y.

debug      move "TestMessage" to pdf-text.
           MOVE 10 TO PDF-FONT-SIZE.
           INSPECT PDF-TEXT REPLACING ALL SPACES BY LOW-VALUES.
           MOVE 0.0            TO PDF-TEXT-WIDTH.
           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_stringwidth"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF
                       BY REFERENCE PDF-TEXT
                       BY VALUE PDF-FONT
                       BY VALUE PDF-FONT-SIZE
debug                  RETURNING PDF-TEXT-WIDTH.

           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_end_page"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF.

           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_close"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF.

           CALL MICROSOFT "PDF_delete"
                USING  BY VALUE PDF.

           STOP RUN.
```

```
DIVISION as DATA-DIVISION. Now that generated a LOT of errors.

Anyway, IF... ELSE constructs don't contain intervening preiods (.).
The rest are  ROTM typos, misspellings.

Good luck, Jack.
```
