```
> luis de reyna wrote:
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Eventually, you will be able to install the tools yourself.

Thank you very much for answering. I'll explain what we are working
in.

We plan to migrate some applications, some in cobol, some in an xbase
like language. We want to compare the results of basic statistics, say
lines, variables (fields), records, sentences paragraphs, verbs or
whatever components the tool may account for, with those of some other
applications we have already migrated, so we can have an estimate of
the work to do.

If the program can give an estimate or parameter for the "complexity"
of the programs, or for the complete system, that will also be of use,
because we could compare it with that of the known programs.

I'm a Linux supporter, and I love to install and try things by myself,
and there are some other Linux guys here.

So, if you are willing to give us a chance, we will try your program,
and tell you about the results.

Thank you in advance. 

Best regards.
```

<a id="t-38688a6133"></a>
## Thread: oo vs structural — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### oo vs structural

- **From:** nathan_hodgen@yahoo.com (Nathan Hodgen)
- **Date:** 2002-11-23T08:12:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e9777d1.0211230812.4dc9856d@posting.google.com>`

```
Can anyone give me an example of an application that would be better
served by structural programming rather than object oriented
programming?

Thanks,
Nathan
```

#### ↳ Re: oo vs structural

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-23T21:19:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DDFF3D9.A31C8FBA@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<8e9777d1.0211230812.4dc9856d@posting.google.com>`

```


Nathan Hodgen wrote:

> Can anyone give me an example of an application that would be better
> served by structural programming rather than object oriented
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nathan

Nathan,

Honestly I don't think it is a fair question. Firstly are you asking
from an OO COBOL background or experience with other OO languages ?

Much of what can be done with OO COBOL can be paralleled with Structured
COBOL - the 'old hands' have accumulated tons of routines as they moved
down the structured path. OO can't be explained in a couple of
sentences, (only by 'touch and see'  - i.e. coding in OO COBOL). It
starts right at square one - your design approach.

Given a problem in 'Structured' you can quickly put the bits together,
file formats, screen layouts and the business logic.

Before you even attempt it in OO, up front you have to PLAN you design,
with a very deep APPRECIATION of the concepts involved,  recognizing
where you need additional support classes containing reusable code, to
be used over and over again, (same principle applies with Structured)  -
but building in flexibility so that it fits more than one case (object
type), i.e. polymorphism. Accepting that as yet there aren't reams of
support classes written in COBOL, (other than what the compiler vendors
give you, to my knowledge there aren't any !), then that is a task you
have to fulfill. That means at the front end you appear to be doing
'non-productive' design. Subject to getting that up front support coded
and working, the payola is down the road. Might be a year before you see
the benefit - but you'll lick your lips with satisfaction when you
realize how easy it is to draw off from that initial support design.

The real problem I see, (specifically N/E users)  is that many OO
programmers are not designing the up front stuff. Copy and paste from an
existing Micro Focus example - particularly in the area of GUIs. Hey it
works ! What the hell ! But at the same time, they say to themselves,
"It takes up a lot of time, just pasting and re-testing". Some realize
the deficiency, but in a busy production environment they just have to
code, code, code and get it out quick. The benefits of OO only come into
play when somebody can take a deep breath, pause and give time to the
design of those support classes I referred to above.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: oo vs structural

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T14:09:44
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3de0df06_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<8e9777d1.0211230812.4dc9856d@posting.google.com> <3DDFF3D9.A31C8FBA@shaw.ca>`

```

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3DDFF3D9.A31C8FBA@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Honestly I don't think it is a fair question.

Why not, Jimmy? Seems perfectly fair to me...

>Firstly are you asking
> from an OO COBOL background or experience with other OO languages ?
>

Maybe he's asking from no experience with OO at all (or maybe no experience
with structured code at all) and is wondering if there are really things
that can be done in structured that can't be done in
OO, or vice versa...Either way, we should try and answer <G>.


> Much of what can be done with OO COBOL can be paralleled with Structured
> COBOL - the 'old hands' have accumulated tons of routines as they moved
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Absolutely! Agree 100%. But I see that as being essential in structured or
OO.

> Given a problem in 'Structured' you can quickly put the bits together,
> file formats, screen layouts and the business logic.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> type), i.e. polymorphism.

Well, that is certainly one approach to OO. It isn't the ONLY one...

>Accepting that as yet there aren't reams of
> support classes written in COBOL, (other than what the compiler vendors
> give you, to my knowledge there aren't any !), then that is a task you
> have to fulfill.

This is why the component approach is superior. It doesn't matter what
Classes are available or what they are written in. Why wait on COBOL Classes
when you can use Java ones, or C++ ones or SmallTalk ones, simply by
packaging them as components?

<snip>

> The benefits of OO only come into
> play when somebody can take a deep breath, pause and give time to the
> design of those support classes I referred to above.
>

Sorry, Jimmy, I disagree with that. Classes for any of the OO languages are
only important while you use ONE language to develop. Experience is showing
that the best way to develop is to use the language best suited for the job.
That means being multi-lingual. Some OO languages are better served with
Class support than others, but the advent of component technology has
rendered it unimportant. If the language you are using doesn't have the
Class you want, you either write the Class yourself or invoke an object
(written in another OO language) that DOES have what you want. The whole
point about OO is that it enables the use of OBJECTS. These objects can be
written in ANY OO language and used by any other (provided they are packaged
correctly, hence use of SOAP, XML, as precursors to .NET)

Pete.

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: oo vs structural

- **From:** ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk (Ron Glazier)
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T04:33:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20021123233308.14967.00000123@mb-cd.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8e9777d1.0211230812.4dc9856d@posting.google.com>`

```
     I never ever did understand what "oo" was in the first place. maybe like
starting to build an 8 storey building by starting at the top floor and working
downward.  can work as an idea but not in reality.  what oo may be is the idea
of an outline of how all relates to each of its parts. (big picture) in other
words sort out the theory before you start. then fill in the small details.
then  do that.
            while procedure referes to small chunks looked at without looking
at the rest of the mess. So 00 would be for big projects that cost a lot before
they get a return of invested energy time and money.
 smaller projects would be done by procedure oriented approach. (and so get
finished while the other ones may not as took to long) This is just an opinion.
 
     RonGlaz6@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: oo vs structural

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T14:22:56
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3de0df6b_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<8e9777d1.0211230812.4dc9856d@posting.google.com> <20021123233308.14967.00000123@mb-cd.aol.com>`

```
Ron Glazier <ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk> wrote in message
news:20021123233308.14967.00000123@mb-cd.aol.com...
>      I never ever did understand what "oo" was in the first place.

You are not alone. Sadly, the majority of fortress COBOL people have written
it off as "structured Modular Programming" re-invented.

It isn't.

But it is never easy to overcome the inertia of learning something new, when
you already have a big investment in learning something that works for you
and that you feel you understand. This is why, in general, COBOL programmers
don't take easily to Object Orientation. It requires effort. And it requires
setting aside the current understanding you have of how computer programming
"must" work.

Newbies to programming, on the other hand, are not encumbered with
pre-conceived ideas about anything and they take to it like ducks to water.

If  it is any consolation I can tell you my own story.

After using COBOL for 30 years (with occasional sallies into  "new"
languages like C and various Assemblers for mainframes and PCs) I decided it
might be good to have a look at OO COBOL, particlarly as I was seeing more
and more use of Objects in the Client/Server arena, and my current compiler
vendor (Fujitsu) had included it into their 4.2 release and based Power
COBOL (which I was using extensively) on it. (Fujitsu did this while the J4
committe were still thinking about including it in the proposed COBOL 2000
standard (which is now COBOL 2002) and we haven't seen yet...). For
PowerCOBOL users it was not a requirement to understand OO, but being of an
inquisitive nature, I wanted to get to grips with it.

Anyway, I got into the documentation and started looking at the OO
extensions to COBOL.

I am not slow when it comes to things computer, but this stuff just left me
feeling like a SCUBA diver who gets 50 feet down and realises he forgot the
aqualung. I was way out of my depth. There were loads of new constructs, the
purpose of them seemed to me to not be readily apparent, and it was like
wading through molasses to try and get anything started. After about three
weeks, I was ready to pack it in.Tried again several times and always gave
up.

I decided to teach myself Java instead. I bought some basic books on Java
and got cracking. Right from day one they started explaining the concepts of
OO. Within two weeks I was into it. Java is a language DESIGNED for OO.
COBOL has had OO "bolted on" (and a very good job of bolting has been done
too, certainly as far as the Fujitsu product is concerned.). With Java I
could use Objects created by other programmers even using other languages.
Fantastic!

Armed with my new found understanding, I went back to OO COBOL. Suddenly it
was all clear. No problem. I started using COM  and ActiveX Objects with
COBOL... started writing my own Objects in COBOL and giving them to VB
people to see if they could use them (they could...). I even had one
application where C++, VB, and COBOL objects ALL co-existed on the same User
form, completely seamlessly and transparent to the user! I got so excited I
wrote an article about
it...(http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com//archives/v3/v30201.asp )

As soon as I acquired this understanding, and started talking to other
developers (swapping components and trying stuff) I realised that EVERYBODY
outside of the COBOL community already took it as a given... VB, Java, C++
all the people who were actually using these languages to make a living (as
opposed to doing a bit of game writing at home) were into objects. The whole
"new technology" is planted firmly on OO.

In the job I do currently, I see people doing things in minutes that would
take days in COBOL. They can do this because they are empowered by OO
technology. Very little actual code gets written. (when it does it is in
VB...)

All presentation is Web Based (XML etc), all data access is via ORACLE or
SQL Server, and the actual "processing" (which really comes down to
"connecting the objects, passing them data, and invoking their methds") is
generated with tools like DTS. Where Business units wish to do their own
processing on corporate data, "Standard" tools like Excel or Access are used
to pass data to Business areas for final slicing and dicing by users to suit
themselves. Objects are constantly re-used. Even the tools we use are based
on OO technology. Procedural coding as used with COBOL is considered
"history", (see the Ferrari/ Horse and Buggy analogy I used in another
thread here recently.)

>maybe like
> starting to build an 8 storey building by starting at the top floor and
working
> downward.

No, it is nothing like that. Like you said, you really don't understand
it...

<incorrect speculations snipped >

>This is just an opinion.
>

Opinion is good. Informed opinion is better. Best of all is to actually
experience something and arrive at an understanding of it.

My advice? Learn Java. You will obtain OO skill as a spin off and you will
then have TWO strings to your bow...

Pete.

PS. I recommend "Learn Java2 in 24 hours" by Rogers Cadenhead, and "Elements
of Object-Oriented COBOL" by Wilson Price, as being ESSENTIAL for anyone who
is serious about expanding their skill set.
```


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: oo vs structural

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T20:58:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE1409B.5E7CCD36@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<8e9777d1.0211230812.4dc9856d@posting.google.com> <20021123233308.14967.00000123@mb-cd.aol.com> <BV6E9.23575$vM1.1715792@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


Russell Styles wrote:

> "Ron Glazier" <ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk> wrote in message
> news:20021123233308.14967.00000123@mb-cd.aol.com...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> floor, etc.

My comments to the original enquirer don't differ dramatically from what you
have both written above - with one exception, and that is Ron's comment that
'it can't work in reality'.

In reality it DOES work. While building a 'hierarchy' of 8 storeys would be
pushing your luck, for lone developers, it does occur with vendor compiler
support classes. Certainly up to about a  5th level, but haven't checked
whether it goes beyond that. The farther you go up through a hierachial tree
the more 'bland' (generic) the methods become. With your 8th storey as the
objective, that's the one which  becomes the easiest for you to define and
code first - you need it 'first' because it has to be available to prove out
sub classes which will  be invoking it.

I've illustrated before - for COBOL files I have only FOUR classes that
actually access files - Sequential, Line Sequential, Relative and ISAM which
as necessary invoke the Class FileErrors for file-status error messages.

Unfortunately that approach doesn't work with SQL. The DB software package
controls the 'record definition' and you can't fool around trying the
equivalent of a COBOL variable record, "occurs x to y depending on z", but at
least I can have a separate class per table containing the equivalent of COBOL
file accesses. Hiving off the SQL as a separate template means that I can copy
the classX to give me ClassA and quickly reproduce the necessary DB query
syntax, irrespective of the Table's contents. (The query generator I use is
N/E's ESQL Assistant module)

Screen layouts - Dialogs. I've already indicated that 'many' just probably
'copy and paste', and then have to thoroughly test each of the 'copies'. Took
some time figuring it out, but I have only ONE invoked class, MyDialog, (my
8th storey, if you like), which is the only class that sets up visual
appearance, displays data  and traps Window events. (One interesting comment
from a N/E user. He found it tedious copying and pasting and decided it was
just as easy to use Windows APIs directly. His logic, GUI classes eventually
'tune in' to Windows APIs, so why not use them directly. To my mind, just as
long winded, less clear and totally missing the point about 'code generators'.
You can see some of his repetitive code if you look at the cobolreport.com
archives <G>).

Jimmy Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: oo vs structural

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T20:56:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ars3gp0106v@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8e9777d1.0211230812.4dc9856d@posting.google.com>`

```

"Nathan Hodgen" <nathan_hodgen@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:8e9777d1.0211230812.4dc9856d@posting.google.com...
> Can anyone give me an example of an application that would be
better
> served by structural programming rather than object oriented
> programming?

The most significant litmus test for me is in the scale of the
application.  If the project is very large at all, it's my
opinion that it can benefit from the organization that
object-oriented programming can lend.

The degree if interaction between components is also a factor.
The more interaction, the more advantage there will be to using
a rigorous method of defining interfaces.  (GUIs are a great
example, but not the only application that has a lot if
interaction.)  This can of course be accomplished with
structure programming, but why invent something that's already
foundational to object-oriented programming.

It's easy to say this without any consideration of your
project's specifics.  Some of the more practical issues:

*  Pre-existing code-base.  If you are evolving a project
that's not object-oriented, refactoring it an object-oriented
model may be more time + money that it's worth.  Certainly,
conversion to OO should not be _the_ only goal of a project.

*  Interoperability with other systems.  In general,
component-based environments allow for easier integration.  If
your current environment does not sport this as a design goal,
build that infrastructure for one application will be of little
use.

*  Technology selection can sometimes determine this.  If
traditional tools are simply better than their OO counterparts,
on technical merits, you have to consider this.

*  Staff experience is a realistic factor.  If the team that is
to implement a design is unfamiliar or hostile to a particular
flavor of development, you have to heed those risks.

My apologies if this repeats what others have said -- I just
wanted to offer a bit of speculation from a clean palate.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: oo vs structural

- **From:** nathan_hodgen@yahoo.com (Nathan Hodgen)
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T04:06:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e9777d1.0211250406.702a4cdf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8e9777d1.0211230812.4dc9856d@posting.google.com> <ars3gp0106v@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Thanks to you all for the input.

The reason I ask the question is because I getting reassurance to make
sure I should not suggest building a system using structural
programming.  I chose to ask the question to the COBOL group for
obvious reasons.  I posted the same question to the Java group.

Our situation is we have the unique opportunity to start from scratch.
 Our system IS complex, but even if it wasn't, I wouldn't want it to
become that way.  I have not seen any arguement convincing enough to
make us use structural programming.

I appreciate all the comments very much.

Nathan
```

<a id="t-6f26970fe4"></a>
## Thread: CBL_ALLOC_MEM -vs- CBL_ALLOC_SHMEM? — 1 msgs

### CBL_ALLOC_MEM -vs- CBL_ALLOC_SHMEM?

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-24T19:18:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4C9E9.20108$ji1.15580@news1.central.cox.net>`

```
Can anyone explain the difference between the NetExpress "CBL_ALLOC_MEM" and
"CBL_ALLOC_SHMEM" calls?  And also, if using the "CBL_ALLOC_MEM", what
"flags" should I be using to force the shared memory to be released when the
run-unit is terminated?

I am using the "CBL_ALLOC_MEM" call for a program that uses IBM Personal
Communications CPI-C support.  I set the "flags" parameter to a value of "1"
(see code excerpt below), meaning allocate a shared memory segment.  I do
not issue a free memory call to release the memory, but rely on the
termination of the run-unit to free it (I know, bad programming practice!).
The memory is allocated successfully the first time through, but subsequent
run unit requests fail - I'm not for sure what the return code is ...  I
guess my real question is what do bits 2 and 3 of the flags parameter
control on the CBL_ALLOC_MEM call?

Any help would be appreciated ...

Thanks - Todd


Code Excerpt:

       01  ws-buffer-pointer           usage pointer.
       01  ws-buffer-length            pic x(4) comp-5.
       01  ws-buffer-alloc-flags       pic x(4) comp-5.
       01  ws-buffer-status            pic x(2) comp-5.

           if first-time
               set not-first-time         to true
               move +32767            to ws-buffer-length
               move 1                      to ws-buffer-alloc-flags
               call 'CBL_ALLOC_MEM'
                    using by reference ws-buffer-pointer
                          by value     ws-buffer-length
                          by value     ws-buffer-alloc-flags
                          returning    ws-buffer-status
               end-call
               move ws-buffer-status       to return-code
               .....



CPI-C Manual Excerpt:
When a program issues a call that supplies a data buffer, Communications
Manager determines whether the data buffer can be shared (giveable memory)
across processes.  If it can, Communications Manager uses the buffer
directly as the source or destination of data. If the buffer cannot be
shared, Communications Manager must copy the data between memory it
allocates and the program's buffer. Therefore, by using memory that can be
shared for the buffer, the program can avoid the extra copying of the data
and the memory allocation that Communications Manager would otherwise do on
each applicable call.

It is recommended for future migration that buffers be defined with the
tiled memory allocation option.
```

## Thread: MicroFocus Cobol and Oracle — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### MicroFocus Cobol and Oracle

- **From:** "luu" <luu@wanadoo.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T00:00:41+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3de15a1e$0$10839$8fcfb86b@news.wanadoo.nl>`

```
Dear newsgroup reader,

I am now responsible for migration from v4.1. cobol applications to
MicroFocus 2.0.1.1 on
AIX 4.3.3. unix machine.

The cobol applications were developed in microfocus v4.1 and contains
embedded SQL.
These embedded sql-codes communicates with ORACLE 7.3.4 which is precompiled
with
pro*cobol 1.8.4.  The whole thing is compiled with a makefile, which
pre-compile the embedded sql
in the cobol-source first en then compile the cobol source as whole.  Due to
the lack of documentation
of previous project and the people who had the knowledge about this
applications, has left the company,
I presumed that the makefile acquired worked. But, alas, I've problem with
the some cobol-programs.
It returns oracle message: ora-03118: two-task coroutine has an invalid
states. After a long quest,
I discovered it had problem to process decimal field. When I used other
field type, the program works
correcty. But when I used packed-decimal, it gives me the same problem. Do
you know how I can
solve this problem? With settings/directives do I need to set to solve this
problem??? Or do you
have an example makefile for me??? Do I need to make a Oracle RTS??? And how
can I link this
ORACLE RTS with my cobol sources???

Please, help me to solve this problem!!!.

Thanks a thousand times
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T10:55:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1038221713@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

### cobol newbie...need help!

- **From:** sweetmarie@myway.com (almostblue)
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T06:47:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c2b0dc20.0211250647.4d884e36@posting.google.com>`

```
I've been working on this program and can't figure out how to get rid
of these errors :(

Can someone help point me in the right direction?  Thanks in
advance....

 *---------------------------------------------------------------
  FILE-CONTROL.                                                  
 *---------------------------------------------------------------
          SELECT          CUST-PAY-FILE                          
          ASSIGN          TO DATABASE-CUSTPAYPF                  
          ORGANIZATION    IS INDEXED                             
          ACCESS MODE     IS RANDOM                              
          RECORD KEY IS   SC-CUSTNUM.                            
                                                                 
          SELECT          DISPLAY-FILE                           
          ASSIGN          WORKSTATION-ASSIGN2DF-SI               
          ORGANIZATION    IS TRANSACTION                         
          CONTROL-AREA    IS WS-CONTROL.                         
 *===============================================================
 DATA DIVISION.                                                 
*===============================================================
 FILE SECTION.                                                  
*---------------------------------------------------------------
 FD  CUST-PAY-FILE.                                             
 01  CUSTPAYR.                                                  
     05 SC-CUSTNUM PIC X(05).                                   
     05 SC-CUSTNAME PIC X(20).                                  
     05 SC-STREET PIC X(20).                                    
     05 SC-CITY PIC X(10).                                      
     05 SC-PROVINCE PIC X(03).                                  
     05 SC-POSTALCODE PIC X(06).                                
     05 SC-STATEMENT-AMT PIC 9(05).                             
     05 SC-PAYMENT-AMT PIC 9(05).                               
     05 SC-DATE-PAY-RECVD PIC 9(08).                            
     05 SC-DATE-PAY-DUE PIC 9(08).                              
                                                                
 FD  DISPLAY-FILE.                                              
 01  DISPLAY-RECORD.                                            
 COPY DD-ALL-FORMATS OF CE344A36-ASSIGN2DF.                     
*--------------------------------------------------------------*
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                       
*--------------------------------------------------------------*
 01  WS-CONTROL.                                                
     05 WS-FUNCTION-KEY    PIC X(2).                            
         88 WS-FUNCTION-KEY-03    VALUE "03".                   
                                                                
 01  WS-INDICATOR-LIST.                                         
     05 IN21     INDICATOR 21    PIC 1   VALUE B"0".            
         88 CUSTNUM-NOT-FOUND         VALUE B"1".               
                                                                
 01  WORK-FIELDS.                                               
     05 INDICATOR-ON    PIC 1    VALUE B"1".                    
                                                                
 01  WS-TEMP-FIELDS.                                            
     05 WS-AMT-STATUS PIC X(20) VALUE SPACE.                    
     05 WS-DATE-STATUS PIC X(20) VALUE SPACE.                   
     05 WS-CUSTNUM PIC X(05) VALUE SPACE.                       
     05 WS-CUSTNAME PIC X(20) VALUE SPACE.                      
     05 WS-STREET PIC X(20) VALUE SPACE.                        
     05 WS-CITY PIC X(10) VALUE SPACE.                          
     05 WS-PROVINCE PIC X(03) VALUE SPACE.                      
     05 WS-POSTALCODE PIC X(06) VALUE SPACE.                    
*===============================================================
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                            
*===============================================================
                                                                
 000-MAIN-MODULE.                                               
                                                                
     PERFORM 100-INITIALIZATION-RTN.                            
     PERFORM 120-PROCESS-SCREEN-RECORD-RTN                      
          UNTIL WS-FUNCTION-KEY-03.                             
     PERFORM 400-TERMINATION-RTN.                               
     STOP RUN.                                                  
                                                                
 100-INITIALIZATION-RTN.                                        
                                                                
     OPEN  INPUT CUST-PAY-FILE                                  
          I-O   DISPLAY-FILE.                         
    WRITE DISPLAY-RECORD                              
        FORMAT IS "SCREEN1".                          
    READ DISPLAY-FILE RECORD.                         
                                                      
120-PROCESS-SCREEN-RECORD-RTN.                        
    MOVE CP-CUSTNUM TO WS-CUSTNUM.                    
    READ CUST-PAY-FILE                                
        INVALID KEY                                   
            SET CUSTNUM-NOT-FOUND TO TRUE             
        NOT INVALID KEY                               
            PERFORM 240-CHECK-STATUS-RTN              
            PERFORM 500-WRITE-SCREEN2-DISPLAY-RTN     
    END-READ.                                         
    IF NOT WS-FUNCTION-KEY-03                         
        INITIALIZE DISPLAY-RECORD                     
        WRITE DISPLAY-RECORD                          
            FORMAT IS "SCREEN1"                       
            INDICATORS ARE WS-INDICATOR-LIST          
            READ DISPLAY-FILE RECORD                  
    END-IF.                                             
                                                        
                                                        
240-CHECK-STATUS-RTN.                                   
                                                        
    IF SC-PAYMENT-AMT > SC-STATEMENT-AMT                
        MOVE "CREDIT DUE" TO WS-AMT-STATUS              
    ELSE                                                
        IF SC-PAYMENT-AMT < SC-STATEMENT-AMT            
            MOVE "PAYMENT INSUFFICIENT" TO WS-AMT-STATUS
    ELSE                                                
            IF SC-PAYMENT-AMT = SC-STATEMENT-AMT        
            MOVE "PAYMENT CORRECT" TO WS-AMT-STATUS     
    END-IF                                              
    END-IF                                              
    END-IF.                                             
                                                        
    IF SC-DATE-PAY-RECVD > SC-DATE-PAY-DUE              
         MOVE "PAYMENT LATE" TO WS-DATE-STATUS          
         IF SC-DATE-PAY-RECVD < SC-DATE-PAY-DUE         
              MOVE "PAYMENT EARLY" TO WS-DATE-STATUS       
              IF SC-DATE-PAY-RECVD = SC-DATE-PAY-DUE       
                   MOVE "PAYMENT ON TIME" TO WS-DATE-STATUS
              END-IF                                       
         END-IF                                            
    END-IF.                                                
                                                           
500-WRITE-SCREEN2-DISPLAY-RTN.                             
    MOVE WS-CUSTNUM TO CP-CUSTNUM.                         
    MOVE WS-CUSTNAME TO CP-CUSTNAME.                       
    MOVE WS-STREET TO CP-STREET.                           
    MOVE WS-CITY TO CP-CITY.                               
    MOVE WS-PROVINCE TO CP-PROVINCE.                       
    MOVE WS-POSTALCODE TO CP-POSTALCODE.                   
    MOVE WS-AMT-STATUS TO AMT-STATUS.                      
    MOVE WS-DATE-STATUS TO DATE-STATUS.                    
    WRITE DISPLAY-RECORD                                   
        FORMAT IS "SCREEN2".                               
    READ DISPLAY-FILE RECORD.                              

400-TERMINATION-RTN.    
    CLOSE DISPLAY-FILE  
          CUST-PAY-FILE.


Errors:
MSGID: LBL1106  SEVERITY: 30  SEQNBR:  004100 
Message . . . . :   File 'CUST-PAY-FILE' not externally         
  described, keyed file. External key specification ignored.    
MSGID: LBL1463  SEVERITY: 30  SEQNBR:  020700                   
Message . . . . :   'AMT-STATUS' previously defined in program, 
  use cannot be determined. Default  item assumed.              
MSGID: LBL1463  SEVERITY: 30  SEQNBR:  020800                   
Message . . . . :   'DATE-STATUS' previously defined in program,
  use cannot be determined. Default  item assumed.              
MSGID: LBL1463  SEVERITY: 30  SEQNBR:  015301                    
Message . . . . :   'CP-CUSTNUM' previously defined in program,  
  use cannot be determined. Default  item assumed.               
MSGID: LBL1463  SEVERITY: 30  SEQNBR:  019900                    
Message . . . . :   'CP-CUSTNUM' previously defined in program,  
  use cannot be determined. Default  item assumed.               
MSGID: LBL1463  SEVERITY: 30  SEQNBR:  020000                    
Message . . . . :   'CP-CUSTNAME' previously defined in program, 
  use cannot be determined. Default  item assumed.
```

### PIC P

- **From:** nacoma <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T10:20:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3de23fb3$1_8@news.onlynews.com>`

```
Hello,

   Could you spare a moment and give me a hand. I need to check some
   results with a different COBOL compiler. I am using FUJITSU-COBOL
   version 6.1.

    Here is the code.


    01 NUM1 PIC 99P(6). 01 NUM2 PIC P(4)9(3). 01 NUM3 PIC P99. 01
    NUM4 PIC PP99.

    MOVE 25 TO NUM1. MOVE 625 TO NUM2. MOVE 25 TO NUM3 NUM4.

    DISPLAY " NUM1 " NUM1. DISPLAY " NUM2 " NUM2. DISPLAY " NUM3 " NUM3.
    DISPLAY " NUM4 " NUM4.

    thanks.

        Nora.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T14:44:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE2A7E8.9D8C5375@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3de23fb3$1_8@news.onlynews.com> <lqk4uu8d5gip1dr5c0b6igofjsb1u6rk6k@4ax.com>`

```
The picture character P, if the leftmost character in a picture, denotes an
assumed decimal point to the left of the first P, so NUM2 - 4 are fractions.

All results are zero in my version of the code.
=====
Binyamin Dissen wrote:

> On 25 Nov 2002 10:20:19 -0500 nacoma <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-11-25T21:20:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0211252120.4dc67b50@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3de23fb3$1_8@news.onlynews.com> <lqk4uu8d5gip1dr5c0b6igofjsb1u6rk6k@4ax.com> <3DE2A7E8.9D8C5375@attglobal.net>`

```
Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote

> 
> All results are zero in my version of the code.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > :>    MOVE 25 TO NUM1. MOVE 625 TO NUM2. MOVE 25 TO NUM3 NUM4.

Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux gives compiler errors saying:

"VALUE OF SENDING ITEM IS NOT REFLECTED IN RECEIVING ITEM"

Changing the literals so that they will create a value in the fields:

           MOVE 25000000   TO NUM1
           MOVE 0.0000625  TO NUM2
           MOVE 0.002      TO NUM3
                              NUM4

gives results:

 NUM1 25
 NUM2 625
 NUM3 02
 NUM4 20
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T18:14:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<txydnQsWGcuhk3mgXTWcpQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3de23fb3$1_8@news.onlynews.com> <3de34e75$2_6@news.onlynews.com>`

```

"nacoma" <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote in message
news:3de34e75$2_6@news.onlynews.com...
> Thank you,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>    I am using.

MOVE 25 TO N1

yields 00000025 internally and the right-most 6 digits are truncated. The
result, after truncation, is stored in the 2-digit field: "00"

On the other hand, had you said:

MOVE 25000000 TO N1

the result would have been "25" (with 6 implied zeros following).

As a check, what do you suppose would be stored in N1 after the following?

MOVE 12345678 TO N1

"12" or "78"?




If you have a COBOL compiler, almost everything will treated exactly the
same way - and yield identical results - on another  COBOL compiler.

If the results are not what you expect, look to your implementation first.
The notion that different compilers could/would generate different answers
is way, way down on the list of possibilities.

P.S.
In my experience the "P" scaling position symbol is extremely rare.
Originally conceived (I assume) to save space, I've only seen it used in a
production program once in 35 years.
```

#### ↳ Re: PIC P

- **From:** nacoma <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T03:49:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3de4872f$2_4@news.onlynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3de23fb3$1_8@news.onlynews.com>`

```
Thank you,

    for the explanation. I did not know how it worked internally.

    The value in N1 will be 12.

    regards,

       Nora.
```

```
You can use a type of redefine 

example

WS-DATE	W		  8	N
  WS-CC	WS-DATE	  2	N
  WS-YY	WS-DATE	+2	N
  WS-MM	WS-DATE	+4	N
  WS-DD	WS-DATE	+6	N


Hope this helped
```

```
013800     STRING  WS-WORK-FIELD(01) WS-WORK-FIELD(02)

WS-WORK-FIELD(06)
015200         DELIMITED BY SPACE

015400     END-STRING
```

```
Hi Jimmy,

The full text of the article can be found in the White Papers secion of
www.microfocus.com

I agree to some extent with Pete's comments (nice tux) about using the right
tool for the right job. That's why Micro Focus have done a lot of work with
Net Express and Server Express to allow Java and COBOL to work together
effectively.

The answer to the question "Java OR COBOL?" in many cases should be Java AND
COBOL.

As we all know here, COBOL is the best language to use for business logic.
When it comes to having a gui interface to works over an intranet or the
internet, Java is probably the most appropriate language. So why not have
the two working together, each doing what it is best at. Many people are now
doing this.

news:3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca...

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Many thanks Paul,

Perhaps Mr. Dashwood "might" now read the article and then formulate an opinion
rather than sitting in pre judgement on something he hasn't read. Mind you of
course, he just can't resist referring to Fortress COBOL, (which is a direct
insult to ALL the mainframers who participate here), Alphabet Soup or anybody
else who doesn't buy into his predetermined judgement that COBOL is doomed and
we should all be doing it in Java, VB etc. avec components.

Yes, components are the right way to go and no, COBOL need not die if people get
up off their arses and positively participate in the evolvement of the language,
coupled with a use of the power offered by OO.

Comments about using Java as the Internet tool might be dead on - I just don't
know. Until such time as I move from a single user, desktop environment into the
Internet, I can't formulate any opinion. It may well be that I can 'salvage' a
lot of existing GUI class usage to control components in COBOL syntax, (a
comment raised by Donald and either ignored by Pete, or I didn't see his reply),
and then use Java as the gatgeway to connect to the Internet. (????) - The jury
is still out until I have looked.  (Specifically Net Express -  I am aware of
two young 'go-getters', using GUI classes for design and then getting into the
Internet   ).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-11-29T05:15:49-08:00

- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0211290515.3a3e753@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca>...
> Many thanks Paul,
> 
> Perhaps Mr. Dashwood "might" now read the article and then formulate an opinion
> rather than sitting in pre judgement on something he hasn't read. 

My opinion about "COBOL vs Java" as opposed to "COBOL and Java" is a
matter of record. I have stated many times that the best tools for the
job should be used. That was all my post was intended to convey.
Reading a Marketing blurb is unlikely to change that opinion.

> Mind you of
> course, he just can't resist referring to Fortress COBOL, (which is a direct
> insult to ALL the mainframers who participate here),

A recent article I wrote received around 25,000 hits in the first few
weeks of it being published on the Web. More than 90% of these people
are currently working in a mainframe environment, which, unlike you,
Jimmy, I do know something about (having spent over 20 years of my
life writing COBOL in such an environment.) (Perhaps you'd like to
tell us all how many years you spent on mainframe sites? If the number
is greater than zero, you MIGHT be qualified to comment...))

Of the 25000, I received no mail from ANYONE who felt "insulted" by
the term "Fortress COBOL". This is hardly surprising as it was not
intended to insult individuals, rather, as a generic term for certain
mainframe sites where innovation is resisted vehemently. Most
mainframe programmers know instinctively what I am talking about. You
obviously don't.

As for insulting the people who post here, moi? you jest...<G> 

I defy anyone to show a post from me that was purely flame and aimed
at a particular individual.

> Alphabet Soup 

I did a quick Google search on this and found that I last used this
term 6 months ago. Please try and keep up...<G>

> or anybody
> else who doesn't buy into his predetermined judgement that COBOL is doomed and
> we should all be doing it in Java, VB etc. avec components.
>
That's about as twisted a spin on my position as it would be possible
to make. I have no idea why you want to ascribe such views to me, or
indeed, why you are determined to attack someone who is not your
enemy, with such vitriol.

For the record <sigh>, my judgement is NOT "predetermined" it is
formed by careful analysis of the market place and current trends in
the industry. If these factors change, so will my opinion. My current
belief is that new ways of working will render procedural programming
redundant and that does mean that COBOL and other procedural languages
are likely to decline.

But why are you so moved by this opinion that you would spin it and
spit it so venomously back at me? I guess I should be flattered that
you think my opinion is so important. (I'm not...)
 
> Yes, components are the right way to go

So we agree on that then <G>?

> and no, COBOL need not die if people get
> up off their arses and positively participate in the evolvement of the language,
> coupled with a use of the power offered by OO.
>

If you had bothered to notice, that is pretty much what I have been
advocating for many years now. That is why I became disillusioned with
the Standards Committee (ooops! nearly said "Alphabet Soup"
...wouldn't want to offend a Body which has done so much for
COBOL...), when it became apparent that they had no intention of
letting the User Base participate in the evolution of the Language (so
long as it was a nice little earner for this "non profit"
organization....).

I am on record in numerous places as stating that OO is the future of
Programming. The lack of uptake of OO by Fortress COBOL is a source of
sadness to me. If they had embraced it several years ago, COBOL would
be much healthier than it is today, but even more importantly, we
would have seen a better level of integration of the mainframe with
"new technology" and this would have delivered better systems, faster,
to the User Base.

Having said all of that, I am now of the opinion that it is probably
too late to save COBOL as the method of choice for commercial
development. (Could be wrong; hope I am...) New technologies are
emerging which simply blow the whole procedural paradigm away. You can
rant all you like about it, Jimmy, but it won't change it. My approach
is to face the reality and deal with it. (I would also try and help
others deal with it.) COBOL remains my personal favourite, and I
intend to use it to the end of my days, but I can't justify using it
for large scale commercial development any more.

> Comments about using Java as the Internet tool might be dead on - I just don't
> know. Until such time as I move from a single user, desktop environment into the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> comment raised by Donald and either ignored by Pete, or I didn't see his reply),

I didn't see Donald's post. I always read posts from him.
Was he expecting a reply from me? If he wasn't, then I can hardly be
accused of ignoring his post... If he was, then he would've mailed me
privately.

> and then use Java as the gatgeway to connect to the Internet. (????) - The jury
> is still out until I have looked. 

And while your jury is out, the whole technology has moved on.

Surprise number one: You don't need Java to bring COBOL to the Web!
Surprise number two: You don't need Java as a GUI front end to COBOL
(there are a number of products that can do this better than Java).
Surprise number three: You can access Java Classes from COBOL (and
vice versa) if you package them properly.
Surprise number four: Java is very useful in its own right.
Surprise number five: So is COBOL.

Finally, coming back to the topic, I believe that a discussion on
COBOL versus Java is a waste of time. I said that and I still think
it. We should be looking at the best tools for the job and that means
Java AND COBOL (and a number of other things too...)

Pete.
```

```
- and I wont know until I've taken a look at the N/E and Java interface. I've
only briefly read Will Price's second book , (can't lay my hands on it for the
title, but it's the one you received with an update to N/E) which is

demand, and it was around the time of the blow hot, blow cold on e-commerce and
the brief life of the dot coms. (Not an unreasonable demand and they (M/F) were
right to provide it). But I wonder. It's just like when N/E tells you that you
can load the GUI module - what do you get - Dialog System ! The GUI class
approach sits hidden in the background, unless you are already aware of its
availability.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-30T15:49:27
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Ken,

The point I was trying to make was using Java Swing's user interace
capabilities with particular regard to the RMI/IIOP system of remote
procedure calls being particularly appropriate in some cases with
internet/intranet applications where the GUI is in applet form. I'm not
saying Java should be used in every case but that it CAN be used if that's
what's required.

I would use COBOL classes and/or dialog system for an application where GUI
is not required to work over the net.

Another appropriate system of doing remote procedure calls over the net is
Web Services, now supported in COBOL by Micro Focus.

"Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> wrote in
message news:as5v03$1lf$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net...

>

JAVA?
> Why is JAVA more appropriate to use over COBOL?
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-11-30T19:18:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0211301918.1baf3ecc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <as5v03$1lf$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <asamfe$cif$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
Paul you mention Java using SWING for the GUI in aplet form to allow
the COBOL program to run over the net...

For YEARS now - Micro Focus customers have been doing something
similar (dare I say better -- than Swing) and that is taking their
applications to exactly this model using the Micro Focus COBOL on the
server and the GUI running on the client machine - using Flexus thin
client technology.  In fact, they take their existing desktop systems
- and with no source code changes - move to this environment.
```

<a id="t-c86f84e472"></a>
## Thread: New z/OS presentation for your use — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### New z/OS presentation for your use

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T04:11:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20021125231134.01534.00003920@mb-mu.aol.com>`

```
Hi,

I've put together a short presentation called "The Future of Mainframes is Now"
on our website. It's a short exploration of z/OS. I've given it for brown bag
technical talks over lunch acouple of times. It can be done in 30 minutes to 60
minutes, depending on your style.

You might find it useful for filling people at your installation in on what's
happening (big picture, but techy big picture) on IBM mainframes today. Feel
free to download it, print it off, use it for talks or a starting point for
discussions.

Go to http://www.trainersfriend.com/Book_site.htm and click on ztalkpr.pdf.
It's free (so, maybe worth what you pay, I don't know).

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: New z/OS presentation for your use

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T03:12:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vKWE9.7068$kz4.409831@news2.west.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<20021125231134.01534.00003920@mb-mu.aol.com>`

```
Been on Z/OS @ Charles Schwab (*) for the last 6 months - it's the same
old same old. As with most mainframe enhancements - ask me 10 years from
now, and I'll have a definite opinion.

Right now, I'm more interested in the DB2 V7 XML extenders. I'm half way
through a 1 year project to bring real time data replication across 7
different environments, and what I can do with Websphere MQ, Toplink, AIX
&  Oracle 9 is the trick.

OS changes seem to take far longer to sink in than application
"accommodations" (with each other).

*Senior CS architects have claimed it as the worlds largest commercial
data processor installation, in terms of MIPS - anyone got any reliable
source to prove/disprove that ? All I know is that after 20 years in the
industry, CS do things with technology, that I hadn't even dreamed of - I
feel like a kid again (not a bad trick, having turned 40YO on 10.02.02
!!! ). Furthermore - CS have a relationship with it's employees like no
other company I ever worked for (or contracted with). I have 15 years to
retirement - I have NO plans to go anywhere !!!

P.S. How about a brown bag on "OO-COBOL - a product to combat mainframe
Java" - that's something I'd be interested in !!!
```

## Thread: Colors in PowerCobol — 4 msgs

### Colors in PowerCobol

- **From:** Carlos Lages <clages@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T09:10:36-02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE356AC.CD1F07E1@attglobal.net>`

```
Hi, Does anybody here Knows why some times
i install  my aplication in a user machine and the Colors changes from
the original verson on a development computer.

In othe words, I put  a Listview  with  backcolor  white e fore Blue
and in  a User Computer appears  completely diferent.

Any clues

Thks

Carlos Lages

ps  Develop machine using win2000  power  6.1
user machine Win98, xp , etc
by the way when i need to update some routine, I copy only DLL that was
changed.
```

#### ↳ Re: Colors in PowerCobol

- **From:** cobolnotdead@hotmail.com (Kate Parker)
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T09:43:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49973e5b.0211260943.781731c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE356AC.CD1F07E1@attglobal.net>`

```
The user most likely has their Windows scheme set to something other
than the "Windows Standard."  My Powercobol applications are
completely unusable with the "Maple" color scheme.

Katie Parker


> Hi, Does anybody here Knows why some times
> i install  my aplication in a user machine and the Colors changes from
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> changed.
```

#### ↳ Re: Colors in PowerCobol

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T17:46:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2xSdnd-d3r4lmnmgXTWcoQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE356AC.CD1F07E1@attglobal.net>`

```

"Carlos Lages" <clages@attglobal.net> wrote in message
news:3DE356AC.CD1F07E1@attglobal.net...
> Hi, Does anybody here Knows why some times
> i install  my aplication in a user machine and the Colors changes from
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any clues

Your told your application to use "Windows Standard" colors and they
probably look swell on your machine.

The user, however, can specify whatever shades he wants for
WindowBackground, ButtonFace, etc. These choices, often done while drunk,
are often bizarre or incompatible.

Your alternatives are two: 1) Use your own colors (Standard Colors) or
2)Leave things alone under the theory that if the user wants goofy colors,
that's his problem.

You may test your program under various renditions of windows 'standard'
colorizations to point out any wild combinations.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Colors in PowerCobol

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-11-28T03:24:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20gF9.886$ZL1.48424134@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE356AC.CD1F07E1@attglobal.net> <2xSdnd-d3r4lmnmgXTWcoQ@giganews.com>`

```
To expand on Jerry's reply, I set all my PowerCobol apps to use the "boring"
Standard Colors, i.e. red, blue, black, cyan, blah, blah.

I don't use the Fujitsu default of "System Colors" any more, although I have
to admit, when I first did apps in "System Colors", there were some rather
cool looking screens.  But others were downright ugly. I can't believe some
of the combinations some users use! I've seen some weird looking "button
face" purples.

So using "Standard Colors", when the app gets moved, the colors "generally"
stay the same .

Denny
```

<a id="t-af75bea6ef"></a>
## Thread: Free Cobol Compiler for Linux — 5 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Free Cobol Compiler for Linux

- **From:** Charles Givens <ctgivens@solatonce.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T15:39:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fe57uugs70d5jtvqhlvd6m8gpadads04al@4ax.com>`

```
Hello All,

 Does anyone know where I can get a hold of a "free" cobol compiler
for Red Hat Linux?

Thanks

Charles
```

#### ↳ Re: Free Cobol Compiler for Linux

- **From:** ookpik1@yahoo.co.uk (Dean Powell)
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T11:15:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f7686d4.0211261115.2b0bdf1b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<fe57uugs70d5jtvqhlvd6m8gpadads04al@4ax.com>`

```
There are a few.  The one I use is called TinyCOBOL, and is available
at http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/.

Another effort, also hosted by the good folks at SourceForge, is
called OpenCOBOL and can be downloaded from
http://open-cobol.sourceforge.net/.  I have never used openCOBOL, so I
couldn't tell you the differences between the two compilers.

Apparently, there is also a product from the Kompany called KOBOL.  It
can be downloaded from the Komany's website at
http://www.thekompany.com/products/kobol/, and works only with KDE
libraries installed.

Hope this helps.

Regards,
Dean Powell
Edmonton, Canada

> Charles
```

## Thread: RFIND not active in Xpediter — 4 msgs

### RFIND not active in Xpediter

- **From:** aruns_nj@yahoo.com (Arun)
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T09:31:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cd5102a5.0211260931.a66cd26@posting.google.com>`

```
Whenever I try to use repeat find function the Xpediter it gives me a
annoying message that 'RFIND not active'. How do we make 'RFIND'
command active in Xpediter?. Rfind command is working fine inside ISPF
editor.

                                                        Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: RFIND not active in Xpediter

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T18:05:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE3B9E6.3070705@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<cd5102a5.0211260931.a66cd26@posting.google.com>`

```


Arun wrote:
> Whenever I try to use repeat find function the Xpediter it gives me a
> annoying message that 'RFIND not active'. How do we make 'RFIND'
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>                                                         Thanks.

The ISPF RFIND PF key is not useable in Xpediter.  Make sure 
the Xpediter menu options are on.  You fill in the search 
value and use one of the menu options.  It is not a PF key 
within Xpediter.

(I am doing this from memory since I am "between 
engagements".)  ;(
```

```
PF5 key works just fine for me when I want to go to the next occurance of a
```

<a id="t-dbf72eda05"></a>
## Thread: OT: Humor Senior Exercise — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Humor Senior Exercise

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T02:37:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c295bd$f81553a0$bc93f243@chottel>`

```

          SENIOR EXERCISE REGIME

Pass this along to your senior friends, for those getting along in years...

Here is a little secret for building arm and shoulder muscles.

Three days a week works well.

Begin by standing outside behind the house, and with a 5-lb. potato sack in

each hand... extend your arms straight out to your sides and
hold them there as long as you can.

After a few weeks, move up to 10-lb. potato sacks and then 50-lb. potato 
sacks, and finally get to where you can lift a 100 lb. potato sack in each 
hand and hold your arms straight for more than a full minute.

Next...start putting a few potatoes in the sacks, but be careful not to 
overdo it.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Humor Senior Exercise

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T02:37:05-08:00

```
Hahaha! I liked this very much... 

As time moves on I feel less and less inclined to exercise. These
days, on the rare occasions when I get the urge to work out, I lie
down until it passes...

Of course, back home in NZ there are hot pools everywhere so all year
swimming is not a problem and there is a ready remedy for aching
limbs. Here in the U.K. the indoor swimming pools are not in the least
attractive and my "work out" area tends to be the sofa <G>.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-03183bc0a8"></a>
## Thread: Inspired by the Anders Hejlsberg's presentation — 1 msgs

### Inspired by the Anders Hejlsberg's presentation

- **From:** "dan marinescu" <support@danmarinescu.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-27T03:28:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4ZWE9.4576$ls5.246375484@newssvr13.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Inspired by the Anders Hejlsberg's presentation (when c# launched) and feeling somehow sad about MS Notepad, I decided to write my own programmer notepad, called Total Notepad. If anyone is interested in a free, small footprint, super fast and professional source beautifier (c, c++, java and c#!) plus editor with publishing capabilities (html and rtf exports), syntax coloring for about 22 languages (including c, c++, java, c#, vb, delphi, fortran, cobol, perl, python, tc/tkl and more.)  plus color printing, well, you can find it at www.danmarinescu.com/totalnotepad/

Perfect complement for free .net framework, java framework, gcc and bcc (free set of gnu compilers). Hey, hey, hey, like I said, it's FREE! (under GNU) - I am NOT trying to sell you anything. This is simply the perfect tool for real programmers, students, whoever wants to get the job done fast, clean and without paying big bucks for super huge tools doing anything but you want them to do here and now. :-)


--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Please compare the following list of features with your programming tool. 

 

  a.. Price
    a.. Zero Dollars
  b.. Editor
    a.. File size: unlimited
    b.. Max number of bookmarks per file: 10
    c.. Smart bookmarks (new technology): yes! (No other ides have it) When you set up a bookmark, you do NOT have to specify the bookmark id. Also, when you want to go to a bookmark, you are actually shown the book-marked line, instead of the bookmark's id! This is super cool (makes your life much easier).
    d.. Line numbering: yes
    e.. Selection modes: 3 (note that other editors have only 1!) the tree selection modes are (1) complete lines, (2) regular, (3) columns with full drag and drop capabilities.
    f.. Scalable fonts: yes
    g.. Internet Explorer style full screen mode: yes
    h.. HTML and RTF super fast accurate exports: yes
    i.. Language independent Brackets matching: yes
    j.. Syntax coloring for 22 languages (the full list includes, ansi c, ansi c++, c#, java, visual basic (including oop features of vb .net), delphi / object pascal, standard pascal, fortran, cobol, perl, python, java script, vb script, html, xml (any flavor, including xml, wsdl, xsd, xsl, xslt, dtd), php, tcl/tk, foxpro (prg), ansi sql, pl sql, transact sql, idl and, of course, html and assembly!
    k.. Extra formats supported: Borland forms (dfm & xfm) and windows INIs.
    l.. Beautifier UNDO (unlimited undos and redos).
    m.. Cross platform capabilities (works excellent with both Windows and UNIX files).
    n.. Tones of new useful short-cats
    o.. Coloring scheme: MS Developer Studio (& .NET) Extended (different color for numbers, strings, symbols).
    p.. Just in time instantiation (start at line number) - for easy integration with external ide-s, programming tools
    q.. Internet Explorer Full Screen mode: yes (still, same F11).
  c.. Beautifier
    a.. Simple, easy to use, standard front end (gui)
    b.. Over 20 professional options for formatting your source code
    c.. Styles! (new concept, super easy to use).
    d.. Supports the following languages, k&r c, ansi c (both 89 and 99), ansi c++, java and c#.
    e.. Invokable from the command line (from outside the ide): yes.
    f.. Now, about performance: imagine a very complex c++ file (around 10,000 lines). To beautify something like that. well, on a regular machine (under 2GHz) you may need. under 3 seconds! So, it's very fast!
    g.. Super fine tuning for formatting options.
  d.. Publisher
    a.. HTML export (super fast, over 10,000 / second! Regular 7200 rpm WD HDD)
    b.. RTF export (super fast, over 9,000 / second! Regular 7200 rpm WD HDD)
    c.. Combined with the editor and the beautifier, this is closing the circle! You can create, maintain, beautify/standardize and publish your work at ease.
  e.. Integrated command line prompt
    a.. Sometimes, you need to access the command interpreter (this case, cmd). It is nice to do that from inside the IDE. Well, we did provide that.
    b.. This comes very handy, as you will normally use it with .net framework, or gcc or bcc (borland free command line compiler). 
    c.. This makes sense from a programmer point of view.
 

 

Thanks,

Daniel & Team
```

<a id="t-1ab395762e"></a>
## Thread: UTB009 — 1 msgs

### UTB009

- **From:** arnb <arnbRemoveThis@Arnb.org>
- **Date:** 2002-11-26T23:51:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<as1ivp$mqqlv$1@ID-128292.news.dfncis.de>`

```
The FSI online system is now comfortable with UTB009. The culprit was ---- tah 
dah-- an old version of utb260, namely utb26032, was being linked with the 
online. Switching to the updated UTB260 solved the problem. Furthermore, UTB009 
must be linked with AMode (31) rmode (any) apparently the default for 31 bit 
modules.

Assume you also fixed UTB440Cd when you changed ATM410 last evening. Good thing 
that was caught before it did some damage.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> I have to say that "teste" is not the singular of "testes".

tes'te n. (Law) (a) A witness.  (b) The witnessing or 
concluding clause of a writ or other precept.

tes'ted  a. (Law) Having the teste duly attached.

However, let's not get testy.  ;)
```

```
area for a full-time Programmer/Analyst position.  If you are
interested, and have at least one year of real experience with
Fujitsu, please fax your resume to my attention at 972.398.4666.

Kate Parker
American Southwest Insurance Managers, Inc.

I promise that I not trolling for resumes, Doc.  ;-)
```

#### ↳ Re: EXPERIENCED Fujitsu Powercobol Developer needed

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-11-28T13:50:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<as5oh4$err$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
In article <49973e5b.0211271222.24572ed1@posting.google.com>,
Kate Parker <cobolnotdead@hotmail.com> wrote:
>I need an experienced Fujitsu Powercobol Developer

[snip]

>I promise that I not trolling for resumes, Doc.  ;-)

Without figures the promise shows little content, Ms Parker... and it 
seems that you've denied only *one* of the usually-given possibilities. 
 Numeri, non verba.

When posting to comp.lang.cobol please include a rate, or range of rates, 
associated with the position(s) offered; doing otherwise leads many to 
conclude that you are either trolling for resumes or running a blind ad to 
determine rates.

DD
```

```
Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message news:3DE691BB.70807@nycap.rr.com...
>
> Volker Bandke wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> limited knowledge of English would be "numbers, not words".

Latin supplies more everyday words than many people realize,
including the mottoes for the British Royal Air Force and the United States Marines.
For a supposedly dead language, it sure gets a lot of usage, doesn't it.

Numerus is the Latin word for number,
and Verbum is the Latin word for, well, word.

So, very good.  To the top of the class you go.

Now you can go around hurling "Nemo me impune lacessit"
at anybody who ever dares to question your intelligence.

It means "No-one attacks me with impunity".

It is, believe it or not, the Scottish national motto.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXPERIENCED Fujitsu Powercobol Developer needed

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-11-30T01:27:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uUF9.30460$vM1.2371161@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<49973e5b.0211271222.24572ed1@posting.google.com> <as5oh4$err$1@panix1.panix.com> <m63duuchjfur0bh771euijqiuovlr5610f@4ax.com> <3DE691BB.70807@nycap.rr.com> <eSDF9.35959$hK4.3135833@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3DE7E1E0.8030601@nycap.rr.com>`

```

Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message news:3DE7E1E0.8030601@nycap.rr.com...
>
> And the Graham Clan motto is "Ne Oublie" - "Never Forget",
> probable based upon one of those battles that Montrose
> fought - Killincrankie (sp?) I believe.

The Marquess of Montrose, James Graham, a poet, was indeed a well-known and well-regarded soldier.
Some claim that he was "probably the finest strategist the world has ever seen".
At least he was, until he was hung, drawn and quartered in Edinburgh in 1650.
Doesn't seem right, somehow.

However, it was John Graham, the Viscount of Claverhouse, "Bonnie Dundee",
who led the Scots to victory against the English at the Battle of Killiecrankie Gorge.
At least he did until somebody popped him.  Dead by a silver button no less.

A colorful lot, the Grahams, to put it mildly.
```

```
I actually didn't know a whole lot about the Graham Clan 
until one day I discovered Montrose was a poet - poetry 
being a passion of mine (reading it, not writing - sigh). 
Then I found a book called "The Proud Servant" which was 
about James Graham and his battles.  It was a fictionalized 
account but if it was anywhere close to what he did, well he 
would certainly qualify as a great general.  Montrose, like 
Wallace, didn't die in peace but in pieces. :(

When I read McKay's (sp?) History of Scotland I discovered 
the Graham's were indead colorful.  One in fact bumped off 
one of the kings.  I guess we all have a black sheep or two 
hanging around our family tree. :)

To keep this relevant to the news group -

I HAVE A COPY OF FUJITSU POWERCOBOL.

There. ;)  (But I am experienced with IBM VS-COBOL-II and 
other Mainframe versions, as well as MicroFocus (DOS) and 
RM-COBOL (also DOS), but I wouldn't say I was experienced 
with PowerCOBOL even though it takes up space on my PC.)
```

```
I hauled out my trusty OED(unabridged, compact), along with the 
magnifying glass to find the pages,and a jewler's loupe to actually read 
them, and read for several days (god the OED is wordy).  The nitty 
gritty is that hanging is a ritual execution, and when done to a person, 
that person has been hanged.  In the other hand, the same person could 
be hung from a rope by the waist, to paint a wall. When one is hanged, 
one is hung by the neck. One is never hanged by the waist, unless you 
run into a very badly trained hangman.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Power .Net

- **From:** pvieira@emporsoft.pt (Paulo Vieira)
- **Date:** 2002-11-29T03:58:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5b8d7c7.0211290358.34ce431f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE61295.7B4005D@attglobal.net> <KYydnRrIkeGVtXugXTWcqg@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<KYydnRrIkeGVtXugXTWcqg@giganews.com>...
[...] 
> 
> I understand you can't use PowerCobol. I further understand Fujitsu is
> working on a conversion routine allowing you to salvage all the millions of
> man-hours invested in existing PowerCOBOL applications.

Is it true? 
I am (what I consider to be) a model pupil of Fujitsu: 
   -I pay my maintenance fees on time. 
   -I buy every new product they release. 
   -I only ask for maintenance on things that are obvious bugs and
usualy promised to be corrected in the next release.

But I never heard of such a tool. Although I am the proud owner of a
Universal compiler of NetCobol for .NET (which by the way, costs me
$1,260 USD more plus FEDEX expenses  then a US local just for the sake
of living in Europe), Fujitsu as failed to inform me of such a tool.
Probably it doesn't matter for them; but for me, having 1500 modules
(programs in the old sense) in production written with PowerCobol, it
makes a lot of sense try to plan ahead what I'm going to do with them
(and my business) when there is no more support for the underlying
techonology.
As you know, doing the transition without a tool to convert the
programs is not a possibility. We can't even read the "ppj" file
without Powercobol, much less translate all references to COM modules
to .NET [web|windows] references.
Apart from that, the paradigm as shifted so much that even the cut &
paste aproach must be done cautiously or you will end up with a source
impossible to compile.
```

<a id="t-6cf71b6c6f"></a>
## Thread: Looking for tool to create/generate screen-sections (screen painter?) — 11 this month / 22 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Looking for tool to create/generate screen-sections (screen painter?)

- **From:** "G���nther De Vogelaere" <Gunther.DeVogelaere@killspammersrug.ac.be>
- **Date:** 2002-11-28T18:02:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<as5ho3$99c$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```
Hello,

I'm looking for a tool to create/generate text-based screensections.

I use Microfocus Netexpress 3.1.11 Service Pack 1 and it seems that such a
tool (DSCH.exe or MicroFocus Dialog System) is  included but it generates
copy-books and 'screens' that only can be used with a call to their 'dialog
system runner'-thingy. Maybe I'm doing something horribly wrong in the
DSCH.exe-tool.

I don't want to use their system in order to be able to generate code that
also will work in a non-microfocus environment (so only accept/display of
screens).

Maybe an old, pre-windows version of this DSCH.exe would do the trick? Can
somebody tell me where I could find/ download such a tool?

Thx,
G�nther De Vogelaere

Please eliminate kill spammers to reply.
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for tool to create/generate screen-sections (screen painter?)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-11-28T19:06:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE66C6D.B8F6C9F7@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<as5ho3$99c$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```


"Gï¿½nther De Vogelaere" wrote:

> Hello,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Please eliminate kill spammers to reply.

Bearing in mind that the DOS Text Screen Section you are using is NOT yet part
of the Standard, (but an updated 'version' will be when COBOL 2002 is issued),
you are kind of stuck. Micro Focus has its own Screen Section, but other than
somebody else confirming, I doubt it currently parallels what is available,
say, in Fujitsu Screen Section.

It may well be compatible with Fujitsu if you ignore the M/F Screen Attributes
and Mouse routines. M/F Screen Section also contains the 'occurs feature' for
repetitive lines for tables. Then of course if you want to get into other
'environments', Liant(RM/COBOL), AcuCOBOL etc. - how do they currently handle
Screen Section ?

It really seems hardly worth the effort - but if you can get an old DOS screen
painter like Saywhat? or Software Bottling Company, (although probably both are
out of business) - these 'paint' pretty pictures in colours. To be 'generic'
you would have to restrict yourself to the simple ANSI  'accept field' or
'display field' - coupled with getting those accepts/displays to line up with
your painted screen. You sure you want to go to all this effort ?

Simple answer - determine which compiler you are going to use to earn your
bread and butter and use their appropriate Screen Section features.

Portability in COBOL is a big "killer" and not just the Screen Section. Don't
assume, having got something to work in compiler X it will happily compile when
you give it to compilers Y and Z - you will get REAMS of compiler errors ! You
are probably not aware, but Net Express, (and same applies to Fujitsu), already
contain many of the new features which will be in COBOL 2002  - but currently
you will get errors switching between compilers.

If you are trying to hedge your bets and Screen portability is a big issue the
best route for you to go would be Flexus SP2 or Norcom's Screen IO. Both
converse happily with a large number of COBOL compilers - you merely supply
parameters to their packages. They have the added bonus, you are programming in
straightforward COBOL syntax and they produce the results for you in Windows
format. (Both packages work with Micro Focus and Fujitsu products).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Looking for tool to create/generate screen-sections (screen painter?)

- **From:** "G���nther De Vogelaere" <Gunther.DeVogelaere@killspammersrug.ac.be>
